# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: de Broglie Wavelength of an Accelerated Electron

> **Problem:** Show that for an electron accelerated through a potential $V$ (non-relativistic), the de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V(\text{volts})}} \text{ nm}$.

### Step 1 — Start with the de Broglie relation
By de Broglie's hypothesis, every moving particle has an associated wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

where $p$ is the linear momentum of the particle.

### Step 2 — Relate momentum to kinetic energy (non-relativistic)
For an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$, all the electrical potential energy is converted into kinetic energy:

$$eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

Solving for $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 eV}{m_e}}$$

The momentum is then:

$$p = m_e v = m_e \sqrt{\frac{2 eV}{m_e}} = \sqrt{2 m_e eV}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into de Broglie relation
Substituting $p = \sqrt{2 m_e eV}$ into $\lambda = h/p$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV}}$$

### Step 4 — Insert numerical values
$h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, and converting metres → nanometres ($\times 10^9$):

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times V}} \text{ m}$$

Simplifying the denominator:

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = \sqrt{2.919 \times 10^{-49}} = 5.403 \times 10^{-25}$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{5.403 \times 10^{-25} \, \sqrt{V}} \text{ m} = \frac{1.226 \times 10^{-9}}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ m} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda(\text{nm}) = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V(\text{volts})}}}$$

### Step 5 — Physical interpretation
For $V = 100$ kV $= 10^5$ V, $\lambda \approx 0.00387$ nm $\approx 3.87$ pm, which is **~50,000× smaller** than visible light (~550 nm). This is precisely why SEM can resolve features invisible to optical microscopes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relativistic Correction (for $V > 30$ kV):** When electron speed approaches $c$, the non-relativistic formula overestimates $\lambda$. The corrected relativistic form (already in the formula sheet) must be used. The relativistic wavelength of a 100 keV electron is **~3.70 pm** (vs. 3.87 pm non-relativistic) — a ~4.5% difference, but the *resolution-limiting* effect of aberrations is far greater.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Penetration Depth — Kanaya–Okayama Range

> **Problem:** Derive the expression for the electron penetration depth in a solid.

### Step 1 — Define the range
The Bethe continuous-slowing-down approximation gives the rate of energy loss as a function of path length $s$:

$$-\frac{dE}{ds} = \frac{2 \pi e^4 Z \rho N_A}{A E} \ln\left(\frac{1.166 E}{J}\right)$$

where $J$ ≈ **11.5 × Z** eV is the mean ionization potential.

### Step 2 — Integrate from initial energy $E_0$ to 0
Kanaya and Okayama performed the integration assuming an empirical constant replaces the slowly varying logarithm, yielding:

$$R_{\text{K-O}} = \frac{0.0276 \, A \, E_0^{1.67}}{Z^{0.889} \, \rho}$$

Units: $A$ in g/mol, $E_0$ in keV, $\rho$ in g/cm³ → $R$ in **µm**.

### Step 3 — Numerical Example (typical KTU problem)
For **silicon (Si)** with $A = 28.09$ g/mol, $Z = 14$, $\rho = 2.33$ g/cm³, at $E_0 = 20$ keV:

$$R = \frac{0.0276 \times 28.09 \times 20^{1.67}}{14^{0.889} \times 2.33} \, \mu\text{m}$$

Calculate term-by-term:
- $20^{1.67} = 20^1 \times 20^{0.67} = 20 \times 7.082 = 141.64$
- $14^{0.889} = e^{0.889 \ln 14} = e^{0.889 \times 2.639} = e^{2.346} = 10.44$

Substituting:

$$R = \frac{0.0276 \times 28.09 \times 141.64}{10.44 \times 2.33} = \frac{109.81}{24.32} \approx 4.51 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> **Physical meaning:** In Si at 20 kV, primary electrons penetrate **~4.5 µm** deep. The interaction volume is teardrop-shaped, and only the **top ~5–50 nm** of escape-depth electrons (SE) carry true surface information.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Total Probe Diameter (Resolution Limit)

The minimum beam spot size at the specimen determines the ultimate SEM resolution. Three contributions add in quadrature (assuming Gaussian profiles):

$$D_{\text{probe}} = \sqrt{d_g^2 + d_s^2 + d_c^2 + d_d^2}$$

where:
- $d_g$ = diameter limited by **gun source size** (≈ demagnified virtual source)
- $d_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s \alpha^3$ → spherical aberration (with $C_s$ = spherical aberration coefficient)
- $d_c = C_c \alpha \tfrac{\Delta E}{E_0}$ → chromatic aberration (with $C_c$ = chromatic aberration coefficient)
- $d_d$ ≈ 0.5 nm → diffraction limit (Rayleigh)

### Worked Example
Given: $d_g = 5$ nm, $C_s = 50$ mm, $\alpha = 5$ mrad, $C_c = 30$ mm, $\Delta E / E_0 = 10^{-3}$.

$$d_s = 0.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 0.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7} = 3.125 \text{ nm}$$

$$d_c = 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3} = 0.15 \text{ nm}$$

$$D = \sqrt{5^2 + 3.125^2 + 0.15^2 + 0.5^2} = \sqrt{25 + 9.77 + 0.0225 + 0.25} = \sqrt{35.04} \approx 5.92 \text{ nm}$$

> **Conclusion:** For this conventional SEM, the probe size is dominated by the **demagnified source** ($d_g$) and **spherical aberration** ($d_s$). Field-emission guns reduce $d_g$ dramatically, pushing resolution below **1 nm**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SEM with TEM & Optical Microscopy

| Parameter | Optical Microscopy | SEM | TEM |
|-----------|-------------------|-----|-----|
| Probe / wavelength | Visible light (~550 nm) | Electrons (1–50 pm) | Electrons (1–10 pm) |
| Resolution (best) | ~200 nm | ~1 nm | ~0.05 nm (atomic) |
| Magnification | 1× – 2000× | 10× – 10⁶× | 50× – 10⁷× |
| Sample type | Living/dead, bulk | Bulk, surface | Ultra-thin (<100 nm) |
| Image type | Surface (reflected/transmitted) | Surface (SE/BSE) | Internal (transmitted) |
| Vacuum needed? | No | Yes (10⁻²–10⁻⁶ Pa) | Yes (10⁻⁵–10⁻⁷ Pa) |
| Field of view | Large | Medium | Very small |
| Cost (INR lakh, approx) | 0.5–10 | 30–250 | 200–1500 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner tip:** SEM is essentially a *surface* technique; TEM is a *bulk transmission* technique. For nanoparticle internal lattice imaging, you must use TEM, not SEM.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an SEM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EMGun[Electron Source Subsystem]
        A1[Filament: W / LaB6 / FE Tip]
        A2[Wehnelt Cylinder - Bias]
        A3[Anode at +V accel - 0.5 to 30 kV]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph Column[Electron Optical Column - High Vacuum]
        B1[Condenser Lens 1 - Demag ~50x]
        B2[Condenser Aperture 30 to 300 um]
        B3[Condenser Lens 2 - Demag ~50x]
        B4[Objective Aperture]
        B5[Objective Lens - Final Focus]
        B6[Scan Coils - Raster Deflection]
        B7[Stigmator - Astigmatism Correction]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B7 --> B6
    end

    subgraph Stage[Specimen Chamber]
        C1[Eucentric 5 axis Stage X Y Z Tilt Rotation]
        C2[Sample - Conductive Coating if needed]
        C1 --> C2
    end

    subgraph Det[Detector Suite]
        D1[Everhart-Thornley SE Detector]
        D2[BSE Solid State Detector]
        D3[EDS Si-Li X-ray Detector]
        D4[CL Photomultiplier]
    end

    subgraph Vac[Vacuum System]
        E1[Rough Pump - Rotary 10 to 1 Pa]
        E2[Turbomolecular Pump 10 to 6 Pa]
        E3[ION Pump 10 to 9 Pa optional]
        E1 --> E2 --> E3
    end

    subgraph DAQ[Signal Processing and Display]
        F1[Amplifier]
        F2[ADC]
        F3[Image Processor - Pixel Mapping]
        F4[High Resolution Monitor]
        F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4
    end

    A3 --> B1
    B6 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
    C2 --> D4
    D1 --> F1
    D2 --> F1
    D3 --> F1
    D4 --> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Description of the flow:** Electrons are generated in the **source**, accelerated down the **column** through electromagnetic lenses and apertures, focused to a nanometre-scale probe, **scanned** across the **specimen** via scan coils, and the resulting secondary/backscattered/X-ray signals are collected by the **detector suite**, amplified, digitised, and used to reconstruct a magnified image on the monitor. The entire column operates under **high vacuum** to prevent electron-gas scattering.

## 4.2 Electron–Specimen Interaction Volume

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InteractionVolume[Interaction Volume Inside Specimen]
        V1[Incident Beam - 20 keV electrons]
        V2[Primary Electron Track - Elastic Scattering]
        V3[SE1 - Direct from Beam - High Res Topography]
        V4[SE2 - From Backscattered e Escape - Lower Res]
        V5[BSE - Elastic Backscatter - Z Contrast]
        V6[Characteristic X-ray - EDS Signal]
        V7[Auger Electron - Surface Chemistry]
        V8[Absorbed Current - Specimen Current Image]
        V1 --> V2
        V2 --> V3
        V2 --> V4
        V2 --> V5
        V2 --> V6
        V2 --> V7
        V2 --> V8
    end
```

> **Note:** The teardrop-shaped interaction volume grows with **increasing accelerating voltage** and **decreasing atomic number**. At higher $V$, the beam penetrates deeper → larger interaction volume → **reduced spatial resolution** for X-ray and BSE signals. This is why low-voltage SEM (1–5 kV) is preferred for **nanoparticle surface imaging**.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right SEM Signal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What information do you need?}
    Q1 -->|Surface topography| SE[Use SE Detector - Everhart-Thornley]
    Q1 -->|Atomic number contrast| BSE[Use BSE Detector - Solid State]
    Q1 -->|Elemental composition| EDS[Use EDS Detector - Energy Dispersive]
    Q1 -->|Light emission from sample| CL[Use CL Detector - Photomultiplier]
    Q1 -->|Surface chemistry top 1 nm| AUG[AES - Auger Electron Spectroscopy]
    Q1 -->|Sub-nm lattice imaging| TEM[Not SEM - Use TEM Instead]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Aligned with KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:**
> - Part A: 3-mark direct questions (Answer in ~50–80 words)
> - Part B: 14-mark analytical questions with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B
> - Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Secondary Electrons (SE) and Backscattered Electrons (BSE) in SEM with respect to their origin, energy, and information conveyed.**

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | Secondary Electrons (SE) | Backscattered Electrons (BSE) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Origin** | Inelastic scattering, weakly bound outer-shell electrons | Elastic scattering by atomic nucleus |
| **Energy** | < 50 eV | > 50 eV (up to $E_0$) |
| **Escape depth** | 5–50 nm (true surface) | 0.1–1 µm (subsurface) |
| **Information** | **Topography** (resolution ~1 nm) | **Compositional / Z-contrast** |
| **Sensitivity to atomic number** | Low | High (BSE coefficient $\eta$ ∝ Z) |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is a high-vacuum environment necessary in SEM? Mention the typical vacuum range used.**

**Model Answer:**
A high vacuum is essential in SEM because (i) accelerated electrons would be **scattered by air molecules**, destroying the focused beam; (ii) the **tungsten filament or FE tip would oxidize** rapidly in the presence of oxygen; and (iii) the specimen surface would be **contaminated by hydrocarbon adsorption**, degrading image resolution. The typical operating vacuum is **10⁻² to 10⁻⁶ Pa (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁸ Torr)**, achieved by a combination of rotary, turbomolecular, and ion pumps.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Modified]`

**"Explain the principle, instrumentation, and working of Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the various signals generated and their applications."**

#### Part (a) — Principle, Instrumentation, and Working (7 marks)

**1. Principle:**
SEM works on the principle of **raster-scanning a finely focused electron probe** across the specimen surface and detecting the **electron–specimen interaction signals** (SE, BSE, X-rays) to form a magnified image. Magnification is achieved by making the **scan area on the sample much smaller** than the **display area on the monitor** (M = L_display / W_scan).

**2. Instrumentation (Block Diagram — see §4.1):**
- **Electron gun** (Tungsten/LaB₆/FE) — produces electrons by thermionic or field emission.
- **Electromagnetic lenses** (condenser + objective) — demagnify the source to a **1–10 nm probe**.
- **Apertures** — control beam current and convergence.
- **Scan coils** — deflect the beam in a TV-like raster synchronized with the display.
- **Specimen stage** — 5-axis eucentric movement (X, Y, Z, tilt, rotation).
- **Detectors** — Everhart–Thornley (SE), solid-state (BSE), EDS (X-rays).
- **Vacuum system** — rotary + turbomolecular + ion pumps.
- **Display system** — amplifier → ADC → image processor → monitor.

**3. Working:**
- Filament heated → electrons emitted.
- Accelerated by $V$ = 0.5–30 kV.
- Focused by lenses into a fine probe.
- Scanned in raster across specimen.
- Interaction generates SE, BSE, X-rays.
- Detectors convert signals → image reconstructed pixel-by-pixel.

#### Part (b) — Signals and Applications (7 marks)

**The five primary signals:**
1. **Secondary Electrons (SE):** < 50 eV, low escape depth, give **topographic contrast**. Resolution ~1 nm. Detector: Everhart–Thornley (positively biased Faraday cage + scintillator + photomultiplier).
2. **Backscattered Electrons (BSE):** > 50 eV, elastic backscatter, give **Z-contrast** (atomic number). Used to image compositional variation in alloys, minerals.
3. **Characteristic X-rays:** Element-specific energies from inner-shell ionization. **EDS detector** provides elemental composition and mapping.
4. **Auger Electrons:** From radiationless de-excitation; give **top ~1 nm surface chemistry**. Requires UHV; more common in AES.
5. **Cathodoluminescence (CL):** e–h recombination → photon emission. Used to study optical/electronic properties of semiconductors, minerals (zircon, quartz).

**Applications in Nanotechnology:**
- Particle size and shape of nanoparticles.
- Fracture surface analysis of nanocomposites.
- Thin film cross-sectional imaging.
- EDS mapping of catalyst distribution.
- CNT/MXene morphology.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Modified]`

**"Derive the expression for the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential V. Hence, calculate the wavelength and limiting resolution at 30 kV. Compare SEM with TEM in terms of resolution, sample preparation, and information obtained."**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of de Broglie Wavelength and Resolution (7 marks)

**Derivation — step by step:**

From de Broglie relation:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

For an electron accelerated through potential V:

$$eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 \implies p = m_e v = \sqrt{2 m_e eV}$$

Substituting:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV}} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V(\text{V})}} \text{ nm}$$

**Numerical calculation at V = 30 kV = 30,000 V (non-relativistic):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{30000}} = \frac{1.227}{173.21} = 7.085 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} = 7.085 \text{ pm}$$

**Relativistic correction at 30 kV:** $E = 30$ keV, rest energy $m_e c^2 = 511$ keV. The relativistic correction factor is:

$$\lambda_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{non-rel}}}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}}} = \frac{7.085 \text{ pm}}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{30}{1022}}} = \frac{7.085}{\sqrt{1.0294}} = \frac{7.085}{1.0146} \approx 6.98 \text{ pm}$$

**Resolution limit (Rayleigh):**
Using $\alpha = 10$ mrad, $n = 1$:

$$d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{\alpha} = \frac{0.61 \times 6.98 \text{ pm}}{0.010} \approx 0.426 \text{ nm}$$

**Practical SEM resolution** is, however, limited by **aberrations and probe size** (~1–10 nm), not by the diffraction limit.

#### Part (b) — SEM vs TEM Comparison (7 marks)

| Aspect | SEM | TEM |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Resolution** | ~1 nm (FE-SEM: ~0.5 nm) | ~0.05 nm (atomic-scale) |
| **Source** | Surface (SE/BSE) | Transmission through ultra-thin sample |
| **Sample prep** | Bulk, conductive coating (Au/Pt sputter) | Ultra-thin section (<100 nm); ion milling / ultramicrotomy |
| **Vacuum** | 10⁻²–10⁻⁶ Pa | 10⁻⁵–10⁻⁷ Pa |
| **Magnification** | 10× – 10⁶× | 50× – 10⁷× |
| **Information** | Morphology, topography, composition (EDS) | Internal structure, crystal lattice, atomic columns |
| **3D info** | Yes (stereo pair) | No (projection) |
| **Field of view** | Larger (mm range) | Very small (µm range) |
| **Cost & maintenance** | Moderate | Very high |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SEM with TEM** — many students incorrectly state SEM resolution is "atomic." SEM is **not atomic resolution** (only TEM/STEM with aberration correctors reaches that).
> 2. **Forgetting the relativistic correction** for V > 30 kV — examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Wrong units in derivation** — remember to convert metres to nanometres explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing SE/BSE roles** — SE = topography, BSE = Z-contrast. Swapping them is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Missing the vacuum justification** — examiners expect *three* reasons (electron scattering, filament oxidation, contamination), not just one.
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram** in 14-mark questions — it carries **at least 3 marks** by itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: SEM**

- ✅ **SEM = Surface technique** that scans a focused electron beam (1–10 nm probe) across the specimen in a raster pattern.
- ✅ **Resolution:** 1–10 nm (conventional) → ~0.5 nm (FE-SEM) — far better than optical (~200 nm).
- ✅ **Key wavelength equation (non-relativistic):** $\lambda = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V(\text{volts})}}$ nm. **Relativistic correction** mandatory above 30 kV.
- ✅ **Three primary signals:**
  - **SE** (< 50 eV) → topography; escape depth 5–50 nm.
  - **BSE** (> 50 eV) → Z-contrast (compositional).
  - **X-rays** → elemental analysis (EDS).
- ✅ **Two auxiliary signals:** Auger e⁻ (surface chemistry) and Cathodoluminescence (optical properties).
- ✅ **Five subsystems:** Gun → Lenses → Scan coils → Stage/Detectors → Vacuum.
- ✅ **Electron sources:** W (cheap, low res) → LaB₆ (better) → FE (best, ~0.5 nm).
- ✅ **Vacuum range:** **10⁻² to 10⁻⁶ Pa** (high vacuum mode).
- ✅ **Kanaya–Okayama range:** $R = \dfrac{0.0276 \, A \, E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.889} \, \rho}$ µm — gives the penetration depth in µm.
- ✅ **Magnification:** $M = L_{\text{display}} / W_{\text{scan}}$.
- ✅ **Resolution limiters (probe size):** demagnified source ($d_g$), spherical aberration ($d_s$), chromatic aberration ($d_c$).
- ✅ **Sample prep:** Conductive coating (Au/Pt sputter, ~5–20 nm) for non-conducting specimens.
- ✅ **SEM vs TEM:** SEM = surface, bulk samples, easier prep. TEM = internal, thin samples, atomic resolution.
- ✅ **Why vacuum?** (1) Prevent electron-gas scattering, (2) prevent filament oxidation, (3) prevent contamination.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Wide magnification range, large depth of field, elemental analysis via EDS.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Requires vacuum, conductive or coated samples, no internal lattice info.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - Memorize the **block diagram** (5 marks alone in a 14-mark question).
> - Practice **one numerical** on de Broglie wavelength and **one on penetration depth**.
> - Be ready to compare **SE vs BSE** and **SEM vs TEM** — these are *guaranteed* questions.
> - Always state the **accelerating voltage range (0.5–30 kV)** and **vacuum range (10⁻²–10⁻⁶ Pa)** in any descriptive answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS)

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS)**, also referred to as **Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDX/EDAX)**, is a non-destructive microanalytical technique used for the **elemental characterization** (qualitative and quantitative) of a material. It is almost always coupled as a complementary detector to electron microscopes such as **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** or **Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)**.

The technique operates on the principle of **inner-shell ionization**: a high-energy incident electron beam (typically **1–30 keV** in SEM) strikes the sample, ejects an inner-shell (K, L, or M) electron, and the resulting electron-hole is filled by an outer-shell electron. The energy difference is released as a **characteristic X-ray photon** unique to each element (Z ≥ 4, i.e., from Beryllium upwards in modern SDD detectors). The energy of these X-rays is then resolved using a solid-state detector, producing a spectrum of intensity versus X-ray energy (in keV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EDS is the *primary* spectroscopic tool expected in Module 3 for confirming the composition of synthesized nanomaterials (nanoparticles, nanowires, quantum dots) alongside structural techniques like XRD and morphological tools like SEM/TEM.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fingerprint of Atoms" Intuition

Imagine a high-speed **billiard ball** (the electron beam) striking a **multi-layered dartboard** (an atom with electron shells K, L, M, …). When the ball hits the innermost (K-shell) ring hard enough, it **knocks out** a target. A player from the outer ring immediately moves in to fill the empty spot, and in doing so, **drops a coloured chip onto the floor**. The **colour of the chip** depends *only* on which ring the player came from and which ring was vacated — this is fixed for every element on the periodic table, much like a unique **fingerprint**.

In EDS:
- **Billiard ball** → Primary electron (from SEM/TEM gun, energy **E₀**)
- **Dartboard rings** → K, L, M shells of the atom
- **Knocked-out target** → Inner-shell electron (ionization)
- **Player dropping a chip** → Outer-shell electron filling the vacancy
- **Colour of the chip** → Energy (and therefore wavelength) of the emitted X-ray
- **Spectrometer** → The "observer" sorting chips by colour to identify the element

So when you see a sharp peak at **E = 1.486 keV** in your EDS spectrum, you can immediately read off: *“That is Aluminium (Al) — Kα line.”* — no chemistry needed, just physics!

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are central to X-ray energy calculations and must be memorized:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value (CODATA 2018) |
|---|---|---|
| Planck's constant | **h** | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| Speed of light in vacuum | **c** | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Electron volt to Joule | **1 eV** | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J** |
| Rydberg constant | **R∞** | **1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹** |
| Classical electron radius | **r_e** | **2.818 × 10⁻¹⁵ m** |

> [!NOTE]
> A single X-ray photon in the typical EDS energy range (0.1 – 20 keV) carries an energy of about **10⁻¹⁶ to 10⁻¹⁵ J**. The detector must therefore be a *single-photon counting* device with very high quantum efficiency.

## 1.4 Position of EDS in the Characterization Toolkit

For a typical nanomaterial, EDS is the third pillar of characterization, complementing:

1. **XRD** → Crystal structure / phase identification
2. **SEM/TEM** → Morphology, particle size
3. **EDS** → *“Of what is it made, and in what proportion?”* (composition)

> [!TIP]
> In KTU theory questions, EDS is often *paired* with TEM/SAED for nanostructures. Be prepared to draw a labelled schematic showing the EDS detector attached to the SEM column.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence of an EDS Measurement

The full physical process from "electron hits sample" to "spectrum is plotted" can be decomposed into **seven sequential steps**. Understanding each step is essential for KTU derivation questions.

### Step 1 — Beam–Sample Interaction (Electron–Atom Collision)
A primary electron from the SEM/TEM gun, accelerated through a potential **V**, gains kinetic energy

$$E_0 = eV$$

where **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** and **V** is in volts. The electron penetrates the sample and undergoes **elastic** (Rutherford) and **inelastic** scattering. For EDS, the relevant inelastic process is **inner-shell ionization**, which has a finite probability quantified by the **ionization cross-section σ**.

### Step 2 — Inner-Shell Ionization (K, L, M Shells)
The primary electron transfers energy **ΔE** to a bound electron. Ionization occurs only if:

$$\Delta E \ge E_c$$

where **E_c** is the **critical ionization energy** (or *absorption edge energy*) of the relevant shell. E_c is element- and shell-specific; for the K-shell it is roughly

$$E_c^K \approx R_\infty (Z-\sigma_s)^2 \left(\frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{\infty^2}\right) \approx 13.6\,(Z-\sigma_s)^2 \text{ eV}$$

with the screening constant **σ_s ≈ 1** for K-shell electrons.

### Step 3 — Atomic Relaxation (Characteristic X-ray Emission)
The ionized atom is unstable. An electron from a higher shell (e.g., L) drops into the K-shell vacancy. The energy difference is released either as:
- A **characteristic X-ray photon** (radiative transition — what EDS detects), or
- An **Auger electron** (non-radiative transition — what AES detects).

The *radiative yield* **ω** (fluorescence yield) is shell-dependent:

| Shell | Approximate Fluorescence Yield (ω) |
|---|---|
| K (low Z, e.g. Na, Z=11) | ~ 0.02 |
| K (high Z, e.g. Cu, Z=29) | ~ 0.44 |
| K (Au, Z=79) | ~ 0.96 |
| L shells | typically 0.01 – 0.40 |
| M shells | typically 0.01 – 0.10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **light elements (Z < 11)**, the K-shell fluorescence yield is very low. EDS becomes inefficient; Auger Electron Spectroscopy (AES) is preferred. This is a common KTU question.

### Step 4 — X-ray Naming Convention (Siegbahn Notation)
Transitions are named by the shells involved:

| Transition | Origin → Destination | Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| L₃ → K | 2p₃/₂ → 1s | **Kα₁** (most intense K line) |
| L₂ → K | 2p₁/₂ → 1s | **Kα₂** |
| M₅ → K | 3d₅/₂ → 1s | **Kβ₁** |
| M₃ → K | 3p₃/₂ → 1s | **Kβ₃** |
| M₅ → L₃ | 3d₅/₂ → 2p₃/₂ | **Lα₁** |

Selection rules: **Δl = ±1**, **Δj = 0, ±1** (electric dipole transitions).

### Step 5 — Detection by a Solid-State Detector
Modern EDS uses a **Silicon Drift Detector (SDD)** (or older Si-Li detector). The incoming X-ray photon is absorbed in the Si crystal, creating **N = E_photon / ε** electron–hole pairs, where **ε = 3.62 eV** is the average ionization energy of Si at 77 K. A bias voltage collects the charge; the pulse height is proportional to the photon energy — hence "energy *dispersive*."

### Step 6 — Multi-Channel Analyzer (MCA) Sorting
The pulses are amplified and sorted by a **Multi-Channel Analyser (MCA)** into 1024 or 2048 channels spanning the energy range (typically 0 – 20 keV). Each channel corresponds to a narrow energy window of about **10 eV** width.

### Step 7 — Spectrum Plot and Quantification
The resulting **counts vs. energy (keV)** histogram is the EDS spectrum. Peaks are identified by energy, and peak areas (after background subtraction) yield, through **ZAF correction**, the elemental concentration.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_0 = eV$ | Primary electron energy | $E_0$ in eV, $V$ in V |
| 2 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | $h$ in J·s, $\lambda$ in m |
| 3 | $E_{\text{photon}} = E_c^{(j)} - E_c^{(i)}$ | Characteristic X-ray energy (shells i, j) | in eV or keV |
| 4 | $\lambda \, (\text{Å}) = \dfrac{12.398}{E \, (\text{keV})}$ | Wavelength–Energy conversion (K-shell lines) | useful constant |
| 5 | $\sqrt{\nu} = C(Z - \sigma)$ | **Moseley's Law** | $\nu$ frequency, $Z$ atomic number, $\sigma$ screening constant |
| 6 | $E_c^K \approx 13.6\,(Z-\sigma_s)^2 \text{ eV}$ | K-edge energy (Hydrogen-like approximation) | $\sigma_s \approx 1$ for K |
| 7 | $N = E_{\text{photon}} / \varepsilon$ | Electron–hole pairs in Si detector | $\varepsilon = 3.62$ eV |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E}$ | Energy resolution of detector (FWHM) | typical SDD: $R \approx 0.125$ keV at Mn-Kα |
| 9 | $C_i = \dfrac{I_i / (ZAF)_i}{\sum_j I_j / (ZAF)_j}$ | ZAF-corrected concentration | $C_i$ mass or atomic fraction |
| 10 | $\sigma_K = \dfrac{\pi e^2}{E_0 E_c} \cdot b \ln\!\left(\dfrac{c E_0}{E_c}\right)$ | K-shell ionization cross-section (Bethe formula) | $b, c$ constants, $\sigma_K$ in cm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-table-break rule applied:** All vertical bars in formulas (such as $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert \vec{r} \vert$) are written using `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

## 2.3 Why EDS Works — The "Why and How" Behind Each Step

- **Why is the X-ray energy characteristic?** Because the binding energies of inner-shell electrons are quantized and depend *only* on the element's atomic number **Z** and the shell's principal quantum number. Therefore the *energy difference* between shells is a unique tag of the element.
- **How does the detector resolve energies?** By counting electron–hole pairs (N). The number N is proportional to photon energy. The statistical fluctuation in N gives a Gaussian peak with **FWHM ≈ 2.35 √(FεE)**, where **F ≈ 0.12** is the Fano factor. This explains the *physical limit* of energy resolution.
- **Why are L-lines used for heavy elements?** For Z > 50 (e.g., Au, Pb), the K-edge energy exceeds 30 keV, often above the energy of primary electrons in standard SEMs (15–25 keV). L-lines (lower energy) become the practical choice.

## 2.4 Engineering and Nanotechnology Utility

| Application | Role of EDS |
|---|---|
| **Nanoparticle composition** | Confirm doping (e.g., Fe in TiO₂) in photocatalysts |
| **Quantum dot purity** | Verify Cd:Se:S ratios in CdSe/ZnS QDs |
| **Catalyst poisoning studies** | Map trace metals on nanoparticle surfaces |
| **Failure analysis in nanofabrication** | Identify sub-micron contamination particles on Si wafers |
| **2D materials (graphene, MoS₂)** | Confirm stoichiometry; distinguish MoS₂ from MoO₃ |
| **Biomedical NPs** | Confirm uptake of AuNPs in cells, presence of Fe in SPIONs |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Characteristic X-ray Energy from a Bohr-like Atom Model

**Goal:** Show that the Kα X-ray energy scales approximately as $E_{K\alpha} \propto (Z-1)^2$ for moderate Z.

**Starting model:** Treat the K-shell electron (1s) as hydrogen-like with effective nuclear charge $Z_{\text{eff}} = (Z - \sigma_s)$, where $\sigma_s = 1$ accounts for screening by the other K electron. Then the binding energy of the 1s electron is:

$$
E_{K} = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2}(Z-\sigma_s)^2 \approx -13.6\,(Z-1)^2 \text{ eV}
$$

For the L-shell (2p electron), screening is much larger; the effective charge is roughly $Z_{\text{eff}} \approx (Z - 3.5)$. The L-shell binding energy is:

$$
E_{L} = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2}\frac{(Z-3.5)^2}{2^2} \approx -\frac{13.6}{4}\,(Z-3.5)^2 \text{ eV}
$$

The Kα X-ray photon energy (energy released when an L electron falls into the K vacancy) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{K\alpha} &= E_{L} - E_{K} \\
&= -13.6\left[\frac{(Z-3.5)^2}{4} - (Z-1)^2\right] \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The minus-of-minus is a positive emission energy; the first term is the *negative* L energy (less bound), the second is the *negative* K energy (more bound).

Expanding the bracket for a clean leading-order estimate, note that $(Z-1)^2 = (Z - 3.5 + 2.5)^2 \approx (Z-3.5)^2 + 5(Z-3.5) + 6.25$, so

$$
\frac{(Z-3.5)^2}{4} - (Z-1)^2 \approx -\frac{3}{4}(Z-3.5)^2 - 5(Z-3.5) - 6.25
$$

The dominant term scales as $(Z-3.5)^2$, confirming **Moseley's Law**: $E_{K\alpha} \approx A(Z-\sigma)^2$ for some constants $A$ and $\sigma$.

> **Sanity check (numerical):** For Cu, Z = 29. $E_{K\alpha} = 13.6 \cdot [29 - 1]^2 \cdot \frac{3}{4} \approx 8.04 \text{ keV}$. The measured value is **8.04 keV** (Cu Kα₁ = 8.0478 keV). Excellent agreement!

## 3.2 Derivation II — Moseley's Law and the Linear $\sqrt{\nu}$ vs. $Z$ Relation

**Goal:** Derive that $\sqrt{\nu_{K\alpha}} \propto (Z - \sigma_K)$, where $\sigma_K \approx 1$ is the K-shell screening constant.

From the previous result, the frequency of the emitted photon is $\nu = E_{K\alpha}/h$. Writing

$$
E_{K\alpha} = h\nu = R_\infty h c\,(Z-\sigma_K)^2 \left(\frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2}\right) = \frac{3}{4} R_\infty h c (Z-\sigma_K)^2
$$

Solving for $\sqrt{\nu}$:

$$
\sqrt{\nu} = \sqrt{\frac{3 R_\infty c}{4}}\,(Z-\sigma_K)
$$

Therefore $\sqrt{\nu}$ is a *linear* function of $Z$, with slope $C = \sqrt{3R_\infty c/4}$. This is the famous **Moseley plot**, which historically established Z as a *measurable* integer (1913–1914).

> **Logic summary (textual):** We started from a hydrogen-like model with two corrections — (i) replace $Z$ by $(Z-\sigma)$ for screening, (ii) use the Lyman–Balmer energy difference formula. Combining these two ideas gives Moseley's law in two lines.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Energy Resolution of an SDD Detector

**Goal:** Show why the EDS energy resolution has a physical lower limit of about 100–130 eV.

In a Si detector, an X-ray photon of energy $E$ generates $N = E/\varepsilon$ electron–hole pairs, where $\varepsilon = 3.62$ eV. The *variance* in $N$ due to the stochastic nature of pair creation is

$$
\sigma_N^2 = F \cdot N
$$

where $F \approx 0.115$ is the **Fano factor** for Si at 77 K. The energy variance is

$$
\sigma_E^2 = \varepsilon^2 \sigma_N^2 = F \varepsilon E
$$

The **Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM)** of the resulting Gaussian peak is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FWHM} &= 2\sqrt{2 \ln 2}\;\sigma_E \\
&= 2.355\,\sqrt{F \varepsilon E}
\end{aligned}
$$

Plugging in $E = 5.894$ keV (Mn Kα — the calibration line of EDS) and $\varepsilon = 3.62 \times 10^{-3}$ keV, $F = 0.115$:

$$
\text{FWHM}_{\text{Mn-K}\alpha} = 2.355\sqrt{0.115 \times 3.62 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.894} \text{ keV}
$$

$$
= 2.355\sqrt{2.45 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ keV} = 2.355 \times 0.0495 \text{ keV} = 0.117 \text{ keV}
$$

This matches the manufacturer specification of modern SDDs (≈ **121–129 eV at Mn Kα**). The small additional broadening comes from electronic noise.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Wavelength–Energy Conversion Constant

**Goal:** Prove the constant 12.398 Å·keV used in EDS tables.

From $E = h c / \lambda$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{hc}{E}
$$

with $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, and $E$ in joules. To get $\lambda$ in Å ($10^{-10}$ m) when $E$ is in keV:

$$
\lambda \,(\text{Å}) = \frac{hc \,(\text{J·m})}{E \,(\text{J})} \times \frac{1 \text{ Å}}{10^{-10}\text{ m}}
$$

Convert $E$ from keV to J: $E (\text{J}) = E(\text{keV}) \times 1.602 \times 10^{-16}$. So

$$
\lambda \,(\text{Å}) = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}{E(\text{keV}) \times 1.602 \times 10^{-16} \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{1.240 \times 10^{-7}}{1.602 \times 10^{-26}} \times \frac{1}{E}
$$

$$
= \frac{12.398}{E \,(\text{keV})} \text{ Å}
$$

This derivation explains the origin of the constant, which is the same constant that appears in XRD (where it is used to compute $d$-spacings from X-ray wavelengths).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Step-By-Step Problem Solving Pattern

For any "given Z, find Kα energy" or "given energy, identify element" problem in the KTU exam, follow this five-step rubric:

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify atomic number Z from problem data | numeric Z |
| 2 | Choose relevant shell (K, L, M) based on E₀ and Z | shell label |
| 3 | Apply $E_{K\alpha} \approx 10.2 \times 10^{-3}\,(Z-1)^2$ keV (semi-empirical) | energy estimate |
| 4 | Compare with database value | accept/reject |
| 5 | State the limitation (light-element Auger, line overlap) | comment |

> **Logic step row:** Each row corresponds to a single thinking step a student must write in the answer book. KTU examiners reward explicit step listing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — EDS Functional Architecture in an SEM Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electron Gun - Tungsten Filament or FEG] --> B[Accelerating Voltage 1 to 30 keV]
    B --> C[Condenser and Objective Lenses - focus beam]
    C --> D[Scanning Coils - raster the beam]
    D --> E[Sample in SEM Chamber - Nanomaterial on stub]
    E --> F1[Secondary Electrons - to Everhart Thornley Detector]
    E --> F2[Backscattered Electrons - to BSE Detector]
    E --> F3[Characteristic X-rays - to EDS Detector]
    E --> F4[Auger Electrons - to AES if fitted]
    F3 --> G[SDD Silicon Drift Detector at 77 K LN2 or Peltier]
    G --> H[Preamplifier - integrates charge pulse]
    H --> I[Main Amplifier and Pulse Processor]
    I --> J[Multi Channel Analyser MCA 2048 channels]
    J --> K[Spectrum - counts vs Energy keV]
    K --> L[Qualitative Analysis - peak identification]
    K --> M[Quantitative Analysis - ZAF correction]
    L --> N[Report - elemental composition of nanomaterial]
    M --> N
```

> **Reading guide for students:** Follow the **path E → F3 → G → H → I → J → K**. That single line traces the journey of *one* X-ray photon from emission to a dot on your spectrum.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Levels and X-ray Line Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHELLS[Atomic Energy Levels]
        Kshell[K shell n equals 1 s]
        Lshell[L shell n equals 2 p]
        Mshell[M shell n equals 3 d]
        Nshell[N shell n equals 4]
    end
    Lshell -->|Ka1 transition 2p3 slash 2 to 1s| Kshell
    Lshell -->|Ka2 transition 2p1 slash 2 to 1s| Kshell
    Mshell -->|Kb1 transition 3d to 1s| Kshell
    Mshell -->|La1 transition 3d to 2p| Lshell
    Nshell -->|Lg1 transition 4f to 2p| Lshell
    Nshell -->|Ma1 transition 4f to 3d| Mshell
    Kshell -->|Ionization by primary electron| VacK[Vacancy in K shell]
    VacK -.-> Lshell
    style Kshell fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style Lshell fill:#dfd,stroke:#333
    style Mshell fill:#ddf,stroke:#333
    style Nshell fill:#fdd,stroke:#333
```

> **How to read this diagram:** An *ionization event* removes a K-shell electron (VacK), creating a vacancy. Arrows pointing *downward* into K represent X-ray emission. Each arrow corresponds to one of the Siegbahn lines (Kα₁, Kα₂, Kβ₁, …).

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Detection Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[X-ray Photon Energy E in keV] --> B[Si Crystal absorbs photon]
    B --> C[Generate N equals E over 3.62 eV electron hole pairs]
    C --> D[Electric field sweeps charges to anode]
    D --> E[Charge pulse with height proportional to E]
    E --> F[Preamplifier - charge to voltage conversion]
    F --> G[Shaping Amplifier - Gaussian pulse]
    G --> H[ADC - analog to digital converter]
    H --> I[Channel k corresponding to energy Ek]
    I --> J[Increment count in channel k by 1]
    J --> K[Final histogram - EDS Spectrum]
    style B fill:#ffe,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#efe,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#eef,stroke:#333
```

> **Why this diagram is useful in KTU answers:** Examiners often give 2–3 marks for a *labelled block diagram* of the detector. This block diagram is the perfect 3-mark answer to "Draw and explain the working of an SDD."

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which X-ray Line to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Element with atomic number Z to analyze] --> Q1{Z less than 11}
    Q1 -- Yes --> LowZ[Light element - use K line at low keV]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Z between 11 and 50}
    Q2 -- Yes --> MidZ[K line - dominant and well resolved]
    Q2 -- No --> HighZ[Z greater than 50 - K line above 25 keV]
    HighZ --> Q3{SEM E0 sufficient}
    Q3 -- No --> UseL[Use L line 1 to 5 keV range]
    Q3 -- Yes --> UseK[Use K line if E0 above K edge]
    LowZ --> Note[Caution - Auger dominates, low fluorescence yield omega]
    MidZ --> OK[Standard EDS - K alpha around 1 to 10 keV]
    UseL --> OK2[L alpha around 1 to 5 keV - check overlaps]
```

> **Pedagogical value:** This is the *exact decision logic* a graduate student uses at the microscope. Memorize it for KTU viva questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"What is the principle of Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS)? Why is it coupled to SEM/TEM?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EDS works on the principle of **inner-shell ionization and characteristic X-ray emission**. When a high-energy primary electron (1–30 keV) from the SEM/TEM beam strikes the sample atoms, it can eject an inner-shell (K, L, or M) electron if its energy exceeds the critical ionization energy E_c. The resulting vacancy is filled by an outer-shell electron, and the energy difference is emitted as an **X-ray photon whose energy is characteristic of the element** (its "fingerprint").

**Why coupled to SEM/TEM (2 marks):** SEM/TEM provides (i) a focused, high-energy electron beam that can induce inner-shell ionization in nanovolumes (~1 nm beam in TEM, ~10 nm in SEM), (ii) morphological context for the same region being analyzed, and (iii) ultra-high vacuum for X-ray detection. Without the electron beam, no characteristic X-rays are produced; without the EDS detector, the SEM/TEM cannot perform elemental analysis.

*(1 mark for the principle + 2 marks for the coupling justification — KTU mark distribution typical for a 3-mark part A question.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define 'fluorescence yield'. Why is the Kα line preferred over Kβ for quantification in EDS?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **fluorescence yield (ω)** is defined as the probability that an inner-shell vacancy is filled by a *radiative* (X-ray) transition rather than a non-radiative Auger process:

$$
\omega = \frac{\text{number of characteristic X-rays emitted}}{\text{number of inner-shell vacancies created}}
$$

**Why Kα over Kβ for quantification (2 marks):**
1. The Kα line (L→K transition) is **roughly 5–7 times more intense** than the Kβ line (M→K transition) for most elements, because the L-shell has many more available sub-shell states and the transition probability is higher.
2. Kα is a *cleaner* line, less overlapped with neighbouring-element lines, giving a larger peak-to-background ratio for trace-element quantification.

*(1 mark for the fluorescence-yield definition + 2 marks for the Kα preference — a frequent KTU concept-testing question.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> KTU Pattern: Each Module typically contributes a 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below; **either** may appear in the exam.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** *With a neat schematic, explain the working of a Silicon Drift Detector (SDD) used in EDS. Discuss its advantages over the older Si(Li) detector. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Derive Moseley's Law from a hydrogen-like atom model and show that $\sqrt{\nu} \propto (Z - \sigma)$ for K-series X-rays. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Working of SDD (4 marks):**

An SDD is a fully depleted silicon wafer (typically 5–10 mm thick, 5–10 mm² area) with a small central collecting anode and a series of concentric **drift rings** on both faces, biased such that a transverse electric field guides the liberated charge carriers (electrons) toward the anode.

Step-by-step signal path:
1. **Photon absorption:** The X-ray photon of energy E enters through a thin (~8 µm) Be window (or polymer ultra-thin window for light elements) and is absorbed in the Si bulk via the photoelectric effect.
2. **Pair creation:** The photoelectron (and any Si K-shell secondary fluorescence) generates **N = E/ε** electron–hole pairs, with **ε = 3.62 eV** in Si at the operating temperature.
3. **Drift under bias:** The applied ring voltages create a parabolic potential well, drifting electrons laterally toward the central anode; holes are collected by the surrounding cathode. The drift is *fast* (tens of ns) and is *not* limited by the detector thickness.
4. **Charge integration:** The anode integrates the charge; the preamplifier outputs a step-pulse whose height is proportional to E.
5. **Pulse processing:** A shaping amplifier produces a Gaussian pulse (shaping time ~1 µs), whose peak height is digitized by the ADC and assigned to a channel in the MCA.

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Photon] → [Be window] → [Si crystal + drift rings] → [Central anode] 
         → [Preamplifier] → [Shaping amplifier] → [ADC + MCA] → [Spectrum channel]
```

**Advantages of SDD over Si(Li) (3 marks):**

| Feature | Si(Li) | SDD |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling | Liquid N₂ (77 K) mandatory | Peltier cooling (≤ −30 °C) sufficient |
| Throughput | Low (~1000 cps) | Very high (up to 100,000 cps) |
| Resolution at Mn Kα | ~130–138 eV | ~121–129 eV |
| Drift time | N/A (charge diffuses) | Short, deterministic → better peak resolution |
| Form factor | Large, with LN₂ dewar | Compact, fits inside SEM chamber closer to sample |

Higher count-rate capability is the biggest advantage — essential for fast mapping of nanostructures.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Model Assumption (1 mark):** Treat the K-shell (1s) electron as hydrogen-like with effective nuclear charge $(Z - \sigma_s)$ where $\sigma_s \approx 1$ (screening by the other K electron). Similarly, the L-shell (2p) electron sees an effective charge $(Z - \sigma_L)$ with $\sigma_L \approx 3.5$ due to greater screening.

**K-shell binding energy (1 mark):**

$$
E_K = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8\varepsilon_0^2 h^2}(Z-\sigma_s)^2 = -13.6\,(Z-1)^2 \text{ eV}
$$

**L-shell binding energy (1 mark):**

$$
E_L = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8\varepsilon_0^2 h^2}\frac{(Z-3.5)^2}{4} = -\frac{13.6}{4}(Z-3.5)^2 \text{ eV}
$$

**Kα X-ray energy (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{K\alpha} &= E_L - E_K \\
&= 13.6\left[(Z-1)^2 - \frac{(Z-3.5)^2}{4}\right] \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the conversion $E = h\nu$ and the empirical approximation that the leading-order scaling is $\propto (Z - 1)^2$, we obtain

$$
h\nu_{K\alpha} \approx \frac{3}{4} \cdot 13.6\,(Z-1)^2 \text{ eV} = 10.2\,(Z-1)^2 \text{ eV}
$$

**Moseley's Law (2 marks):**

$$
\sqrt{\nu_{K\alpha}} = \sqrt{\frac{3R_\infty c}{4}}\,(Z-1) = C(Z-\sigma_K)
$$

where $C = \sqrt{3R_\infty c/4} \approx 2.47 \times 10^{7}$ Hz$^{1/2}$ and $\sigma_K = 1$.

Hence a plot of $\sqrt{\nu}$ versus Z is a *straight line* with slope C. Moseley measured this in 1913 and used it to identify missing elements and to *fix* the atomic number sequence — a landmark in the history of physics.

**Numerical sanity check (1 mark):** For Cu (Z=29), $\nu = 1.95 \times 10^{18}$ Hz → $E = h\nu = 8.05$ keV, matching the measured Cu Kα₁ = 8.0478 keV. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** *Discuss the ZAF correction method used in quantitative EDS analysis. Mention the physical origin of each factor. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A Cu Kα X-ray has energy 8.048 keV. Calculate (i) its wavelength, (ii) its frequency, and (iii) verify that the photon energy equals the energy difference between the Cu K-edge (8.979 keV) and Cu L₃-edge (0.933 keV). (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**ZAF Correction — Definition (1 mark):** In quantitative EDS, the *measured* X-ray intensity $I_i$ from element *i* is not directly proportional to the concentration $C_i$, because the intensity is affected by three physical factors collectively called **ZAF**: atomic number (Z), Absorption (A), and Fluorescence (F). The corrected concentration is:

$$
C_i = \frac{I_i / (ZAF)_i}{\sum_j I_j / (ZAF)_j} \times 100\%
$$

**Z — Atomic Number Effect (2 marks):** The atomic-number effect combines **(i) backscatter correction** (some primary electrons backscatter out of the sample before producing X-rays) and **(ii) stopping power** (the rate at which the electron loses energy per unit path length, $S = -dE/dx$). Heavier atoms have higher backscatter and higher stopping power, both reducing the generated X-ray intensity per unit mass. The Z-factor compensates this by computing the *generated intensity* from first principles using the Bethe cross-section $\sigma_K$.

**A — Absorption Correction (2 marks):** Once generated, the X-ray photon must travel through the sample to reach the detector. Some are absorbed by the photoelectric effect. The transmission fraction is

$$
T = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\mu}{\rho}\,\rho\,z\,\csc\psi\right)
$$

where $\mu/\rho$ is the mass-absorption coefficient (cm²/g), $\rho z$ the mass thickness, and $\psi$ the take-off angle. A-factors use tabulated $\mu/\rho$ (e.g., from the Heinrich or Chantler databases) and the actual geometry.

**F — Fluorescence Correction (1 mark):** The X-rays of element *i* can be *enhanced* if another element *j* in the sample emits X-rays whose energy exceeds the absorption edge of *i*. This "secondary fluorescence" can add 5–30% intensity to the apparent line, especially when an Fe Kα line (6.40 keV) enhances a Cr Kα line (5.41 keV) in stainless steel. F-factors subtract this enhancement using Sherman equations.

**Limitation note (1 mark):** ZAF assumes a *flat, homogeneous, polished* sample. For nanomaterials — which are often rough, agglomerated, or supported on a thin film — the **φ(ρz) or "matrix" correction** is preferred.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $E_{K\alpha,\text{Cu}} = 8.048 \text{ keV}$.

**(i) Wavelength (3 marks):** Using $E = hc/\lambda$,

$$
\lambda = \frac{hc}{E}
$$

Convert to SI: $E = 8.048 \times 10^{3} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 1.289 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$.

$$
\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}{1.289 \times 10^{-15}} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{1.987 \times 10^{-25}}{1.289 \times 10^{-15}} = 1.541 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} = 1.541 \text{ Å}
$$

**(ii) Frequency (2 marks):**

$$
\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{2.998 \times 10^{8}}{1.541 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.945 \times 10^{18} \text{ Hz}
$$

**Cross-check (1 mark):**

$$
E = h\nu = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 1.945 \times 10^{18} = 1.289 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 8.05 \text{ keV} \; ✓
$$

**(iii) Verification of Energy Balance (2 marks):**

Given: Cu K-edge $E_K = 8.979$ keV, Cu L₃-edge $E_{L_3} = 0.933$ keV.

Energy released in L₃ → K transition:

$$
\Delta E = E_K - E_{L_3} = 8.979 - 0.933 = 8.046 \text{ keV}
$$

This matches $E_{K\alpha,\text{Cu}} = 8.048 \text{ keV}$ within 0.002 keV, confirming the energy-balance equation $E_{K\alpha} = E_K - E_{L_3}$. The tiny discrepancy is because $E_{K\alpha} = E_K - E_{L_3} - E_{\text{recoil}}$, and the recoil energy is ~0.002 keV (negligible for a heavy atom). ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in EDS questions:**
> 1. **Confusing WDS with EDS:** WDS (Wavelength Dispersive Spectroscopy) uses Bragg diffraction at a crystal; EDS uses a solid-state detector. Mixing them in the answer is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting the Fano factor:** When asked for the resolution of a Si detector, students quote the FWHM formula without mentioning the Fano factor — the derivation gets *zero* marks.
> 3. **Writing "$E = h\nu$" without units or value:** Always *plug in the numbers* and *show the unit conversion* (keV ↔ J). Examiners penalize "book-formula-only" answers.
> 4. **Not labelling the schematic:** A block diagram without labels (X-ray, Si crystal, preamplifier, MCA, spectrum) is awarded only 1 of 3 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring light-element limitations:** For Z < 11 (Na, C, N, O), state the limitation: low fluorescence yield + Be-window absorption. This is the most common 2-mark "depth-of-understanding" question.
> 6. **Mixing Auger vs. X-ray relaxation:** These are *competing* processes governed by fluorescence yield ω. A complete answer must mention both.
> 7. **Misapplying ZAF:** Many students write $C_i = I_i / \sum I_j$, omitting the ZAF factors. This *looks* right but is fundamentally wrong for *bulk* samples.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist (High Density Summary)

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- **Principle:** Inner-shell ionization + characteristic X-ray emission.
- **Kα line:** L₃ → K transition, most intense K-series line.
- **Kβ line:** M → K transition, ~5× weaker than Kα.
- **Moseley's Law:** $\sqrt{\nu} = C(Z - \sigma)$; slope $C$ is universal for a given series.
- **Fluorescence yield ω:** Probability of X-ray emission; increases with Z; low for light elements.
- **Auger process:** Competing non-radiative relaxation; dominates for Z < 11 in K-shell.
- **Fano factor** $F \approx 0.115$ in Si — gives resolution limit.

### Numbers to Remember
- **Cu Kα:** 8.048 keV (most-cited EDS line).
- **Mn Kα:** 5.894 keV (calibration standard).
- **Detector resolution at Mn Kα:** ≈ 121–129 eV (SDD), 130–138 eV (Si-Li).
- **Pair-creation energy in Si:** ε = 3.62 eV.
- **Constant λ(Å)·E(keV) = 12.398.**
- **Detection limit of EDS:** ~0.1 wt% (1000 ppm); WDS is ~10× better.
- **Spatial resolution of EDS in SEM:** ~1 µm (interaction-volume limited); in STEM-EDS: <5 nm.

### Practical / Experimental Points
- EDS detector is mounted on the SEM chamber at a take-off angle of typically 30–40° from the sample surface.
- Light elements (Z < 4) cannot be detected with a standard Be window — need an ultra-thin window or windowless SDD.
- Spectrum artefacts: **sum peaks** (two photons arriving simultaneously), **escape peaks** (Si Kα escape at $E - 1.74$ keV), **dead-time** losses at high count rates.
- **Mapping mode:** The beam is rastered; an EDS spectrum is collected at each pixel, producing a 2D elemental map — essential for nanoparticle distribution studies.

### Compare-and-Contrast (Favourite KTU Question Style)
- **EDS vs. WDS:** EDS — solid-state detector, fast, lower resolution (~130 eV), multi-element parallel detection; WDS — diffracting crystal, slow, high resolution (~5–10 eV), sequential element-by-element.
- **EDS vs. XPS:** EDS probes bulk ~1 µm; XPS probes top ~5 nm; XPS gives chemical state, EDS does not.
- **EDS vs. Auger:** EDS detects X-rays (good for bulk, heavy elements); Auger detects low-energy electrons (good for surface, light elements).

### Derivation Drills (Practice These on the Answer Book)
1. Moseley's Law from a hydrogen-like model.
2. Fano-limited energy resolution of a Si detector.
3. Wavelength–energy conversion constant 12.398 Å·keV.
4. Bethe cross-section for K-shell ionization.
5. ZAF-correction formula with each factor's physical meaning.

### Typical KTU Question Patterns
- "**Explain the principle and working of EDS with a neat block diagram.**" *(7 marks)*
- "**Derive Moseley's Law.**" *(7 marks)*
- "**Discuss ZAF correction in quantitative EDS.**" *(7 marks)*
- "**Compare EDS and WDS.**" *(3–7 marks)*
- "**A Cu Kα X-ray has energy 8.048 keV. Find its wavelength and frequency.**" *(numerical, 3–7 marks)*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)** is a high-resolution imaging technique in which a high-energy, monochromatic beam of electrons (typically 60–1000 keV) is transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen (thickness **< 100 nm**) to form a magnified image. The image is generated by the **elastic and inelastic scattering** of electrons as they interact with the atomic lattice of the specimen, and is subsequently magnified by a series of **electromagnetic lenses** onto a phosphor screen, photographic plate, or CCD detector.

The technique was first developed by **Ernst Ruska and Max Knoll in 1931**, and Ruska was awarded the **Nobel Prize in Physics in 1986** for his foundational work in electron optics. Modern aberration-corrected TEMs can achieve **sub-Ångstrom (≈ 0.05 nm) resolution**, allowing direct imaging of atomic columns in crystalline materials.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Ultra-Thin Watermarked Paper" Concept**

Imagine holding a thin sheet of paper up against a bright light bulb. If the paper has watermarks or embossed letters, the light passes through differently — dense inked regions let less light through (dark spots), while blank regions let more light through (bright spots). Your eye sees a **shadow image** of the invisible watermark.

**TEM works on exactly this principle**, but with two upgrades:
1. The "light bulb" is replaced by a beam of **high-energy electrons** (which have wavelengths **~100,000× shorter** than visible light, enabling far higher resolution).
2. The "paper" must be **ultra-thin** (< 100 nm, about 1000× thinner than a human hair) so electrons can pass through it without being fully absorbed.

The denser (or thicker) parts of the specimen scatter more electrons → appear **dark**. The thinner/less dense parts allow more electrons to pass → appear **bright**. This contrast is what forms the magnified image on the detector.

---

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Values

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Accelerating Voltage | **60 – 1000 kV** | Determines electron energy & penetrating power |
| Electron Wavelength (λ) | **0.0017 – 0.0037 nm** | Far below visible light (~550 nm) |
| Point Resolution | **0.1 – 0.05 nm** | Atomic-column imaging capability |
| Specimen Thickness | **< 100 nm** | Required for electron transmission |
| Vacuum Level | **10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁷ Pa** | Prevents electron-gas scattering |
| Magnification Range | **×50 to ×50,00,000** | Continuous tuning via lens currents |

---

### 1.4 Why TEM is Critical for Nanomaterials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For nanomaterials (1–100 nm), features are **below the diffraction limit of optical microscopes (~200 nm)**. TEM is one of the **only techniques** capable of directly imaging individual nanoparticles, lattice fringes, dislocations, grain boundaries, and core-shell structures with atomic-scale precision. This makes TEM indispensable in **KTU Module 3** for characterization of carbon nanotubes, quantum dots, nanofibers, and nanocomposites.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why & How"

TEM operates on a sequence of **electron-optical steps**, each performed by an electromagnetic lens. The fundamental principle can be broken down as:

**Step 1 — Electron Generation (Electron Gun)**
- A tungsten (W) filament, LaB₆ crystal, or field-emission tip is heated (thermionic emission) or subjected to a strong electric field (field emission).
- Electrons are emitted and accelerated through a potential difference **V** (typically 60–300 kV).
- *Why?* A coherent, high-energy electron beam is needed because visible light cannot resolve nanoscale features due to its long wavelength.

**Step 2 — Beam Conditioning (Condenser Lens System)**
- Two condenser lenses (C1 and C2) demagnify the electron source and focus a parallel or convergent beam onto the specimen.
- *How?* By varying the lens current, the user controls **beam convergence** and **spot size** — critical for switching between imaging (parallel beam) and diffraction (convergent beam) modes.

**Step 3 — Specimen Interaction**
- The electron beam strikes the ultra-thin specimen.
- **Elastic scattering**: electrons are deflected by Coulomb interaction with nuclei → forms **diffraction contrast** and **mass-thickness contrast**.
- **Inelastic scattering**: electrons lose energy → generates **secondary electrons**, **X-rays (EDS)**, and **plasmon losses (EELS)**.

**Step 4 — Image Formation (Objective + Intermediate + Projector Lenses)**
- The objective lens (the most critical lens) forms the **first intermediate image** and the **diffraction pattern** in the back focal plane.
- The intermediate and projector lenses further magnify the image (×10³ to ×10⁶) and project it onto the viewing screen.

**Step 5 — Detection**
- The image is displayed on a **fluorescent phosphor screen** (real-time observation) and recorded on a **CCD/CMOS camera** or photographic film.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols are standard SI. Note that **|** (pipe) inside mathematical expressions has been replaced with **\vert** to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$ | **De Broglie wavelength** (non-relativistic) | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V$ in volts |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2meV\left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2mc^{2}}\right)}}$ | **Relativistic wavelength** (used for V > 50 kV) | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 3 | $\lambda_{rel} \approx \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6}\,V)}}$ nm | **Practical shortcut formula** | $V$ in volts |
| 4 | $d = 0.61\,\dfrac{\lambda}{n\sin\alpha}$ | **Abbe resolution limit** | $\alpha$ = semi-angle of aperture, $n$ = refractive index (≈1 for electrons in vacuum) |
| 5 | $d_{Scherzer} = 0.66\,(C_{s}\,\lambda^{3})^{1/4}$ | **Scherzer resolution** (with spherical aberration $C_{s}$) | $C_{s}$ = spherical aberration coefficient (mm) |
| 6 | $M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{int} \times M_{proj}$ | **Total magnification** | Each $M_i$ depends on lens focal length |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{e}{8m_{e}V}\int B^{2}(z)\,dz$ | **Electromagnetic lens focal length** | $B(z)$ = axial magnetic flux density |
| 8 | $2d\sin\theta = n\lambda$ | **Bragg's Law** (for SAED pattern indexing) | $d$ = interplanar spacing, $\theta$ = diffraction angle |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Materials Science**: Direct imaging of dislocations, stacking faults, and grain boundaries in metals/alloys.
- **Nanotechnology**: Size, shape, and lattice fringe measurement of nanoparticles, CNTs, and quantum dots.
- **Semiconductor Industry**: Defect analysis in silicon wafers and thin-film transistors.
- **Biology & Medicine**: Visualization of viruses, bacteria, and cellular organelles (after heavy-metal staining).
- **KTU Relevance**: Forms the backbone of characterization for **nanomaterials** studied in this course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: De Broglie Wavelength of an Accelerated Electron

**Starting Premise (Louis de Broglie, 1924):**
Every moving particle has an associated wavelength given by $\lambda = h/p$.

**Step 1 — Relate momentum to kinetic energy**

The electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$. The work done by the electric field equals the kinetic energy gained.

$$
E_{k} = eV = \frac{p^{2}}{2m_{e}}
$$

Rearranging for momentum $p$:

$$
p = \sqrt{2m_{e}eV}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into De Broglie relation**

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{e}eV}}
$$

This is the **non-relativistic** form, valid for $V < 50$ kV approximately.

**Step 3 — Relativistic correction**

At voltages above ~50 kV, the electron velocity approaches a significant fraction of the speed of light, and special relativity must be applied. The relativistic total energy is:

$$
E_{total} = \gamma m_{e}c^{2} = m_{e}c^{2} + eV
$$

where $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - v^{2}/c^{2}}}$. Solving for momentum:

$$
p = \sqrt{2m_{e}eV\left(1 + \frac{eV}{2m_{e}c^{2}}\right)}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda_{rel} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{e}eV\left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2m_{e}c^{2}}\right)}}\;}
$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Evaluation: Wavelength of 100 keV Electron

**Given:**
$V = 100{,}000$ V, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_{e} = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute $eV$**

$$
eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (1 \times 10^{5}) = 1.602 \times 10^{-14}\;\text{J}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute relativistic correction term**

$$
\frac{eV}{2m_{e}c^{2}} = \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times (9.109 \times 10^{-31}) \times (9 \times 10^{16})}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6396 \times 10^{-13}} = 0.0977
$$

Therefore:

$$
1 + 0.0977 = 1.0977
$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator**

$$
2m_{e}eV \times 1.0977 = (2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-14}) \times 1.0977
$$

$$
= 2.919 \times 10^{-44} \times 1.0977 = 3.204 \times 10^{-44}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3.204 \times 10^{-44}} = 1.790 \times 10^{-22}\;\text{kg·m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Final wavelength**

$$
\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.790 \times 10^{-22}} = 3.70 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda \approx 3.70 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{nm} = 3.70\;\text{pm}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This wavelength is **~150,000× shorter** than green visible light (550 nm). This is the fundamental reason why TEM achieves atomic resolution while optical microscopes cannot.

---

### 3.3 Derivation II: Abbe Resolution Limit for TEM

**Starting Premise (Ernst Abbe, 1873):**
Two point objects can be resolved when the central diffraction maximum of one coincides with the first minimum of the other. For a circular aperture (electron beam):

$$
d = \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{n\sin\alpha}
$$

**Application to TEM:**
- Electrons travel in vacuum → refractive index $n \approx 1$.
- $\alpha$ is the objective aperture semi-angle (typically 10–50 mrad).

**For 100 keV electron** ($\lambda = 3.70$ pm), assuming $\alpha = 25$ mrad = 0.025 rad:

$$
d = \frac{0.61 \times 3.70 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times \sin(0.025)} \approx \frac{2.257 \times 10^{-12}}{0.025} \approx 9.0 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d \approx 0.09\;\text{nm}\;}
$$

This matches experimentally achieved point resolutions in modern TEMs (~0.08–0.1 nm).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TEM vs SEM vs Optical Microscope

| Parameter | Optical Microscope | SEM | TEM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source | Visible light | Electron beam (scanned) | Electron beam (transmitted) |
| Wavelength | 400–700 nm | ~0.01 nm | ~0.004 nm |
| Resolution | ~200 nm | ~1–10 nm | **~0.05–0.1 nm** |
| Specimen | Bulk, any | Bulk, surface-coated | Ultra-thin (< 100 nm) |
| Image Type | Surface reflection | Surface topography | Internal structure + lattice |
| Vacuum Required | No | Yes | Yes (high) |
| Max Magnification | ×2000 | ×2,00,000 | ×50,00,000 |
| KTU Application | Basic | Nanoparticle surface | Atomic-level imaging |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of TEM (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electron Gun - W filament or LaB6] --> B[Accelerating Anode 60-300 kV]
    B --> C[Condenser Lens C1 - demagnifies source]
    C --> D[Condenser Aperture - controls beam current]
    D --> E[Condenser Lens C2 - focuses on specimen]
    E --> F[Specimen Stage - ultra-thin sample less than 100 nm]
    F --> G[Objective Lens - forms first image]
    G --> H[Objective Aperture - selects diffracted beam]
    H --> I[Intermediate Lens - switches imaging or diffraction mode]
    I --> J[Projector Lens - final magnification]
    J --> K[Phosphor Screen - real-time viewing]
    J --> L[CCD or CMOS Camera - digital recording]
    J --> M[Vacuum System 10 to power of minus 5 Pa]
    M -.pumps.-> A
    M -.pumps.-> F
```

---

### 4.2 Imaging Mode Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Electron beam exits specimen] --> Q1{Is objective aperture inserted?}
    Q1 -- No, beam straight through --> BF[Bright Field Image BF - direct beam]
    Q1 -- Yes, aperture selects scattered beam --> Q2{Which diffracted beam is selected?}
    Q2 -- Single spot --> DF[Dark Field Image DF - specific reflection]
    Q2 -- Multiple spots centered --> CDF[Centered Dark Field CDF]
    Start --> SAED[Selected Area Electron Diffraction SAED - back focal plane]
    SAED --> Indexing[Index rings or spots to find d spacing]
```

---

### 4.3 Signal Generation Pathways (Electron–Specimen Interaction Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Beam[Incident Electron Beam] --> S1[Elastic Scattering]
    Beam --> S2[Inelastic Scattering]
    S1 --> R1[Diffracted Beams - SAED pattern]
    S1 --> R2[Transmitted Direct Beam - BF image]
    S1 --> R3[Scattered Beam - DF image]
    S2 --> R4[Characteristic X-rays - EDS signal]
    S2 --> R5[Energy Loss Electrons - EELS signal]
    S2 --> R6[Secondary Electrons - SEM-like imaging]
    S2 --> R7[Backscattered Electrons - Z contrast]
```

---

### 4.4 Detailed Component Layout (Vertical Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph top_section[Top Electron Source Column]
        g1[Electron Gun]
        g2[Wehnelt Cylinder - controls emission]
        g3[Anode - accelerates electrons]
        g1 --> g2 --> g3
    end
    g3 --> mid_section
    subgraph mid_section[Illumination and Imaging Column]
        cl1[Condenser Lens 1]
        cl2[Condenser Lens 2]
        ca[Condenser Aperture]
        sa[Specimen and Holder]
        ol[Objective Lens]
        oa[Objective Aperture]
        il[Intermediate Lens]
        pl1[Projector Lens 1]
        pl2[Projector Lens 2]
        cl1 --> cl2 --> ca --> sa --> ol --> oa --> il --> pl1 --> pl2
    end
    pl2 --> bot_section
    subgraph bot_section[Detection System]
        scr[Phosphor Viewing Screen]
        cam[CCD or CMOS Camera]
        vac[Vacuum Pumps attached along column]
        scr --> cam
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

Define **resolving power** of an electron microscope. Why is TEM able to resolve atomic-scale features that an optical microscope cannot?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Resolving power is the **minimum distance between two closely spaced objects** at which they can still be distinguished as separate entities (3 marks for definition).
>
> The wavelength of visible light (~400–700 nm) limits optical resolution to ~200 nm (Rayleigh criterion). In contrast, 100 keV electrons have a wavelength of **~0.0037 nm**, which is ~10⁵ times shorter. Since resolution is proportional to wavelength, TEM achieves ~0.1 nm resolution — small enough to image atomic columns (1 mark for the wavelength-reasoning and 1 mark for the resolution comparison).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

What is the role of **electromagnetic lenses** in a TEM? Name any two types.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Electromagnetic lenses use a **solenoid coil with adjustable current** to generate a magnetic field that focuses the electron beam, similar to how a convex glass lens focuses light (1.5 marks for role).
>
> Two types:
> 1. **Condenser lens** — focuses the electron beam onto the specimen.
> 2. **Objective lens** — forms the primary magnified image (the most critical lens in TEM) (1.5 marks for the two types).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEBBT833]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a Transmission Electron Microscope** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of each major component.

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the wavelength of an accelerated electron**, including relativistic correction, and calculate the wavelength of a **200 keV** electron.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Principle (1 mark):**
A high-energy electron beam is transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen. Electrons are scattered by atoms, and the resulting contrast is magnified by electromagnetic lenses to form an image on a fluorescent screen.

**Construction — Major Components (4 marks, 0.5 each):**

1. **Electron Gun** — Produces electrons by thermionic emission from a W or LaB₆ filament, accelerated by 60–300 kV.
2. **Condenser Lens System** — Two-stage demagnification of the source; controls beam diameter and convergence.
3. **Specimen Stage** — Holds the ultra-thin sample; allows translation, tilt, and rotation.
4. **Objective Lens** — The most critical lens; forms the first image and the diffraction pattern.
5. **Intermediate and Projector Lenses** — Further magnify the image up to ×10⁶.
6. **Apertures** — Condenser aperture (limits beam current) and objective aperture (selects transmitted/scattered beam for BF/DF imaging).
7. **Viewing Screen and Camera** — Phosphor screen for real-time viewing; CCD for digital recording.
8. **Vacuum System** — Maintains 10⁻⁵–10⁻⁷ Pa to prevent electron-gas scattering.

**Working (2 marks):**
Electrons generated → accelerated → focused on specimen → interact (elastic + inelastic scattering) → transmitted/scattered electrons collected by objective lens → magnified → projected onto screen/camera. Two main modes: **Imaging mode** (objective aperture selects direct or scattered beam) and **Diffraction mode** (back focal plane gives SAED pattern).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (4 marks):** See Section 3.1 of these notes for full derivation.

Final relativistic wavelength expression:

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{e}eV\left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2m_{e}c^{2}}\right)}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation for 200 keV (3 marks):**

Given: $V = 2 \times 10^{5}$ V.

$$
eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (2 \times 10^{5}) = 3.204 \times 10^{-14}\;\text{J}
$$

$$
\frac{eV}{2m_{e}c^{2}} = \frac{3.204 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6396 \times 10^{-13}} = 0.1954
$$

$$
1 + 0.1954 = 1.1954
$$

$$
2m_{e}eV \times 1.1954 = (2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.204 \times 10^{-14}) \times 1.1954
$$

$$
= 5.837 \times 10^{-44} \times 1.1954 = 6.977 \times 10^{-44}
$$

$$
\sqrt{6.977 \times 10^{-44}} = 2.641 \times 10^{-22}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{2.641 \times 10^{-22}} = 2.51 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda \approx 2.51 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{nm} = 2.51\;\text{pm}\;}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEBBT833]`

**(a)** Explain **electron–specimen interactions** in TEM. With a diagram, describe **Bright Field, Dark Field, and SAED** imaging modes.

**(b)** Discuss **sample preparation techniques** for TEM analysis of nanomaterials. Compare TEM with SEM.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Electron–Specimen Interactions (3 marks):**

When the high-energy electron beam strikes the specimen, the following interactions occur:

1. **Elastic scattering** — electrons deflected by the Coulomb field of nuclei with **no energy loss**. Forms **diffraction contrast** and is the basis of **SAED**.
2. **Inelastic scattering** — electrons lose energy to atomic electrons. Generates:
   - **Characteristic X-rays** → used in **EDS** for elemental analysis.
   - **Plasmon excitations** → used in **EELS** for electronic structure.
   - **Secondary electrons** and **backscattered electrons** → also used for Z-contrast imaging.
3. **Unscattered (transmitted) electrons** → form the **Bright Field** image.

**Imaging Modes (4 marks, ~1.3 each):**

| Mode | Beam Used | Contrast Type | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bright Field (BF)** | Direct/transmitted beam | Mass-thickness + diffraction | General morphology |
| **Dark Field (DF)** | Specific diffracted beam | Diffraction contrast | Defects, precipitates |
| **Centered DF (CDF)** | Specific spot on optic axis | Enhanced DF contrast | Particle identification |
| **SAED** | All diffracted beams simultaneously | Diffraction pattern | Crystal structure, d-spacing |

(Refer to the diagrams in **Section 4** of these notes.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Sample Preparation Techniques (4 marks):**

For TEM, specimens must be **< 100 nm thick** to allow electron transmission. Common methods:

1. **Ultramicrotomy** — Specimen embedded in resin, sectioned with a diamond knife into 50–70 nm slices. Used for **biological samples, polymers, soft nanomaterials**.
2. **Ion Beam Milling (FIB or Ar⁺)** — Bulk sample thinned by bombarding with high-energy ions. Used for **metals, ceramics, semiconductors**.
3. **Electropolishing** — Electrochemical dissolution of bulk material. Used for **metallic foils**.
4. **Dispersion Method** — Nanoparticles dispersed in solvent, drop-cast on a **carbon-coated Cu grid** (3 mm diameter). Used for **nanotubes, quantum dots, nanopowders**.
5. **Replica Technique** — Surface replica of bulk material. Used for **fracture surfaces**.
6. **Focused Ion Beam (FIB)** lift-out — Site-specific TEM lamella preparation.

**TEM vs SEM Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | TEM | SEM |
|---|---|---|
| Detected electrons | Transmitted | Secondary / backscattered |
| Specimen type | Ultra-thin (< 100 nm) | Bulk / surface |
| Information | Internal structure, lattice | Surface topography |
| Resolution | ~0.05–0.1 nm | ~1–10 nm |
| Max magnification | ×50,00,000 | ×2,00,000 |
| 3D view | No (2D projection) | Yes (stereo pairs possible) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not confuse** "transmitted electrons" in TEM with "secondary electrons" in SEM. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this common slip.
> - When asked to "compare," always present a **tabular form** — KTU examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for a clean comparison table.
> - In numerical problems on electron wavelength, **forgetting the relativistic correction term** at voltages ≥ 50 kV causes a ~10% error and costs 1 mark. Always write the relativistic form.
> - Sample preparation questions require **at least 3 methods** with their respective applications — listing methods without specifying application types loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Takeaways — Must Remember for KTU Exam:**

- **TEM principle**: Electron beam → ultra-thin specimen → scattering → magnified image by EM lenses → detection.
- **De Broglie wavelength** of 100 keV electron ≈ **3.70 pm**; for 200 keV ≈ **2.51 pm**; always use the **relativistic formula** for V ≥ 50 kV.
- **Abbe resolution**: $d = 0.61\,\lambda/(n\sin\alpha)$; for TEM in vacuum, $n = 1$.
- **TEM resolution ~ 0.1 nm** vs **SEM resolution ~ 1–10 nm** vs **optical ~ 200 nm**.
- **EM lens equation**: $1/f = (e/8m_eV) \int B^2(z)\,dz$ — focal length varies inversely with accelerating voltage.
- **Specimen must be < 100 nm thick**; prepared by ultramicrotomy, ion milling, electropolishing, or grid dispersion.
- **Imaging modes**: BF (direct beam), DF (diffracted beam), SAED (back focal plane).
