- **Electron–specimen interactions**: Elastic (diffraction, BF/DF, SAED) and inelastic (EDS, EELS, plasmon losses).
- **Inventors**: **Ernst Ruska & Max Knoll (1931)**; Ruska → Nobel Prize in Physics 1986.
- **Component order**: Gun → Condenser Lenses → Specimen → Objective Lens → Intermediate Lens → Projector Lenses → Screen/CCD.
- **Vacuum level**: 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ Pa; mandatory to prevent electron-gas scattering.
- **Comparison with SEM**: TEM = internal/transmission, 2D projection, higher resolution; SEM = surface, 3D topography, lower resolution.
- **Bragg's law** $2d\sin\theta = n\lambda$ is used to index SAED patterns and calculate interplanar spacings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** is a high-resolution type of **Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM)** that produces topographic images of surfaces by measuring the interatomic forces (typically van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary, and short-range repulsive forces) between a sharp probe tip (apex radius $\approx$ **2–20 nm**) mounted at the end of a flexible micro-cantilever and the atoms of the sample surface. AFM achieves **lateral resolution of ~1–2 nm** and **vertical resolution of ~0.01 nm**, enabling true atomic-scale imaging in ambient, liquid, and ultra-high vacuum environments.

Invented in **1986** by **Gerd Binnig, Calvin Quate, and Christoph Gerber** at Stanford University as a successor to the Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM), AFM extends nanoscale imaging to **non-conductive samples** — a critical breakthrough because STM requires electrically conducting surfaces.

The governing interaction is described by the **Lennard-Jones Potential**:

$$V(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the depth of the potential well, $\sigma$ is the distance at which the inter-particle potential is zero, and $r$ is the inter-atomic separation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blind Man's Cane" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a blind man (the AFM tip) walking across a surface (the sample) using a sensitive walking cane (the cantilever). As the cane tip moves up and down over bumps and dips, the cane's other end (where the man holds it) bends slightly. The man — or in our case, a **laser beam reflected off the back of the cantilever** — senses these tiny deflections and builds a mental (or computer-generated) "map" of the surface texture.

The key insight is that AFM does **not** "see" the surface optically or via electrons — it **feels** the surface by measuring **forces as small as ~10 pN** (pico-Newtons), which is roughly the weight of a single bacterium multiplied by $10^{-9}$.

## 1.3 Distinguishing AFM from Other Microscopies

| Property | AFM | SEM | TEM | STM |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Resolution | Atomic scale | nm scale | Sub-Å | Atomic |
| Sample type | Any (conductive/insulating) | Conductive (mostly) | Ultra-thin | Conductive only |
| Environment | Air/Liquid/Vacuum | Vacuum | Vacuum | Vacuum/Air |
| Imaging signal | Interatomic force | Secondary electrons | Transmitted electrons | Tunneling current |
| 3D Topography | **Yes (native)** | No (2D projection) | No (2D projection) | Yes |
| Typical tip radius | **2–20 nm** | n/a | n/a | Atomically sharp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AFM is a **non-destructive**, **versatile** technique that can image biological samples (DNA, proteins, cells), polymers, ceramics, metals, and even single molecules in physiological environments — capabilities that SEM and TEM cannot match.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Instrumentation Architecture

The AFM system consists of five interdependent subsystems:

1. **Probe (Tip + Cantilever)** — the sensing element
2. **Piezoelectric Scanner** — provides nanometer-precise XYZ positioning
3. **Optical (or Capacitive) Detection System** — laser + position-sensitive photodiode (PSPD)
4. **Feedback Electronics** — maintains constant operating parameter
5. **Sample Stage / Tip Stage** — holds the specimen

### 2.1.1 The Cantilever — Mechanical Foundation

The cantilever behaves as a **Hookean spring** with deflection governed by:

$$F = -k \cdot z$$

where $k$ is the cantilever spring constant (typically **0.01 – 100 N/m**) and $z$ is the vertical deflection.

The **resonance frequency** of the cantilever is:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m^*}}$$

where $m^*$ is the effective mass of the cantilever.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cantilever is critical:** A stiffer cantilever ($k$ large) resists thermal noise and drift, but it also reduces sensitivity to small force gradients. A softer cantilever (small $k$) is more sensitive but more susceptible to noise. KTU problems often ask for trade-off analysis in cantilever selection.

### 2.1.2 The Tip–Sample Force Regime

The total force experienced by the tip is a sum of contributions:

$$F_{total} = F_{vdW} + F_{capillary} + F_{electrostatic} + F_{chemical} + F_{magnetic}$$

For the dominant van der Waals component, integration of the Lennard-Jones potential over a spherical tip and flat surface yields:

$$F_{vdW}(D) = -\frac{H \cdot R}{6 D^2}$$

where:
- $H$ = **Hamaker constant** (typically $10^{-20}$ to $10^{-19}$ J)
- $R$ = tip radius of curvature
- $D$ = tip–sample separation

## 2.2 Three Primary Operating Modes

### Mode 1: Contact Mode (Repulsive Regime, $D < 0.5$ nm)

- Tip is dragged across the surface in **constant contact**
- Force setpoint: **0.1 – 1 nN** (repulsive)
- High lateral resolution but can damage soft samples
- Two sub-modes:
  - **Constant Height Mode:** Scan at fixed Z; measure cantilever deflection
  - **Constant Force Mode:** Feedback adjusts Z to maintain deflection

### Mode 2: Non-Contact Mode (Attractive Regime, $D \approx 1$ – 10 nm)

- Tip oscillates **5 – 10 nm above** the surface at resonance
- Senses **long-range van der Waals** and **electrostatic** forces
- Force gradient alters resonance frequency (frequency modulation detection)
- Resolution: **lateral ~5 nm**; **vertical < 1 nm**
- **Zero mechanical contact** → ideal for soft, delicate, or biological samples

### Mode 3: Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact)

- Tip oscillates with amplitude **20 – 100 nm** at or near $f_0$
- Periodically "taps" the surface during each oscillation cycle
- Amplitude reduction is the feedback signal
- **Best of both worlds:** high resolution + low sample damage
- The most commonly used mode in modern AFM (and the KTU-favoured mode for biological samples)

## 2.3 Force–Distance Curve

The **F–D curve** is the fundamental diagnostic of AFM:

1. **Approach phase:** Tip approaches; attractive van der Waals pulls tip in (snap-in)
2. **Contact region:** Repulsive Pauli's exclusion dominates
3. **Retract phase:** Adhesion holds tip down (snap-out)
4. **Pull-off force** = adhesion force measurement

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $V(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$ | Lennard-Jones potential | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $F = -k z$ | Hooke's law for cantilever | N (Newtons) |
| 3 | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m^*}}$ | Cantilever resonance frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $F_{vdW}(D) = -\frac{H R}{6 D^2}$ | van der Waals force (sphere–flat geometry) | N |
| 5 | $\Delta f = \frac{f_0}{2k} \cdot \frac{\partial F}{\partial z}$ | Frequency shift due to force gradient | Hz |
| 6 | $Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f_{FWHM}}$ | Quality factor of cantilever | dimensionless |
| 7 | $S_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 k_B T k}{\pi k^2 f_0 Q}}$ | Minimum detectable force | N |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{A_0 - A}{A_0} \times 100\%$ | Tapping mode amplitude damping | % |
| 9 | $R_a = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_i - \bar{z} \vert$ | Average surface roughness | nm |
| 10 | $R_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$ | RMS roughness | nm |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor industry:** Atomic-scale metrology of wafer roughness, line-width measurement at sub-7 nm nodes
- **Pharmaceutical R&D:** Drug morphology, polymorph identification, tablet surface texture
- **Biotechnology:** Imaging DNA, proteins, viruses, cell membranes in physiological buffers
- **Materials Science:** Polymer chain conformation, nanocomposite dispersion, fracture surface analysis
- **Data Storage:** Bit-patterned media inspection, hard-disk surface inspection
- **Solar Cells / Battery Research:** Perovskite film morphology, electrode surface characterization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Point:** When selecting AFM for industrial QC, the **scan speed** is the bottleneck. High-speed AFMs (HS-AFM) developed by Toshio Ando can achieve **12.5 frames per second** for video-rate biomolecular imaging — a technology adopted in modern KTU-aligned industry 4.0 metrology labs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cantilever Spring Constant from Geometry

For a rectangular cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$ made of material with Young's modulus $E$, the spring constant is derived from beam-bending theory.

**Step 1:** The deflection $\delta$ of a rectangular cantilever with an end load $F$ is:

$$\delta = \frac{4 F L^3}{E w t^3}$$

**Step 2:** By Hooke's Law, $F = k \delta$. Substituting:

$$F = k \cdot \frac{4 F L^3}{E w t^3}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $F$ from both sides and solve for $k$:

$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

> This is the standard expression used in AFM software (e.g., Bruker's NanoScope) to calculate the spring constant from the cantilever's geometric and material parameters.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Van der Waals Force Between a Sphere and a Flat Surface

The Lennard-Jones pair potential between two atoms separated by $r$ is:

$$V_{LJ}(r) = 4 \varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

**Step 1:** The attractive part is the $-C_6/r^6$ term. The corresponding pair force is:

$$F_{pair}(r) = -\frac{dV_{LJ}}{dr} = -\frac{6 C_6}{r^7}$$

**Step 2:** The Hamaker constant $H$ relates to $C_6$ and atomic number density $\rho$:

$$H = \pi^2 \rho^2 C_6$$

**Step 3:** Integrate $F_{pair}$ over all atoms in a sphere (tip, radius $R$) interacting with a semi-infinite half-space (sample):

$$F_{vdW}(D) = \int_{tip} \int_{sample} F_{pair} \cdot dN_{tip} \, dN_{sample}$$

**Step 4:** Performing the integration for a sphere of radius $R$ at distance $D \ll R$ above a flat surface gives the final result:

$$F_{vdW}(D) = -\frac{H R}{6 D^2}$$

**Step 5:** The negative sign indicates the force is **attractive** (pulls the tip toward the sample).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Force Gradient → Frequency Shift (Non-Contact Mode)

In non-contact AFM, the cantilever's effective spring constant is modified by the tip–sample force gradient:

$$k_{eff} = k - \frac{\partial F_{ts}}{\partial z}$$

**Step 1:** The modified resonance frequency is:

$$f_{mod} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k_{eff}}{m^*}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k - \partial F_{ts}/\partial z}{m^*}}$$

**Step 2:** Using Taylor expansion for small perturbations $\left( \partial F_{ts}/\partial z \ll k \right)$:

$$f_{mod} = f_0 \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{k} \frac{\partial F_{ts}}{\partial z}} \approx f_0 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2k} \frac{\partial F_{ts}}{\partial z} \right)$$

**Step 3:** The frequency shift is therefore:

$$\Delta f = f_{mod} - f_0 = -\frac{f_0}{2k} \frac{\partial F_{ts}}{\partial z}$$

This $\Delta f$ is the **measurable quantity** in frequency-modulation AFM, and the topographic image is reconstructed by plotting iso-$\Delta f$ contours.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Lennard-Jones Equilibrium Separation

The equilibrium separation $r_0$ occurs where the net force is zero, i.e., where the potential is minimum.

**Step 1:** Differentiate $V_{LJ}$ with respect to $r$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dV_{LJ}}{dr} = 4 \varepsilon \left[ -12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + 6 \frac{\sigma^6}{r^7} \right] = 0$$

**Step 2:** Factor and simplify:

$$4 \varepsilon \cdot \frac{6 \sigma^6}{r^7} \left[ 1 - 2 \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^6 \right] = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $r$:

$$1 = 2 \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^6 \implies r^6 = 2 \sigma^6$$

**Step 4:** Take the sixth root:

$$r_0 = 2^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.122 \sigma$$

**Step 5:** The minimum potential energy is found by substituting $r = r_0$ back into $V_{LJ}$:

$$V_{min} = 4 \varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{2^{1/6} \sigma} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{2^{1/6} \sigma} \right)^6 \right]$$

$$= 4 \varepsilon \left[ \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{2} \right] = 4 \varepsilon \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{4} \right) = -\varepsilon$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The well-known result $r_0 = 2^{1/6} \sigma$ and $V_{min} = -\varepsilon$ are **standard board-exam derivations** and have appeared in KTU Nanotechnology modules repeatedly.

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Force Calculation

**Problem:** An AFM tip with $R = 20$ nm is positioned at $D = 0.5$ nm above a sample. Hamaker constant $H = 2 \times 10^{-19}$ J. Calculate the van der Waals force.

**Solution:**

$$F_{vdW} = -\frac{H R}{6 D^2} = -\frac{(2 \times 10^{-19}) (20 \times 10^{-9})}{6 (0.5 \times 10^{-9})^2}$$

**Step 1:** Numerator:
$$H \cdot R = 2 \times 10^{-19} \times 20 \times 10^{-9} = 4 \times 10^{-27} \, \text{J·m}$$

**Step 2:** Denominator:
$$6 D^2 = 6 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-9})^2 = 6 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-18} = 1.5 \times 10^{-18} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Final division:
$$F_{vdW} = -\frac{4 \times 10^{-27}}{1.5 \times 10^{-18}} = -2.67 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{N} = -2.67 \, \text{nN}$$

This magnitude is squarely in the **contact-mode force range**, consistent with a tip scanning in the repulsive regime.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AFM Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the complete signal flow, from physical tip–sample interaction to reconstructed topographical image.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Sample Surface] -->|Interatomic Forces| T[AFM Tip radius 2 to 20 nm]
    T -->|Mechanical Bending| C[Microcantilever k = 0.01 to 100 N per m]
    C -->|Reflected Beam| L[Laser Source 650 nm typical]
    L --> C
    C -->|Deflected Beam| PD[Position Sensitive Photodiode PSPD]
    PD -->|Voltage Signal| FB[Feedback Controller PI Loop]
    FB -->|Correction Voltage| PZT[Piezoelectric Scanner XYZ]
    PZT -->|Nanopositioning| P
    FB -->|Height Map Data| DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP -->|Topographic Image| MON[Display 2D and 3D Rendering]
    PD -->|Raw Deflection| MON
    FB -.->|Setpoint Comparison| MON
```

## 4.2 Operating Mode Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Sample Analysis] --> B{Sample Type?}
    B -->|Hard Inorganic| C[Contact Mode]
    B -->|Soft Biological| D{Resolution Needed?}
    B -->|Polymer or Organic| E[Tapping Mode]
    D -->|High Lateral Resolution| C
    D -->|True Non Contact| F[Non Contact Mode]
    C --> G[Force Setpoint 0.1 to 1 nN]
    E --> H[Oscillate near resonance f0]
    F --> I[Oscillate above surface 5 to 10 nm]
    G --> J[Generate Topography]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Analyze F-D Curves for Adhesion]
```

## 4.3 Force–Distance Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tip Far from Surface] -->|Approach long range attraction| B[Snap In Contact]
    B -->|Repulsive Paulis regime| C[Stable Contact Region]
    C -->|Retract adhesion holds| D[Snap Out Pull Off]
    D -->|Tip Released| E[Free Cantilever]
    E -.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The "snap-in" (point B) and "snap-out" (point D) on the force–distance curve correspond to local minima of the tip–sample interaction potential. The **pull-off force** at point D, multiplied by the cantilever spring constant, yields the **adhesion force** — a parameter used in pharmaceutical and nanoparticle research.

## 4.4 Lennard-Jones Potential Plot (Schematic Description)

```
V(r)
  |        Repulsive Wall
  |         ↑
ε |_________|_________  ← V = 0
  |        |\
  |        | \________  V_min = -ε
  |        |          \
  |________|________________________
  0        r₀ ≈ 1.122σ              r
         (Equilibrium)
```

- At $r < r_0$: **Strong repulsion** (Pauli exclusion)
- At $r = r_0 = 2^{1/6}\sigma$: **Equilibrium** (minimum energy $V = -\varepsilon$)
- At $r > r_0$: **Attractive** van der Waals (decays as $1/r^6$)

## 4.5 AFM System Block Diagram (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1[Mechanical Subsystem]
        A1[Cantilever] --> A2[Sharp Tip apex radius 2 to 20 nm]
        A1 --> A3[Support Chip]
    end
    subgraph S2[Actuation Subsystem]
        B1[X Piezo] --> B2[Y Piezo]
        B2 --> B3[Z Piezo]
        B3 --> B4[Sample Stage nanopositioning]
    end
    subgraph S3[Detection Subsystem]
        C1[Laser Diode] --> C2[Mirror]
        C2 --> A1
        A1 --> C3[PSPD 4 quadrants]
        C3 --> C4[Differential Amplifier A minus B]
    end
    subgraph S4[Control Subsystem]
        C4 --> D1[Lock in Amplifier]
        D1 --> D2[PID Feedback Controller]
        D2 --> B3
        D2 --> D3[Data Acquisition ADC]
    end
    subgraph S5[Visualization]
        D3 --> E1[Raster Image Generator]
        E1 --> E2[Topography 3D Map]
        E1 --> E3[Phase Image]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reminder:**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (Answer in ~3–4 sentences each)
> - Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (with **internal choice** between Q-A and Q-B)
> - Total per module end-semester contribution follows KTU's 60:40 ESE-ISA split.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Explain the principle of Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and mention its major advantage over Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

AFM works on the principle of measuring **interatomic forces** between a sharp tip (apex radius 2–20 nm) mounted on a flexible micro-cantilever and the atoms of the sample surface. As the tip scans across the sample (raster motion via piezoelectric scanner), force-induced deflections of the cantilever are detected by a **laser–photodiode system** and used to construct a topographical map with sub-nanometer vertical resolution.

**Major advantage over STM:** AFM can image both **conducting and non-conducting** samples (insulators, polymers, biological specimens), whereas STM is **restricted to conductive or semi-conductive materials** because it depends on quantum-mechanical tunneling current. *(2 marks for principle + 1 mark for advantage)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]
**Distinguish between contact mode and tapping mode of AFM operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Contact Mode | Tapping Mode |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Tip–sample gap | Always in physical contact | Intermittent contact during oscillation |
| Force regime | Repulsive (0.1–1 nN) | Mixed, averaged over cycle |
| Sample damage | High (lateral shear) | Negligible (no lateral drag) |
| Resolution | High lateral, low for soft samples | High, suitable for soft samples |
| Cantilever motion | Static with feedback | Oscillates near $f_0$ |
| Ideal samples | Hard, flat surfaces | Polymers, biomolecules, soft matter |

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of an Atomic Force Microscope. List the various forces acting between the tip and the sample. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Construction (3 marks):**
An AFM consists of:
- **Sharp tip** (Si or Si$_3$N$_4$, radius 2–20 nm) microfabricated at the end of a **micro-cantilever** (Si or Si$_3$N$_4$).
- **Piezoelectric scanner** (PZT ceramic tube) that moves the sample (or tip) in X, Y, Z with sub-Ångström precision using applied voltages.
- **Laser diode** (typically 650 nm) focused on the back of the cantilever.
- **Position-Sensitive Photodiode (PSPD)** with four quadrants that detects the reflected laser beam's position.
- **Feedback electronics** (PI or PID loop) that adjusts the Z-piezo to maintain a constant setpoint.
- **Computer** with software for image reconstruction and F–D curve analysis.

**(ii) Working (3 marks):**
1. The sample is raster-scanned beneath the (stationary or moving) tip using the X-Y piezo.
2. Interatomic forces deflect the cantilever vertically.
3. The reflected laser spot moves on the PSPD; the difference signal $(A-B) / (A+B)$ gives a voltage proportional to deflection.
4. The feedback loop adjusts the Z-piezo voltage to keep the deflection (force) constant.
5. The Z-piezo voltage at each (X, Y) point is recorded and rendered as a topographic image.

**(iii) Forces (1 mark):**
- van der Waals (attractive)
- Short-range Pauli repulsion
- Capillary (from water meniscus)
- Electrostatic
- Chemical (covalent/ionic)
- Magnetic (in MFM variant)

---

**Q.A (b)** Derive the expression for the Lennard-Jones potential and hence find the equilibrium separation and minimum potential energy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Complete derivation):**

The Lennard-Jones (LJ) potential describes the interaction between two neutral atoms:

$$V(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

**Step 1 — Origin of the two terms:** The $(1/r^{12})$ term represents the **short-range Pauli repulsion** (overlap of electron clouds); the $(1/r^6)$ term represents the **long-range van der Waals attraction** (induced dipole–induced dipole).

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition:** Equilibrium occurs at $r = r_0$ where $F = -dV/dr = 0$.

$$\frac{dV}{dr} = 4\varepsilon \left[ -12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + 6 \frac{\sigma^6}{r^7} \right] = 0$$

$$\frac{6 \sigma^6}{r^7} = \frac{12 \sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} \implies r^6 = 2 \sigma^6$$

$$\boxed{r_0 = 2^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.122 \, \sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum potential energy:** Substitute $r_0$ back into $V(r)$:

$$V_{min} = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{2^{1/6}\sigma} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{2^{1/6}\sigma} \right)^{6} \right]$$

$$= 4\varepsilon \left[ \frac{1}{(2^{1/6})^{12}} - \frac{1}{(2^{1/6})^{6}} \right] = 4\varepsilon \left[ \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{2} \right]$$

$$\boxed{V_{min} = -\varepsilon}$$

**Physical interpretation:** At separation $r_0 = 1.122\sigma$, the potential energy is at its minimum value of $-\varepsilon$, meaning the system is most stable. Closer than $r_0$, repulsion dominates; farther, attraction dominates.

---

### 📌 Question B — Alternative Choice (KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

**Q.B (a)** Explain the three principal modes of AFM operation — contact, non-contact, and tapping — with force diagrams. Discuss when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Contact Mode (Repulsive regime) (2.5 marks)**
- Tip is in **continuous physical contact** with the surface.
- Operating force: **0.1 – 1 nN** (repulsive Pauli regime).
- Cantilever deflection = feedback signal; Z-piezo adjusts to maintain constant force (constant-force mode) or constant height (constant-height mode).
- **High lateral resolution** (~1 nm) and fast scanning.
- **Drawback:** Lateral shear forces can damage soft samples, drag contaminants, and deform biological structures.
- **Preferred for:** Hard, flat, inorganic surfaces (metals, ceramics, wafer inspection).

**2. Non-Contact Mode (Attractive regime) (2.5 marks)**
- Tip oscillates **5–10 nm above** the surface, never touching it.
- Senses **long-range van der Waals** and electrostatic forces via **frequency-shift** detection.
- Force gradient $\partial F/\partial z$ alters resonance frequency $f_0$; the feedback maintains constant $\Delta f$.
- **Zero mechanical contact** → no sample damage; suitable for ultra-soft samples.
- **Drawback:** Lower lateral resolution (~5 nm); requires ultra-clean, dry conditions because capillary forces from adsorbed water layers can dominate.
- **Preferred for:** Atomically flat, contamination-sensitive surfaces, UHV-STM/AFM combined systems.

**3. Tapping Mode (Intermittent contact) (2 marks)**
- Tip oscillates with amplitude 20–100 nm near $f_0$, periodically "tapping" the surface.
- Amplitude reduction (damping) is the feedback signal.
- **Combines advantages:** high resolution of contact mode + non-damaging nature of non-contact.
- **Phase imaging** capability provides compositional/mechanical contrast.
- **Preferred for:** Polymers, biomolecules (DNA, proteins), thin films, and most routine industrial samples — the **workhorse mode** of modern AFM.

---

**Q.B (b)** An AFM cantilever has length $L = 200 \, \mu m$, width $w = 30 \, \mu m$, thickness $t = 2 \, \mu m$, and is made of silicon with Young's modulus $E = 169 \, GPa$. Calculate (i) the spring constant and (ii) the resonance frequency, given the effective mass $m^* = 5 \times 10^{-11}$ kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Spring Constant (3 marks):**

Using the standard formula for a rectangular cantilever:

$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$k = \frac{(169 \times 10^9) (30 \times 10^{-6}) (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4 (200 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

**Numerator:**
$$E \cdot w \cdot t^3 = 169 \times 10^9 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 10^{-18}$$
$$= 169 \times 30 \times 8 \times 10^{9-6-18} = 40{,}560 \times 10^{-15} = 4.056 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Denominator:**
$$4 L^3 = 4 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 4 \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 32 \times 10^{-15} = 3.2 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Division:**
$$k = \frac{4.056 \times 10^{-11}}{3.2 \times 10^{-14}} = 1267.5 \, \text{N/m}$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 1.27 \, \text{N/m}}$$

**(ii) Resonance Frequency (4 marks):**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m^*}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{1267.5}{5 \times 10^{-11}}}$$

**Compute the ratio:**
$$\frac{k}{m^*} = \frac{1267.5}{5 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.535 \times 10^{13} \, \text{s}^{-2}$$

**Square root:**
$$\sqrt{2.535 \times 10^{13}} = 1.5922 \times 10^{6.5} = 5.035 \times 10^{6}$$

**Final division:**
$$f_0 = \frac{5.035 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi} = \frac{5.035 \times 10^6}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 8.01 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Hz} = 801 \, \text{kHz}}$$

This resonance frequency lies in the typical range (hundreds of kHz to a few MHz) used in tapping-mode AFMs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors in Lennard-Jones derivation:** Students often forget that $V_{min} = -\varepsilon$ (not $+\varepsilon$); the negative sign indicates a bound (stable) state.
> 2. **Unit mismatches in cantilever calculations:** Always convert $\mu m$ to $m$ (factor $10^{-6}$) and $GPa$ to $Pa$ (factor $10^9$) **before** substitution.
> 3. **Confusing contact vs. tapping mode:** Contact mode does **not** mean "the tip touches lightly" — it means the tip is **always in physical contact** under a repulsive force. Tapping is the one that "touches intermittently."
> 4. **Mixing up $f_0$ and $\Delta f$:** $f_0$ is the free resonance frequency; $\Delta f$ is the **shift** caused by the force gradient. These are very different quantities.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A 7-mark question that asks "with a neat diagram" but presents no diagram typically loses **2 marks** even if the written content is perfect.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — AFM**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ AFM is a **Scanning Probe Microscope** (SPM) that measures **interatomic forces** — it does **not** use electrons or photons for imaging.
- ✅ Invented by **Binnig, Quate, and Gerber (1986)** as a non-conductive alternative to STM.
- ✅ Components: **tip + cantilever + piezo scanner + laser + PSPD + feedback**.
- ✅ Cantilever obeys **Hooke's Law** $F = -kz$; spring constant $k = E w t^3 / 4 L^3$.
- ✅ Resonance frequency $f_0 = (1/2\pi) \sqrt{k/m^*}$.

### Forces in AFM
- ✅ **Attractive (long range):** van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic.
- ✅ **Repulsive (short range):** Pauli exclusion, Born repulsion.
- ✅ Lennard-Jones combines both: $V(r) = 4\varepsilon [(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$.
- ✅ van der Waals force for sphere–flat: $F_{vdW} = -HR/(6D^2)$.

### Operating Modes
- ✅ **Contact:** 0.1–1 nN repulsive; high resolution, damages soft samples.
- ✅ **Non-contact:** 5–10 nm above surface; frequency-modulation; no damage, lower resolution.
- ✅ **Tapping (intermittent):** Most commonly used; combines advantages of both; phase imaging gives material contrast.

### Key Results to Memorize
- ✅ $r_0 = 2^{1/6} \sigma$ (LJ equilibrium)
- ✅ $V_{min} = -\varepsilon$ (LJ well depth)
- ✅ $\Delta f = -(f_0 / 2k) \cdot (\partial F / \partial z)$ (non-contact detection)

### Typical Numerical Values
- ✅ Tip radius: **2 – 20 nm**
- ✅ Spring constant: **0.01 – 100 N/m**
- ✅ Hamaker constant: **$10^{-20}$ to $10^{-19}$ J**
- ✅ Force range: **pN to nN**
- ✅ Lateral resolution: **1 – 2 nm**; Vertical: **~0.01 nm**

### Application Domains
- ✅ Semiconductor metrology • Biological imaging (DNA, proteins) • Polymer science • Pharmaceutical tablet analysis • Solar cell / battery research • Data storage inspection.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "**explain AFM with a neat diagram**," always start with the **tip + cantilever**, then proceed sequentially to **laser → photodiode → feedback → piezo → computer**. Examiners award marks for **logical flow**, not just keyword density.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) - Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM), UV-Visible Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) - Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM), UV-Visible Spectroscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) — The Governing Concept

**Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM)** is a branch of surface-analysis instrumentation that uses an atomically sharp physical probe rastered across a sample surface to map topographical, electronic, magnetic, or chemical features at the nanometer and sub-nanometer scale. Unlike optical microscopes, SPM does not rely on photons; instead, it exploits a *local physical interaction* (tunneling current, atomic force, near-field optical coupling, etc.) between the tip apex and the specimen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus):** *SPM refers to a class of microscopy techniques that image surfaces by mechanically scanning a physical probe across the sample and recording the tip–sample interaction as a function of spatial position.*

The two principal families encountered in Module 3 are:
1. **Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)** — based on quantum-mechanical electron tunneling.
2. **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** — based on interatomic force gradients (treated as a comparative reference here).

---

### 1.2 Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** STM is a scanning probe technique that uses a bias-voltage-biased, atomically sharp conducting tip brought within ~0.3–1 nm of a conducting (or semiconducting) sample so that a measurable **quantum-mechanical tunneling current** flows across the vacuum gap. The magnitude of this current is exponentially sensitive to the tip–sample distance, enabling true atomic-resolution imaging of surface electron density.

**Working Principle (one-line essence):** *Position the tip close enough that electrons can "tunnel" through the vacuum barrier; record the current (or height) as the tip scans.*

#### Intuitive Analogy — "The Whisper in a Noisy Hall"

Imagine standing in a vast, silent hall (the vacuum gap) and whispering to a friend on the other side. Sound cannot travel across the hall, but if both of you press your ears against the wall, you might *just* hear each other — and the loudness drops dramatically as you step back even a few centimeters. STM's tunneling current behaves the same way: a tiny increase in the tip–sample gap (1 Å) can drop the current by an order of magnitude. This extreme distance sensitivity is what gives STM its *atomic* resolution.

**Key Performance Metrics (KTU board-favorite constants):**
- Typical tunneling current: **1 pA – 10 nA**
- Typical bias voltage: **0.01 V – 4 V** (sample bias)
- Lateral resolution: **~0.1 nm** (atomic)
- Vertical resolution: **~0.01 nm** (sub-picometer)
- Tip material: **Tungsten (W)** or **Pt-Ir** alloy
- Work function of typical tip: **~4.5 eV**

> [!WARNING]
> **Sample Restriction:** STM *requires* a conducting or semiconducting sample because the technique relies on the flow of electrons through the tip. Insulating samples cannot be imaged by STM; AFM is used instead.

---

### 1.3 Modes of Operation

| Mode | Controlled Variable | Recorded Variable | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Current Mode** | Tip vertical position (Z) | Tunneling current held constant via feedback loop | Most common, true topography |
| **Constant Height Mode** | Tip Z held fixed | Tunneling current measured | Fast, atomically flat surfaces only |

---

### 1.4 UV-Visible (UV-Vis) Spectroscopy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** UV-Vis spectroscopy is an optical absorption technique in which a sample is illuminated with electromagnetic radiation spanning the ultraviolet (200–400 nm) and visible (400–800 nm) regions, and the attenuation of transmitted (or reflected) light is measured as a function of wavelength to deduce the electronic structure, optical band gap, and concentration of chromophores in the material.

**Working Principle (one-line essence):** *Shine UV-Vis light through the sample; the material absorbs photons whose energy matches its electronic transitions; the absorption pattern fingerprints the material's electronic structure.*

#### Intuitive Analogy — "The Sunglass Test"

Hold a pair of sunglasses under a white flashlight. The light that emerges is dimmer, and its color has shifted because the glass (or coating) absorbed specific wavelengths. UV-Vis spectroscopy does the same thing scientifically: a *spectrometer* measures exactly which wavelengths the sample ate up, producing a graph of absorbance vs. wavelength — the **absorption spectrum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is UV-Vis critical in Nanotechnology?** When a material's particle size shrinks to the nanoscale (especially below the **Bohr exciton radius**), quantum confinement widens its effective band gap, blue-shifting the absorption onset. UV-Vis is therefore the *cheapest, fastest experimental proof* that you actually synthesized nanoparticles rather than bulk material.

**Key Performance Metrics:**
- Spectral range: **200–800 nm** (photon energy ≈ **1.55 – 6.20 eV**)
- Beer-Lambert linear range: typically **A = 0.1 – 1.0**
- Molar absorptivity (ε) of strong chromophores: **10³ – 10⁵ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**
- Typical solvents: water, ethanol, hexane, toluene (must be transparent in the scanned range)
- Sample form: **solution, thin film, or suspension** in a **1 cm path-length quartz cuvette**

---

### 1.5 Connecting STM and UV-Vis — The Module-3 Narrative

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Takeaway:** STM gives you the *physical* map of a nanostructure (size, shape, lattice), while UV-Vis gives you the *electronic* map (band gap, confinement, defect states). Together, they cross-validate that a synthesized nanomaterial is both *small* and *quantum-confined*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Quantum Mechanical Foundation of STM

#### 2.1.1 The Tunneling Barrier

When two conductors are separated by a thin insulating layer (here: a vacuum gap of width *d*), classical electrodynamics forbids electron flow because the vacuum energy level exceeds the electron's energy. Quantum mechanics, however, permits a finite probability for the electron's wavefunction to penetrate — and traverse — the barrier. This is **quantum tunneling**.

The **tunneling probability** through a rectangular potential barrier of height $\phi$ (the average work function) and width *d* is:

$$T \;\approx\; \exp\!\left(-2\kappa d\right)$$

where the **decay constant** $\kappa$ is given by:

$$\kappa \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2m_e(\phi - E)}}{\hbar} \;\approx\; \frac{\sqrt{2m_e\phi}}{\hbar}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical metal work function $\phi \approx 4.5 \,\text{eV}$, this gives $\kappa \approx 1.1 \,\text{Å}^{-1}$. Because the exponential is so steep, a 1 Å increase in *d* reduces the tunneling current by roughly an order of magnitude — the physical reason for STM's atomic resolution.

#### 2.1.2 The Tunneling Current

Applying a small bias voltage *V* between tip and sample allows electrons to tunnel preferentially in one direction. The resulting **tunneling current** at low bias and low temperature is:

$$I \;=\; \frac{4\pi e}{\hbar}\!\int_{0}^{eV}\!\rho_{\text{tip}}(E)\,\rho_{\text{sample}}(E - eV)\,\vert M \vert^{2}\, dE$$

where:
- $\rho_{\text{tip}}$ and $\rho_{\text{sample}}$ are the local densities of states (LDOS) of tip and sample
- $M$ is the tunneling matrix element
- $eV$ is the applied bias energy window

For a small bias and approximately constant LDOS (the *Tersoff-Hamann approximation*), the current simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\,I \;\propto\; V \cdot \rho_{\text{sample}}(E_F) \cdot \exp\!\left(-2\kappa d\right)\,}$$

This expression contains the two pillars of STM imaging:
1. **Topographic contrast** — via the exponential $e^{-2\kappa d}$ term.
2. **Electronic / spectroscopic contrast** — via the LDOS term $\rho_{\text{sample}}(E_F)$, which enables **Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy (STS)**.

#### 2.1.3 Piezoelectric Scanner

The tip is mounted on a **piezoelectric tube scanner** (typically PZT — lead zirconate titanate). Applying a voltage $V_p$ across the piezo produces a mechanical displacement:

$$\Delta x \;=\; d_{31}\,\frac{V_p \cdot L}{t}$$

where $d_{31}$ is the piezo strain coefficient, *L* is the length, and *t* is the wall thickness. This sub-Ångstrom positioning capability is what allows STM to literally *place* the tip with atomic precision.

---

### 2.2 UV-Vis Spectroscopy — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 The Beer-Lambert Law

The fundamental law governing quantitative UV-Vis absorption is the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$A \;=\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) \;=\; \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell$$

where:
- $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless)
- $I_0$ = intensity of incident light
- $I$ = intensity of transmitted light
- $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
- $c$ = concentration (mol·L⁻¹)
- $\ell$ = optical path length (cm)

The **transmittance** is $T = I/I_0 = 10^{-A}$, and the **percentage transmittance** is $T\% = 100 \cdot 10^{-A}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Beer-Lambert is the workhorse for determining the *concentration* of nanoparticles in colloidal suspensions, monitoring *reaction kinetics* of nanoparticle growth, and verifying *chromophore stability* in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs).

#### 2.2.2 The Tauc Plot — Extracting the Optical Band Gap

For semiconductor nanomaterials, the **Tauc relation** expresses the absorption coefficient $\alpha$ as a function of photon energy $h\nu$:

$$\alpha h\nu \;=\; A \left(h\nu - E_g\right)^{n}$$

where $n$ depends on the nature of the electronic transition:
- $n = 1/2$ → **direct allowed** transition (e.g., ZnO, TiO₂, CdSe)
- $n = 2$ → **indirect allowed** transition (e.g., Si, Ge)
- $n = 3/2$ → direct forbidden
- $n = 3$ → indirect forbidden

The optical band gap $E_g$ is obtained by extrapolating the linear portion of the $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n}$ vs. $h\nu$ plot to $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n} = 0$.

The **absorption coefficient** itself is derived from absorbance via:

$$\alpha \;=\; \frac{2.303 \cdot A}{\ell}$$

#### 2.2.3 Quantum Confinement in Nanoparticles

When a nanoparticle's radius *R* is comparable to or smaller than the **Bohr exciton radius** $a_B$, the electron–hole pair is spatially confined. Using the **particle-in-a-box (effective mass approximation)** model:

$$E_g^{\text{nano}} \;\approx\; E_g^{\text{bulk}} \;+\; \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu R^2} \;-\; \frac{1.8 e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r R}$$

where $\mu = (m_e^* m_h^*)/(m_e^* + m_h^*)$ is the reduced effective mass and the second term is a (smaller) Coulomb correction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** This blue-shift of $E_g$ with decreasing *R* is the *direct optical fingerprint* of quantum confinement, and UV-Vis is the standard technique to confirm that a synthesis has produced true nanoparticles (typically, bulk ZnO has $E_g \approx 3.2$ eV; ZnO quantum dots can shift this to 3.4–4.0 eV).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I \propto V \cdot \rho_s(E_F) \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$ | STM tunneling current | A (amperes) |
| 2 | $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e\phi}/\hbar$ | Tunneling decay constant (vacuum) | m⁻¹ |
| 3 | $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I) = \varepsilon c \ell$ | Beer-Lambert absorbance law | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T = 10^{-A}$ | Transmittance from absorbance | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\alpha = 2.303\,A/\ell$ | Absorption coefficient from absorbance | cm⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^{n}$ | Tauc relation for band gap | varies with *n* |
| 7 | $E_g^{\text{nano}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \hbar^2\pi^2/(2\mu R^2)$ | Quantum confinement blue-shift | eV |
| 8 | $E = hc/\lambda = 1240/\lambda(\text{nm})$ | Photon energy | eV |
| 9 | $\Delta x = d_{31} V_p L / t$ | Piezo displacement of STM scanner | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical constants for KTU board calculations:**
> - Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - Reduced Planck's constant $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - Electron rest mass $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
> - Elementary charge $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - $hc = 1240$ eV·nm (memorize this — it appears in every spectroscopy problem)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tunneling Current Expression (Tersoff-Hamann)

We start with the **Bardeen tunneling current formula**, which describes the rate of electron transfer between two electrodes separated by a barrier:

$$I \;=\; \frac{4\pi e}{\hbar}\!\int_{0}^{eV}\!\rho_{\text{tip}}(E)\,\rho_{\text{sample}}(E - eV)\,\vert M \vert^{2}\, dE \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the small-bias, constant-LDOS (Tersoff-Hamann) approximation.**

For small applied bias $V$ such that $eV \ll \phi$ (the work function), and assuming the tip LDOS is approximately constant over the energy window of integration, the integral simplifies to:

$$I \;\approx\; \frac{4\pi e^2 V}{\hbar}\,\rho_{\text{tip}}(E_F)\,\rho_{\text{sample}}(E_F)\,\vert M \vert^{2} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the tunneling matrix element.**

Bardeen showed that for a planar barrier geometry and an s-wave tip, the matrix element magnitude can be approximated as:

$$\vert M \vert^{2} \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(-2\kappa d\right) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

where $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e\phi}/\hbar$ and *d* is the tip–sample separation.

**Step 3 — Combine (2) and (3).**

Substituting (3) into (2) and absorbing all bias-independent prefactors into a single constant $C$:

$$\boxed{\,I \;\approx\; C \cdot V \cdot \rho_{\text{sample}}(E_F) \cdot \exp\!\left(-2\kappa d\right)\,} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

This is the canonical STM current expression. The three factors carry the entire physics:
- $V$ → controls the energy window of tunneling electrons
- $\rho_{\text{sample}}(E_F)$ → samples the local electronic structure (enables STS)
- $e^{-2\kappa d}$ → provides the topographic contrast (the "zoom lens" of STM)

**Step 4 — Quantitative estimate of distance sensitivity.**

For $\phi = 4.5$ eV:

$$\kappa \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 4.5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$2\kappa \;\approx\; 2.2 \;\text{Å}^{-1} \;\Longrightarrow\; I \propto e^{-2.2\,d(\text{Å})}$$

A change of $\Delta d = 1$ Å therefore alters the current by a factor of $e^{-2.2} \approx 0.11$, i.e., an order-of-magnitude change. This is why STM can resolve sub-Ångstrom height features.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Tauc Band-Gap Relation

We start with the definition of the **absorption coefficient** in the vicinity of an absorption edge for a direct allowed transition. Fermi's Golden Rule gives the transition rate as:

$$W_{i \to f} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\hbar}\,\vert\langle f \mid H' \mid i\rangle\vert^{2}\,\rho(E) \quad \text{...(5)}$$

where $\rho(E)$ is the joint density of states (JDOS) and $H'$ is the optical perturbation.

**Step 1 — Joint Density of States near the band edge.**

For parabolic bands, the JDOS scales as:

$$\rho_{\text{joint}}(E) \;\propto\; \sqrt{E - E_g} \quad \text{(3D bulk)} \quad \text{...(6)}$$

**Step 2 — Connect to absorption coefficient.**

The absorption coefficient is proportional to the JDOS (under the assumption that the optical matrix element is slowly varying near the edge):

$$\alpha \;\propto\; \sqrt{h\nu - E_g} \quad \text{...(7)}$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides and rearrange.**

$$\alpha^2 \;\propto\; h\nu - E_g \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (\alpha h\nu)^2 \;\propto\; (h\nu - E_g) \quad \text{...(8)}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to all transition types.**

Allowing the exponent *n* to depend on the dimensionality and transition type:

$$\boxed{\,(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n} \;=\; A^{1/n}\,(h\nu - E_g)\,} \quad \text{...(9)}$$

This is the **Tauc relation**. The plot of $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n}$ on the y-axis vs. $h\nu$ on the x-axis gives a straight line; extrapolating the linear region to the x-axis intercept yields $E_g$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Finding the Optical Band Gap of a ZnO Nanoparticle

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A UV-Vis absorption spectrum of ZnO nanoparticles dispersed in ethanol (cuvette path length 1 cm) yields the following data points near the absorption edge:

| Wavelength (nm) | Absorbance (A) |
|---|---|
| 360 | 0.45 |
| 370 | 0.30 |
| 380 | 0.18 |
| 390 | 0.10 |
| 400 | 0.05 |
| 410 | 0.025 |

Assuming a direct allowed transition ($n = 1/2$), estimate the optical band gap $E_g$.

**Step 1 — Convert wavelength to photon energy.**

Use $E = 1240/\lambda(\text{nm})$ eV:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda = 360\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; h\nu = 1240/360 = 3.444\,\text{eV} \\
\lambda = 370\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; h\nu = 1240/370 = 3.351\,\text{eV} \\
\lambda = 380\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; h\nu = 1240/380 = 3.263\,\text{eV} \\
\lambda = 390\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; h\nu = 1240/390 = 3.179\,\text{eV} \\
\lambda = 400\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; h\nu = 1240/400 = 3.100\,\text{eV} \\
\lambda = 410\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; h\nu = 1240/410 = 3.024\,\text{eV} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absorption coefficient.**

Using $\alpha = 2.303\,A/\ell$ with $\ell = 1$ cm:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda = 360\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 2.303 \times 0.45 = 1.036\,\text{cm}^{-1} \\
\lambda = 370\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 2.303 \times 0.30 = 0.691\,\text{cm}^{-1} \\
\lambda = 380\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 2.303 \times 0.18 = 0.415\,\text{cm}^{-1} \\
\lambda = 390\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 2.303 \times 0.10 = 0.230\,\text{cm}^{-1} \\
\lambda = 400\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 2.303 \times 0.05 = 0.115\,\text{cm}^{-1} \\
\lambda = 410\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 2.303 \times 0.025 = 0.0576\,\text{cm}^{-1} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ for the Tauc plot (direct allowed → n = 1/2).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda = 360\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; (\alpha h\nu)^2 = (1.036 \times 3.444)^2 = 12.74 \\
\lambda = 370\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; (\alpha h\nu)^2 = (0.691 \times 3.351)^2 = 5.36 \\
\lambda = 380\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; (\alpha h\nu)^2 = (0.415 \times 3.263)^2 = 1.83 \\
\lambda = 390\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; (\alpha h\nu)^2 = (0.230 \times 3.179)^2 = 0.535 \\
\lambda = 400\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; (\alpha h\nu)^2 = (0.115 \times 3.100)^2 = 0.127 \\
\lambda = 410\,\text{nm} &\;\Rightarrow\; (\alpha h\nu)^2 = (0.0576 \times 3.024)^2 = 0.0303 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Linear extrapolation to find $E_g$.**

Plotting $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ vs. $h\nu$ and fitting the steepest linear region (between $h\nu = 3.10$ and $3.35$ eV) gives a line of the form:

$$(\alpha h\nu)^2 = m\,(h\nu - E_g)$$

Fitting the last three points (3.100, 0.127), (3.179, 0.535), (3.263, 1.83):

$$\text{slope } m \;\approx\; \frac{1.83 - 0.127}{3.263 - 3.100} = \frac{1.703}{0.163} \approx 10.45$$

The x-intercept:

$$E_g \;=\; h\nu - \frac{(\alpha h\nu)^2}{m} \;=\; 3.100 - \frac{0.127}{10.45} \;\approx\; 3.088\,\text{eV}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with bulk ZnO and conclude.**

Bulk ZnO has $E_g^{\text{bulk}} \approx 3.20$ eV. The observed $E_g \approx 3.09$ eV appears *red-shifted*, which would normally indicate either larger particles or surface defect states. *Note for the student:* If the calculation had yielded an $E_g > 3.20$ eV, that would be the signature blue-shift of quantum confinement. The methodology — Tauc plot extrapolation — is the same in both cases.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of STM vs. UV-Vis

| Parameter | STM | UV-Vis Spectroscopy |
|---|---|---|
| Physical principle | Quantum tunneling current | Photon absorption by electronic transitions |
| Probe | Atomically sharp metal tip | UV-Vis electromagnetic radiation |
| Sample requirement | Conducting or semiconducting | Any (solid, liquid, gas) with chromophores |
| Spatial resolution | ~0.1 nm (atomic) | None (ensemble average over cuvette) |
| Information provided | Topography + LDOS (electronic) | Concentration + band gap + transition types |
| Typical imaging time | Minutes per scan | Seconds per spectrum |
| Vacuum requirement | UHV often needed (not always) | Ambient, no vacuum |
| Cost | High (~₹50 Lakh+) | Low (~₹2–5 Lakh) |
| Quantitative output | Current, height (nm) | Absorbance, ε, c, E_g |
| Destructive? | No (non-contact) | No (transmission) |

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern, Dec 2024 / May 2025]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] What is meant by the term "tunneling" in the context of STM? Why is STM restricted to conducting samples?**

**Model Answer:**
Tunneling refers to the quantum-mechanical phenomenon in which an electron has a finite probability of crossing a potential barrier (such as a vacuum gap) that it classically cannot surmount. In STM, a bias voltage applied between a sharp metal tip and the sample causes electrons to tunnel through the narrow vacuum gap (~0.3–1 nm) between them. The resulting tunneling current is exponentially sensitive to the gap distance, which is the basis for atomic-scale imaging.

STM is restricted to conducting or semiconducting samples because the technique fundamentally requires a continuous supply of mobile electrons at the sample surface to maintain the tunneling current. Insulating samples cannot sustain this current, and the technique fails. AFM is the appropriate alternative for non-conducting samples.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's key markers (worth 1 mark each):** (a) Definition of quantum tunneling, (b) mention of vacuum gap, (c) justification for sample-conductivity requirement.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] State the Beer-Lambert law and explain the significance of the molar absorptivity (ε) in nanoparticle characterization.**

**Model Answer:**
The Beer-Lambert law is expressed as:

$$A \;=\; \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$$

where $A$ is the absorbance, $\varepsilon$ is the molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), $c$ is the molar concentration of the absorbing species, and $\ell$ is the optical path length (cm).

The molar absorptivity $\varepsilon$ is a wavelength-dependent characteristic of a given chromophore and indicates *how strongly* the species absorbs light at a particular wavelength. In nanoparticle characterization, a high $\varepsilon$ value at the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) wavelength is a signature of metallic nanoparticles (e.g., Au, Ag). The position of the SPR peak also reports on particle size, shape, and aggregation state, making $\varepsilon$ a key figure of merit for plasmonic nanomaterials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's key markers:** (a) Statement of the law with units, (b) definition of $\varepsilon$, (c) one application to nanoparticles (e.g., SPR detection).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Format (As per KTU 2024 ESE Regulations):** Answer ANY ONE from Q-A or Q-B.

---

#### **Q-A. (14 Marks) — Scanning Tunneling Microscopy**

**(a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Scanning Tunneling Microscope. Describe constant-current and constant-height modes of operation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The major components of an STM are:
1. **Atomically sharp tip** — typically etched tungsten (W) or Pt-Ir wire, with apex radius of curvature ~10–50 nm. The tip is often treated *in situ* to remove oxide contamination.
2. **Piezoelectric scanner** — a PZT (lead zirconate titanate) tube that expands/contracts in X, Y, and Z directions in response to applied voltages, enabling sub-Ångstrom positioning.
3. **Coarse positioning mechanism** — a stepper motor or "louse" / "Pan walker" inchworm that brings the tip to within tunneling range of the sample.
4. **Vibration isolation** — typically a stack of metal plates separated by Viton spacers, often combined with an eddy-current damper, to eliminate mechanical noise.
5. **Control electronics** — bias voltage source, transimpedance amplifier (converts pA currents to measurable voltages), and a feedback loop.
6. **Sample stage** — a conducting sample mounted on a holder, electrically grounded.

**Working (2 marks):**

A small bias voltage (typically 0.01–4 V) is applied between the tip and the conducting sample. When the tip is brought within ~0.3–1 nm of the surface, electrons tunnel quantum-mechanically through the vacuum gap, producing a measurable tunneling current (1 pA – 10 nA). The magnitude of this current is exponentially sensitive to the gap distance *d*:

$$I \;\propto\; V \cdot \exp(-2\kappa d)$$

The tip is then rastered across the surface (line by line in X–Y) while the current is recorded, building up a 2-D image of the surface.

**Modes of operation (2 marks):**

- **Constant-current mode (most common):** A feedback loop continuously adjusts the tip's Z-height to keep the tunneling current fixed. The Z-voltage applied to the piezo at each (X, Y) point is plotted, producing a *true topographic image*. Safe for rough surfaces but slower (~minutes per image).
- **Constant-height mode:** The tip Z is held fixed and the tunneling current is measured directly. Faster (atomic-resolution videos possible) but can crash the tip into tall features — used only on atomically flat surfaces.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the tunneling current in STM starting from Bardeen's tunneling formalism. Highlight the role of the local density of states (LDOS).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bardeen's starting formula (2 marks):**

Bardeen (1961) derived the tunneling current between two electrodes separated by a thin insulating barrier as:

$$I \;=\; \frac{4\pi e}{\hbar}\!\int_{0}^{eV}\!\rho_{\text{tip}}(E)\,\rho_{\text{sample}}(E - eV)\,\vert M \vert^{2}\, dE$$

where $\rho_{\text{tip}}$ and $\rho_{\text{sample}}$ are the densities of states on either side of the barrier, and $M$ is the tunneling matrix element.

**Step 2 — Tersoff-Hamann approximation (2 marks):**

Tersoff and Hamann (1983, 1985) showed that, for an s-wave (spherically symmetric) tip apex, the matrix element can be evaluated in the limit of small bias, yielding:

$$\vert M \vert^{2} \;\propto\; \rho_{\text{tip}}(E_F)\,\exp(-2\kappa d)$$

Substituting this and assuming the tip LDOS is constant:

$$I \;\approx\; C \cdot V \cdot \rho_{\text{sample}}(E_F) \cdot \exp(-2\kappa d)$$

where $C$ is a bias-independent constant and $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e\phi}/\hbar$.

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation (2 marks):**

The expression contains two distinct physical contributions:
- The exponential term $e^{-2\kappa d}$ provides *topographic* sensitivity — a 1 Å increase in tip–sample distance reduces the current by an order of magnitude (for $\phi = 4.5$ eV, $2\kappa \approx 2.2$ Å⁻¹).
- The factor $\rho_{\text{sample}}(E_F)$ — the **local density of states** of the sample at the Fermi level — means the tunneling current is *not purely topographic*. STM also maps the spatial variation in electronic structure, which is why STM images often show subtle electronic effects (charge density waves, surface states, etc.) superimposed on topography.

**Step 4 — Link to Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy (1 mark):**

By ramping the bias voltage $V$ and recording $dI/dV$, one obtains a direct measure of $\rho_{\text{sample}}(E)$ at the position of the tip — this is the principle of **STS**, which gives energy-resolved electronic structure at the nanoscale.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student errors in this derivation:**
> 1. Confusing the *tunneling matrix element* with the LDOS — the matrix element describes the *coupling* between tip and sample states, while the LDOS describes the *availability* of those states.
> 2. Forgetting the factor of 2 in $2\kappa d$ — the wavefunction decays as $e^{-\kappa x}$ on entering the barrier and continues to decay as $e^{-\kappa(d - x)}$ on the other side, giving a total $\propto e^{-\kappa d}$ transmission probability and $e^{-2\kappa d}$ current.
> 3. Stating the LDOS dependence without noting that it makes STM **electronic-structure-sensitive**, not just topographic.

---

#### **Q-B. (14 Marks) — UV-Visible Spectroscopy (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the instrumentation of a double-beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Discuss the role of each component.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above) and component-wise description (7 marks):**

1. **Light sources (1.5 marks):**
   - **Deuterium (D₂) lamp** — emits continuous UV radiation in the 200–400 nm range via electrical discharge in deuterium gas.
   - **Tungsten-halogen lamp** — emits continuous visible / near-IR radiation in the 350–800 nm range from a heated tungsten filament in a halogen atmosphere.
   - The two lamps are combined by a beam combiner / mirror to produce a single beam covering 200–800 nm.

2. **Monochromator (1.5 marks):**
   - A grating (or prism) disperses the polychromatic light into its constituent wavelengths. The entrance slit defines the source size; the exit slit selects a narrow band of wavelengths (typically 0.5–2 nm bandwidth) to pass through to the sample.
   - The grating is rotated to scan across wavelengths.

3. **Beam chopper and sample compartment (1.5 marks):**
   - In a *double-beam* instrument, a rotating chopper (mirror) alternately directs the monochromatic beam through (i) the reference cuvette (containing pure solvent) and (ii) the sample cuvette (containing the analyte in the same solvent).
   - Both cuvettes are typically 1 cm path-length fused-quartz (transparent throughout the UV-Vis range).
   - The chopper frequency (typically 50–100 Hz) allows the detector to measure $I$ and $I_0$ quasi-simultaneously, automatically compensating for source intensity drift and solvent absorption.

4. **Detector and readout (1.5 marks):**
   - **Photomultiplier tube (PMT)** or **photodiode array** — converts transmitted photons into an electrical signal.
   - The signal is digitized by an ADC, processed by a microcontroller, and the absorbance $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ is computed in real time.
   - The output is plotted as **absorbance vs. wavelength** on a computer display.

5. **Why double-beam? (1 mark):**
   - Compensates for solvent absorption, lamp fluctuations, and electronic drift, giving a much more stable baseline than a single-beam instrument — critical for quantitative Beer-Lambert measurements.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the Tauc plot method for the determination of the optical band gap of a semiconductor nanomaterial. How does the Tauc plot confirm quantum confinement?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Optical band gap concept (1 mark):**

The optical band gap $E_g$ of a semiconductor is the minimum photon energy required to excite an electron from the valence band maximum to the conduction band minimum. For direct transitions, this is the band gap itself; for indirect transitions, momentum-conserving phonons are also involved.

**Step 2 — Tauc relation (2 marks):**

The Tauc relation, derived from Fermi's Golden Rule with the joint density of states, gives the absorption coefficient $\alpha$ near the absorption edge:

$$\alpha h\nu \;=\; A\,(h\nu - E_g)^{n}$$

where $n$ depends on the transition type: $n = 1/2$ for direct allowed, $n = 2$ for indirect allowed.

**Step 3 — Experimental procedure (2 marks):**

1. Record the UV-Vis absorbance spectrum of the sample.
2. Convert absorbance to absorption coefficient: $\alpha = 2.303 A/\ell$.
3. Compute $h\nu = 1240/\lambda(\text{nm})$ for each wavelength.
4. Plot $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n}$ vs. $h\nu$.
   - For direct allowed: plot $(\alpha h\nu)^2$ vs. $h\nu$.
   - For indirect allowed: plot $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$ vs. $h\nu$.
5. Identify the linear (steepest) region of the plot near the absorption edge.
6. Extrapolate this linear segment to $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n} = 0$. The x-intercept gives $E_g$.

**Step 4 — Quantum confinement signature (2 marks):**

Bulk semiconductors have a fixed band gap. As the particle radius *R* shrinks below the Bohr exciton radius, quantum confinement raises the effective band gap according to the effective mass approximation:

$$E_g^{\text{nano}} \;=\; E_g^{\text{bulk}} \;+\; \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu R^2} \;-\; \text{(Coulomb correction)}$$

A Tauc plot that yields a *larger* $E_g$ than the bulk value therefore *directly confirms* quantum confinement. Furthermore, the magnitude of the blue-shift $(\Delta E_g)$ can be used to estimate the particle size *R*, providing an independent cross-check on TEM/STM measurements.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student errors in Tauc plot problems:**
> 1. Using the wrong exponent *n* — for ZnO, TiO₂, CdSe, CdS (direct band gap), use $n = 1/2$ → plot $(\alpha h\nu)^2$. For Si, Ge, use $n = 2$ → plot $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/2}$. Confusing these gives a meaningless straight line.
> 2. Forgetting the $h\nu$ factor on the left side — the y-axis must be $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n}$, *not* just $\alpha^{1/n}$.
> 3. Extrapolating the *wrong* linear region — choose the region with the steepest, most linear slope adjacent to the absorption edge, not the low-energy tail (scattering) or the saturating high-energy region.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students consistently lose marks on this module:**
> 1. **Mixing up "topography" and "current" images** in STM — topography comes from the Z-feedback signal in constant-current mode, NOT from the raw current.
> 2. **Omitting the factor of 2 in the tunneling decay** — examiners explicitly look for $e^{-2\kappa d}$, not $e^{-\kappa d}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\rho_{\text{sample}}(E_F)$ term** in the STM current expression — this is the basis of STS and is a favourite 2-mark follow-up.
> 4. **Beer-Lambert violations** — students often forget that Beer-Lambert is linear only at low concentrations (A < 1); at high concentrations, deviations occur due to scattering and solute–solute interactions.
> 5. **Confusing optical band gap with electrical band gap** — the Tauc plot gives the *optical* $E_g$, which is often slightly different from the electrical $E_g$ measured by transport methods.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert wavelength to photon energy** when plotting Tauc graphs — examiners routinely set traps here.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **STM principle:** Quantum tunneling of electrons across a vacuum gap; current $\propto V \cdot \rho_s(E_F) \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$.
- [x] **Tunneling decay constant:** $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e\phi}/\hbar \approx 1.1$ Å⁻¹ for $\phi \approx 4.5$ eV.
- [x] **STM requirement:** Sample must be conducting or semiconducting; tip is sharp W or Pt-Ir; piezo scanner gives sub-Å positioning.
- [x] **Two STM modes:** Constant current (feedback adjusts Z) vs. constant height (Z fixed, current mapped).
- [x] **STS extension:** $dI/dV$ vs. $V$ gives LDOS at the tip position — electronic-structure mapping.
- [x] **UV-Vis principle:** Photon absorption by electronic transitions; A = log(I₀/I).
- [x] **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon c \ell$ — linear up to A ≈ 1; deviates beyond.
- [x] **Photon energy shortcut:** $h\nu(\text{eV}) = 1240/\lambda(\text{nm})$.
- [x] **Tauc relation:** $\alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^n$; $n = 1/2$ direct, $n = 2$ indirect.
- [x] **Tauc plot procedure:** Plot $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n}$ vs. $h\nu$; extrapolate linear region to x-axis.
- [x] **Quantum confinement blue-shift:** $E_g^{\text{nano}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \hbar^2\pi^2/(2\mu R^2)$; confirms nanoparticle formation.
- [x] **STM gives STRUCTURE, UV-Vis gives ELECTRONICS** — together they fully characterize a nanomaterial.
- [x] **Instrumental caution:** STM needs UHV and vibration isolation; UV-Vis is cheap, fast, and works on solutions.
- [x] **Path length:** Standard UV-Vis cuvette is 1 cm; $\alpha = 2.303 A / \ell$ converts absorbance to absorption coefficient.
- [x] **SPR connection:** Metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag) show a strong surface plasmon resonance in UV-Vis — its position and intensity report on size, shape, and aggregation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raman Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Raman Spectroscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raman Spectroscopy** is a *vibrational spectroscopic technique* based on the **inelastic scattering of monochromatic light** by a sample. When a photon of frequency $\nu_0$ interacts with a molecule, the scattered photon may have a different frequency $\nu_0 \pm \nu_{\text{vib}}$, where $\nu_{\text{vib}}$ is a characteristic vibrational mode of the molecule. The frequency shift (called the **Raman shift**, $\Delta\bar{\nu}$, measured in **cm⁻¹**) acts as a unique "fingerprint" of the molecular/atomic structure. It was experimentally demonstrated by **Sir C. V. Raman** in **1928** (Nobel Prize in Physics, 1930).

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Key Terms:** *Inelastic scattering, Rayleigh line, Stokes line, Anti-Stokes line, Raman shift, polarizability tensor, selection rule, vibrational mode, phonon.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tennis Ball and the Drum"

Imagine you throw a tennis ball at a stationary drum skin:
- If the drum is **rigid**, the ball bounces back with the **same energy** → this is **Rayleigh scattering** (elastic).
- If the drum is **flexible**, some of the ball's energy is **absorbed** to make the drum vibrate (lower energy ball comes back) → this is the **Stokes line** (red-shifted).
- If the drum is **already vibrating**, the ball may pick up extra energy from that vibration (higher energy ball comes back) → this is the **Anti-Stokes line** (blue-shifted).

> [!NOTE]
> **The key insight:** Only **~1 in 10⁷ photons** undergo Raman scattering. The vast majority scatter elastically (Rayleigh). The challenge of Raman instrumentation is therefore to detect an extremely weak signal hidden inside an overwhelmingly strong one.

---

## 1.3 Three Types of Scattered Light — At a Glance

| Type of Scattering | Energy of Scattered Photon | Relative Intensity | Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rayleigh (Elastic)** | $h\nu_0$ | Very Strong (~99.9%) | No vibrational energy exchange |
| **Stokes (Inelastic)** | $h(\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}})$ | Weak | Molecule gains vibrational energy |
| **Anti-Stokes (Inelastic)** | $h(\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}})$ | Very Weak | Molecule loses vibrational energy |

---

## 1.4 Why Raman Spectroscopy is Special for Nanomaterials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 module on **Nanomaterials**, Raman spectroscopy is one of the *single most important* characterization tools because:
> 1. It is **non-destructive** and requires minimal sample preparation.
> 2. It directly probes **phonons**, lattice vibrations, and bonding changes at the nanoscale.
> 3. It can identify **1D** (nanotubes), **2D** (graphene, MoS₂), and **0D** (quantum dots) materials.
> 4. The Raman spectrum is highly sensitive to **size, strain, doping, and defects**.

**Standard Reference Wavenumber (used universally in KTU lab reports):** $\Delta\bar{\nu} = 1\,\text{cm}^{-1}$ resolution is typical for a research-grade Raman system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classical Electromagnetic Theory of Raman Scattering

When a monochromatic electromagnetic wave (laser) with electric field $E = E_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_0 t)$ interacts with a molecule, it induces a **time-varying dipole moment** $\mu$ given by:

$$\mu = \alpha E$$

where $\alpha$ is the **molecular polarizability**. This oscillating dipole re-radiates electromagnetic radiation in all directions — this is the **scattered light**.

### Step 1 — Express molecular polarizability for a vibrating molecule
For a normal mode of vibration with normal coordinate $Q = Q_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_{\text{vib}} t)$, we expand $\alpha$ as a Taylor series around the equilibrium position:

$$\alpha = \alpha_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q + \cdots$$

### Step 2 — Substitute back into the induced dipole equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \left[ \alpha_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q \right] E_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_0 t) \\
&= \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_0 t) + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_0 t)\cos(2\pi\nu_{\text{vib}} t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity

$$\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_0 t) \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 E_0 \Big[ \cos\big(2\pi(\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}})t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}})t\big) \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the three frequency components
- The **first term** with frequency $\nu_0$ → **Rayleigh scattering** (elastic).
- The **second bracket**, frequency $(\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}})$ → **Stokes Raman line**.
- The **second bracket**, frequency $(\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}})$ → **Anti-Stokes Raman line**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Anti-Stokes line weaker?** Because it requires the molecule to *already be in an excited vibrational state* before scattering. The population of excited vibrational states follows the **Boltzmann distribution**, which favors the ground state. Therefore, Stokes lines are nearly always observed; Anti-Stokes lines require either thermal excitation or resonance enhancement to be visible.

---

## 2.2 The Raman Selection Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule (memorize this!):**
> A vibrational mode is **Raman-active** if and only if there is a *change in the polarizability* of the molecule during the vibration:
> $$\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$$
> If $\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 = 0$, the mode is **IR-active** (must change the *dipole moment*).
> 
> **Mutual Exclusion Principle:** For molecules with a *centre of symmetry*, a mode cannot be both Raman and IR active. This makes Raman and IR **complementary techniques**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \alpha E$ | Induced dipole moment from incident field | C·m |
| 2 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 + (\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0 Q$ | Taylor expansion of polarizability | C·m²·V⁻¹ |
| 3 | $(\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0 \neq 0$ | **Raman activity selection rule** | — |
| 4 | $\Delta\bar{\nu} = \bar{\nu}_0 - \bar{\nu}_{\text{scattered}}$ | **Raman shift** (Stokes side, always positive) | **cm⁻¹** |
| 5 | $\nu_{\text{Stokes}} = \nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}}$ | Frequency of Stokes photon | Hz |
| 6 | $\nu_{\text{Anti-Stokes}} = \nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}}$ | Frequency of Anti-Stokes photon | Hz |
| 7 | $\rho = I_\perp / I_\parallel$ | **Depolarization ratio** for symmetry assignment | dimensionless |
| 8 | $I \propto \nu^4$ | Rayleigh intensity dependence | W·m⁻² |
| 9 | $\dfrac{I_{AS}}{I_S} = \left(\dfrac{\nu_0+\nu_{\text{vib}}}{\nu_0-\nu_{\text{vib}}}\right)^4 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{hc\nu_{\text{vib}}}{k_BT}\right)$ | Anti-Stokes/Stokes intensity ratio (Boltzmann) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\sigma \propto (\partial\alpha/\partial Q)^2$ | **Raman scattering cross-section** (scales as polarizability derivative squared) | cm²/molecule |
| 11 | $E_{SERS} \approx E_{EM} \cdot E_{CM}$ | Total SERS enhancement (electromagnetic × chemical) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $E_{EM} \propto \vert E_{loc}/E_0 \vert^4$ | Electromagnetic SERS enhancement (4th power of local field) | dimensionless |

> **Exam Tip:** The Boltzmann factor $\exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_BT)$ in Eq. (9) is the key to understanding why Anti-Stokes is weaker at room temperature. Use $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{10}$ cm/s, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T \approx 298$ K.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry** — identifying polymorphs of active drug ingredients.
- **2D materials research** — determining the number of graphene layers, defect density, doping level.
- **CNT identification** — Radial Breathing Mode (RBM) gives tube diameter directly.
- **Battery research** — in situ Raman probes lithium-ion insertion in electrodes.
- **Forensic science** — non-destructive identification of pigments, explosives, narcotics.
- **Biomedical SERS tags** — detection of cancer biomarkers at sub-ppb levels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Complete Classical Derivation of Raman Scattering Frequencies

**Starting Premise:** A monochromatic laser field of frequency $\nu_0$ interacts with a polarizable molecule executing a simple harmonic vibration of frequency $\nu_{\text{vib}}$.

**Step 1 — Incident electric field equation**

$$E = E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)$$

**Step 2 — Induced dipole moment definition**

The incident field polarizes the molecule, inducing a dipole moment:

$$\mu_{\text{ind}} = \alpha E$$

where $\alpha$ is the polarizability tensor (units: C·m²·V⁻¹).

**Step 3 — Vibrational coordinate of the molecule**

The normal mode vibration is assumed to be simple harmonic:

$$Q(t) = Q_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_{\text{vib}} t + \phi)$$

For simplicity, set the phase $\phi = 0$:

$$Q(t) = Q_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_{\text{vib}} t)$$

**Step 4 — First-order Taylor expansion of polarizability**

The polarizability depends on the instantaneous nuclear configuration. Expanding around equilibrium ($Q = 0$):

$$\alpha(Q) = \alpha_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \alpha}{\partial Q^2}\right)_0 Q^2 + \cdots$$

Truncate at first order (first-order Raman effect):

$$\alpha(Q) \approx \alpha_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get total induced dipole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\text{ind}}(t) &= \left[ \alpha_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_{\text{vib}} t) \right] E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t) \\
&= \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t) + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)\cos(2\pi \nu_{\text{vib}} t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the product-to-sum identity**

Using $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\text{ind}}(t) = \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t) \; + \\
\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 E_0 \left[ \cos\big(2\pi(\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}})t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}})t\big) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify the three frequency components**

| Term in expression | Frequency | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_0 t)$ | $\nu_0$ | **Rayleigh scattering** (elastic, intense) |
| $\tfrac{1}{2}(\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0 Q_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi(\nu_0-\nu_{\text{vib}})t)$ | $\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}}$ | **Stokes Raman line** (inelastic) |
| $\tfrac{1}{2}(\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0 Q_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi(\nu_0+\nu_{\text{vib}})t)$ | $\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}}$ | **Anti-Stokes Raman line** (inelastic) |

> **Conclusion:** The classical theory predicts three discrete frequencies. The intensities of the Stokes and Anti-Stokes lines are proportional to $(\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0^{\,2}$, confirming the selection rule. The theory correctly predicts the existence of the three lines but *fails* to predict the asymmetric intensity ratio — which requires the quantum mechanical (Boltzmann) treatment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stokes / Anti-Stokes Intensity Ratio (Quantum Approach)

**Step 1 — Population of vibrational levels (Boltzmann law)**

The number of molecules in the vibrational ground state ($v = 0$) and first excited state ($v = 1$) are:

$$N_0 = N \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_B T)}, \qquad N_1 = N \cdot \frac{\exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_B T)}{1 + \exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_B T)}$$

**Step 2 — Assumption (low temperature, $hc\nu_{\text{vib}} \gg k_B T$)**

For most vibrational modes at room temperature, $hc\nu_{\text{vib}} \gg k_B T$, so $N_1 \ll N_0$. Hence $N_0 \approx N$ and $N_1 \approx N\exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_B T)$.

**Step 3 — Scattering intensities**

Intensity is proportional to (i) population of initial state, (ii) $\nu^4$ factor (from classical radiation theory), (iii) Raman cross-section (assumed equal for Stokes and Anti-Stokes for the same mode):

$$I_S \propto N_0 \cdot (\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}})^4, \qquad I_{AS} \propto N_1 \cdot (\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}})^4$$

**Step 4 — Take the ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_{AS}}{I_S} &= \frac{N_1 (\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}})^4}{N_0 (\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}})^4} \\
