&= \frac{N \exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_B T) \cdot (\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}})^4}{N \cdot (\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}})^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final result**

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{I_{AS}}{I_S} = \left(\dfrac{\nu_0+\nu_{\text{vib}}}{\nu_0-\nu_{\text{vib}}}\right)^{\!4} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{h c \nu_{\text{vib}}}{k_B T}\right)\;}$$

> **Numerical sanity check:** For $\nu_0 = 6 \times 10^{14}$ Hz (green laser, 500 nm) and $\nu_{\text{vib}} = 10^{13}$ Hz (1000 cm⁻¹) at $T = 300$ K:
> - The frequency ratio factor ≈ $(1.017)^4 ≈ 1.07$
> - The Boltzmann factor $\approx \exp(-4.8) \approx 0.008$
> - So $I_{AS}/I_S \approx 0.009$ → **Anti-Stokes is ~100× weaker**, agreeing with experiment. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SERS Enhancement Factor (Local-Field Picture)

**Step 1 — Far-field Raman intensity**

For a molecule in the incident field $E_0$ (without any metal), the Raman intensity is:

$$I_{\text{far}} \propto \sigma \cdot \vert E_0 \vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Near-field enhancement at a plasmonic nanostructure**

A rough noble-metal surface (Au, Ag) supports localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), which amplifies the incident field to $E_{\text{loc}} = g E_0$ at the molecule's location. Here $g$ is the field enhancement factor (typically $10$–$100$).

**Step 3 — Both the incident laser AND the Raman-scattered photon are enhanced**

Because the LSPR has a broad spectral bandwidth, *both* the incoming laser and the outgoing Raman-shifted photon benefit from the local-field amplification:

$$E_{\text{enh, in}} = g_{\text{in}} E_0, \qquad E_{\text{enh, out}} = g_{\text{out}} E_0$$

**Step 4 — Total SERS intensity**

The Raman signal scales as the *product* of incoming and outgoing enhancements:

$$I_{SERS} \propto \sigma \cdot \vert g_{\text{in}} E_0 \vert^2 \cdot \vert g_{\text{out}} \vert^2$$

**Step 5 — Approximation (resonance case, $g_{\text{in}} \approx g_{\text{out}} = g$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SERS} &\propto \sigma \cdot g^4 \cdot \vert E_0 \vert^2 \\
\frac{I_{SERS}}{I_{\text{far}}} &\approx g^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Result**

$$\boxed{\;E_{SERS}^{\,EM} = \dfrac{I_{SERS}}{I_{\text{far}}} \approx g^4 \approx 10^6 \text{ to } 10^{10}\;}$$

> **Engineering implication:** A modest field enhancement of $g = 100$ produces a Raman enhancement of $10^8$ — enough for **single-molecule detection** at "hot spots" of nanostructured Ag or Au substrates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy-Level Diagram — Rayleigh, Stokes, and Anti-Stokes Transitions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef ground fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef excited fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef virtual fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#b7950b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef photon fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    E0[Ground Vibrational State v=0]:::ground
    E1[Excited Vibrational State v=1]:::excited
    E2[Excited Vibrational State v=2]:::excited

    VR1[Virtual State]:::virtual
    VR2[Virtual State]:::virtual
    VR3[Virtual State]:::virtual

    P1[Photon In: h nu 0]:::photon
    P2[Photon Out: h nu 0]:::photon
    P3[Photon Out: h nu 0 minus h nu vib Stokes]:::photon
    P4[Photon Out: h nu 0 plus h nu vib Anti Stokes]:::photon

    P1 --> VR1
    VR1 --> P2
    VR1 --> E0

    P1 --> VR2
    VR2 --> P3
    VR2 --> E1

    E1 --> VR3
    VR3 --> P4
    VR3 --> E0
```

**How to read this figure (KTU board-exam standard):**
- The **horizontal dashed lines** labelled v=0, v=1, v=2 are real vibrational levels.
- The **yellow "Virtual State"** is a non-stationary, very short-lived (~$10^{-15}$ s) intermediate level — the molecule is not actually in an eigenstate here.
- **Stokes** (red arrow down to v=1): the molecule *gains* vibrational energy from the photon → scattered photon has **less** energy.
- **Anti-Stokes** (green arrow up from v=1): the molecule *loses* vibrational energy to the photon → scattered photon has **more** energy.
- **Rayleigh** (blue): returns to v=0, no energy change.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Instrumentation of a Modern Raman Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef source fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sample fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef optic fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detect fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    L[Laser Source 532 nm or 785 nm]:::source
    M1[Mirror or Notch Filter Rayleigh Rejection]:::optic
    SH[Sample Holder Microscope or Macro]:::sample
    M2[Collection Lens]:::optic
    MON[Monochromator Diffraction Grating]:::optic
    NF[Notch or Edge Filter]:::optic
    DET[Detector CCD or PMT]:::detect
    PROC[Computer and Software Spectrum]:::comp

    L --> M1
    M1 --> SH
    SH --> M2
    M2 --> NF
    NF --> MON
    MON --> DET
    DET --> PROC
```

**Component functions:**
1. **Laser Source** — Provides intense, monochromatic, coherent photons. Common wavelengths: 532 nm (green), 633 nm (HeNe red), 785 nm (NIR for biology).
2. **Notch / Rayleigh-Rejection Filter** — Critical because Rayleigh is $10^6$–$10^8$× stronger than Raman. Without it, the detector is blinded.
3. **Sample Stage** — May be a microscope (for spatial resolution ~1 µm) or a cuvette holder.
4. **Monochromator** — Spatially separates different Raman-shifted wavelengths using a diffraction grating.
5. **Detector** — Modern systems use a **CCD** (Charge-Coupled Device) cooled to $-70$°C to reduce thermal noise.
6. **Computer** — Plots **Intensity (counts) vs. Raman shift (cm⁻¹)**.

---

## 4.3 Typical Raman Spectrum of a Nanomaterial (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef axis fill:#f8f9f9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef rayleigh fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stokes fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef antistokes fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    XAXIS[Raman shift axis in cm to the power of negative 1]:::axis
    RAY[Rayleigh line at 0 cm to the power of negative 1 very intense]:::rayleigh
    S1[Stokes peaks at positive shifts e.g. D band 1350 G band 1580]:::stokes
    AS1[Anti Stokes peaks at negative shifts mirror image much weaker]:::antistokes

    XAXIS --> RAY
    RAY --> S1
    RAY --> AS1
```

> **Important note for board exams:** The Raman shift is **always reported as a positive value** on the Stokes side, even though mathematically it represents energy lost by the photon. By convention, the Rayleigh line is placed at the origin (0 cm⁻¹) and the spectrum is plotted from ~ −3500 to +3500 cm⁻¹ (Anti-Stokes to Stokes).

---

## 4.4 SERS Substrate — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef laser fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef metal fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mol fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef signal fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    LAS[Incident Laser Photon]:::laser
    NP[Metal Nanoparticle Ag or Au 20 to 100 nm LSPR Active]:::metal
    HOT[Hot Spot Inter particle Junction]:::metal
    MOL[Analyte Molecule Adsorbed]:::mol
    EM[Enhanced Local Field E loc 10 to 100 times E 0]:::signal
    RAM[Enhanced Raman Scattering 10 to the power of 6 to 10 to the power of 10]:::signal

    LAS --> NP
    NP --> HOT
    HOT --> MOL
    MOL --> EM
    EM --> RAM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` What is Rayleigh scattering? How is it different from Raman scattering? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Rayleigh scattering is the **elastic scattering** of light by particles or molecules much smaller than the wavelength of light. The scattered photon has the **same frequency** as the incident photon ($\nu_0$), i.e., there is no energy exchange. Its intensity is proportional to $1/\lambda^4$ (explains the blue sky).

In **Raman scattering**, the interaction is **inelastic**: the scattered photon has frequency $\nu_0 \pm \nu_{\text{vib}}$, where $\nu_{\text{vib}}$ is a vibrational frequency of the molecule. Rayleigh scattering is the dominant process (~99.9% of all scattering), while Raman scattering is rare (~1 in $10^7$ photons).

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Define Stokes and Anti-Stokes lines. Why is the Anti-Stokes line weaker than the Stokes line? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Stokes line:** Scattered radiation of frequency $\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}}$, produced when the molecule *gains* vibrational energy from the incident photon.
- **Anti-Stokes line:** Scattered radiation of frequency $\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}}$, produced when the molecule is *already* in an excited vibrational state and *gives* energy to the scattered photon.

**Reason for weakness:** Anti-Stokes scattering requires the initial population of *excited* vibrational states, given by the **Boltzmann factor** $\exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_B T)$, which is very small at room temperature. Hence $I_{AS} \ll I_S$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]` (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Derive the classical theory of Raman scattering. Explain Stokes and Anti-Stokes lines with a neat energy-level diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**
1. Write the incident electric field: $E = E_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_0 t)$.
2. State that the induced dipole is $\mu = \alpha E$.
3. Express the vibrating normal coordinate: $Q = Q_0 \cos(2\pi\nu_{\text{vib}} t)$.
4. Taylor-expand $\alpha$ to first order in $Q$.
5. Substitute and use $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B)+\cos(A+B)]$.
6. Identify the three resulting frequency components and label them.
7. State the selection rule $(\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0 \neq 0$.
8. Draw the energy-level diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes).

> Full derivation is in **Section 3.1** above. The board expects to see all 7 steps written out sequentially; skipping the trigonometric expansion loses 2 marks.

#### **(b) With a block diagram, describe the instrumentation of a Raman spectrometer. Compare Raman and IR spectroscopy. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**
1. List the components in correct order: **Laser → Sample → Notch filter → Monochromator → Detector → Computer**.
2. Explain the function of each (refer to Section 4.2 block diagram).
3. Present a comparison table:

| Feature | Raman Spectroscopy | IR Spectroscopy |
|---|---|---|
| Phenomenon | Inelastic scattering | Absorption |
| Selection Rule | Change in polarizability | Change in dipole moment |
| Sample Prep | Minimal (solid/liquid/gas) | KBr pellet, Nujol mull |
| Water Interference | Weak Raman signal from H₂O | Strong, masks spectrum |
| Complementary? | **Yes** — fills IR-inactive modes | **Yes** — fills Raman-inactive modes |

> **Valuation cue:** A common omission is explaining *why* the notch filter is essential (because Rayleigh is $10^6$–$10^8$× stronger). Always mention it.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]` (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) State and explain the selection rule for Raman activity. Discuss the principle and enhancement mechanism of Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**
1. **Selection rule:** A vibration is Raman-active iff $(\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0 \neq 0$. Provide a one-line justification: "the polarizability must change during the vibration."
2. Provide the **Mutual Exclusion Principle** for centrosymmetric molecules.
3. **SERS Principle:** When a molecule is adsorbed on a roughened noble-metal (Au, Ag, Cu) surface, the Raman signal is enhanced by factors of $10^6$–$10^{10}$.
4. **Two enhancement mechanisms:**
   - **Electromagnetic (EM)** — localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) amplifies the local field. Enhancement $\propto \vert E_{\text{loc}}/E_0 \vert^4$.
   - **Chemical (CM)** — charge-transfer complex forms between metal and molecule; resonance Raman effect.
5. Total enhancement: $E_{\text{total}} = E_{EM} \times E_{CM}$.

> Refer to Section 3.3 for the rigorous $g^4$ derivation.

#### **(b) Describe the applications of Raman spectroscopy in the characterization of nanomaterials. Give at least three specific examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline (one paragraph each, 2 marks × 3 = 6, plus introduction = 1):**

1. **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs):** The **Radial Breathing Mode (RBM)** at low wavenumbers ($100$–$400$ cm⁻¹) gives the tube diameter via $\omega_{\text{RBM}} = A/d + B$ (with $A \approx 248$ cm⁻¹·nm). The **G-band** (~1580 cm⁻¹) and **D-band** (~1350 cm⁻¹) ratio indicates defect density.

2. **Graphene:** The position and width of the **2D-band** (~2680 cm⁻¹) reveals the number of layers (monolayer, bilayer, few-layer). The ratio $I_D/I_G$ gives defect density.

3. **Semiconductor Quantum Dots:** **Resonance Raman spectroscopy** with laser energy near the band gap selectively enhances specific phonon modes, allowing size determination.

4. **In-situ battery research:** Real-time monitoring of Li-ion insertion/extraction in electrodes via peak shifts in transition-metal oxide cathodes.

5. **SERS-based biosensors:** Detection of cancer biomarkers at femtomolar concentrations.

> **Bonus point (1 mark):** Mention **Tip-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (TERS)** for sub-10 nm spatial resolution using an AFM tip coated with Ag.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically LOSE marks in Raman Spectroscopy questions (KTU ESE pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\nu^4$ factor** when discussing Rayleigh or Raman intensities. Always write $I \propto \nu^4$.
> 2. **Stating the selection rule wrongly** as "change in dipole moment" — that is the IR selection rule. Raman requires change in **polarizability**.
> 3. **Conflating Stokes and Anti-Stokes frequencies.** Stokes: $\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}}$ (lower). Anti-Stokes: $\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}}$ (higher).
> 4. **Skipping the notch filter** in the instrument block diagram. This costs 1 full mark every time.
> 5. **Not drawing the energy-level diagram** when the question says "with a neat diagram" — instant 2-mark penalty.
> 6. **Writing the Raman shift as a frequency** in Hz. Always convert to **wavenumber (cm⁻¹)** because KTU board expects the standard unit.
> 7. **Missing the Boltzmann factor** in the Anti-Stokes/Stokes ratio derivation. Without it, the asymmetric intensities are inexplicable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Raman Spectroscopy (Module 3, KTU 2024)**

- **Discovery:** C. V. Raman, 1928 (Nobel 1930). Based on **inelastic light scattering**.
- **Three lines in the spectrum:** Rayleigh (elastic, $\nu_0$), Stokes ($\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{vib}}$), Anti-Stokes ($\nu_0 + \nu_{\text{vib}}$).
- **Intensity ordering:** $I_{\text{Rayleigh}} \gg I_{\text{Stokes}} \gg I_{\text{Anti-Stokes}}$.
- **Anti-Stokes weakness:** Boltzmann factor $\exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_BT)$ — excited states are sparsely populated.
- **Raman selection rule:** $(\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0 \neq 0$ — *change in polarizability*.
- **IR selection rule:** $(\partial\mu/\partial Q)_0 \neq 0$ — *change in dipole moment*.
- **Mutual Exclusion Principle:** For centrosymmetric molecules, no mode is both Raman and IR active.
- **Key equations to memorize:**
  - $\mu = \alpha E$
  - $\alpha = \alpha_0 + (\partial\alpha/\partial Q)_0 Q$
  - $\Delta\bar{\nu} = \bar{\nu}_0 - \bar{\nu}_{\text{scattered}}$ (in **cm⁻¹**)
  - $I_{AS}/I_S = \left(\dfrac{\nu_0+\nu_{\text{vib}}}{\nu_0-\nu_{\text{vib}}}\right)^4 \exp(-hc\nu_{\text{vib}}/k_BT)$
  - SERS enhancement $\propto g^4$ (electromagnetic mechanism).
- **Instrumentation block order:** **Laser → Notch Filter → Sample → Collection Optics → Monochromator → Detector (CCD) → Computer**.
- **Common laser wavelengths:** 532 nm (green), 633 nm (red HeNe), 785 nm (NIR for biology), 1064 nm (FT-Raman).
- **Notch filter is non-negotiable** in any block diagram answer.
- **For nanomaterials:**
  - Graphene — 2D band ~2680 cm⁻¹ → number of layers.
  - CNTs — RBM frequency → tube diameter.
  - $I_D/I_G$ ratio → defect density in carbon-based materials.
- **SERS substrates:** Roughened Ag, Au, Cu nanoparticles. Enhancement up to $10^{10}$ → single-molecule detection.
- **Comparison with IR:** Raman is **complementary**, not competitive. Use Raman for symmetric vibrations and aqueous samples; use IR for asymmetric vibrations and strong dipole-changing modes.
- **Most common marks-loser in KTU exam:** Wrong selection rule (use polarizability, NOT dipole moment) and missing the notch filter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (NMR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (NMR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy** is a non-destructive, quantitative analytical technique that exploits the magnetic properties of certain atomic nuclei to determine the physical and chemical structure of molecules. It is based on the principle that nuclei with non-zero spin (e.g., **¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁹F, ³¹P, ²⁹Si**), when placed in a strong external magnetic field ($B_0$), absorb and re-emit electromagnetic radiation at characteristic resonance frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the course OEBBT833, NMR is studied as a *structural characterization tool for nanomaterials* — especially in distinguishing surface vs. bulk atomic environments in quantum dots, nanotubes, and nanoporous frameworks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Top in a Magnetic Field"

Imagine a child's spinning top tilted slightly from the vertical axis. As it spins, the gravitational pull makes its axis **wobble** in a circular motion. This is called **precession**.

Now replace gravity with a powerful magnet, and the top with an atomic nucleus. The nucleus "wobbles" around the magnetic field direction at a frequency called the **Larmor frequency** ($\omega_0$). When we apply a radiofrequency (RF) pulse exactly at this precession frequency, the nucleus **absorbs energy** and flips its spin orientation — this is *resonance*. When the pulse is turned off, the nucleus relaxes back, emitting a signal that we detect and convert into a spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Example:** NMR is the same technology behind **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** in hospitals, but at the *nanomaterial* scale it is used to probe atomic environments in graphene, CNTs, and zeolites.

## 1.3 Fundamental Atomic Prerequisites

For a nucleus to be NMR-active, it must possess a **non-zero nuclear spin quantum number ($I$)**:

| Nucleus | Spin ($I$) | NMR Frequency (MHz at 7.05 T) | Natural Abundance |
|---|---|---|---|
| ¹H | 1/2 | 300.0 | 99.98% |
| ¹³C | 1/2 | 75.4 | 1.10% |
| ¹⁹F | 1/2 | 282.2 | 100% |
| ³¹P | 1/2 | 121.4 | 100% |
| ²⁹Si | 1/2 | 59.6 | 4.70% |
| ²H | 1 | 46.0 | 0.015% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **gyromagnetic ratio ($\gamma$)** is the fingerprint constant for each nucleus. **For ¹H, $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^{8}$ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ (or 42.577 MHz/T)** — a critical KTU exam value.

## 1.4 Spin States and Energy Splitting (Zeeman Effect)

A nucleus with $I = 1/2$ (like ¹H) has **two spin states** in a magnetic field:
- **$m = +1/2$** (spin aligned with $B_0$) — **Lower energy (α-state)**
- **$m = -1/2$** (spin opposed to $B_0$) — **Higher energy (β-state)**

The energy difference $\Delta E$ is given by:
$$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$$

where $\hbar$ is the **reduced Planck's constant = $1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plank's constant (h) = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** and **Boltzmann constant ($k_B$) = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** are essential constants for NMR derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle of Resonance (Larmor Condition)

When an external static magnetic field $B_0$ is applied along the z-axis, the nuclear magnetic moment vector $\vec{\mu}$ precesses around $B_0$ at the **Larmor angular frequency**:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

In Hz (cycles per second):
$$\nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resonance occurs** when the frequency of the applied RF pulse ($\nu_{RF}$) exactly equals the Larmor precession frequency ($\nu_0$). At this exact matching, the nucleus absorbs energy and transitions from the lower to the higher spin state.

## 2.2 The Bloch Equations Framework

The **Bloch equations** (1946) describe the time evolution of the macroscopic nuclear magnetization vector $\vec{M} = (M_x, M_y, M_z)$:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (M_y B_z - M_z B_y) - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma (M_z B_x - M_x B_z) - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \gamma (M_x B_y - M_y B_x) - \frac{M_z - M_0}{T_1}$$

Where:
- $M_0$ = equilibrium magnetization
- $T_1$ = **spin-lattice (longitudinal) relaxation time**
- $T_2$ = **spin-spin (transverse) relaxation time**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $T_1$ and $T_2$ Matter in Nanomaterials:** In nanoparticles, surface atoms have **shorter $T_1$** due to restricted mobility. KTU problems often ask how $T_1$ varies with particle size — smaller particles → more surface defects → faster relaxation.

## 2.3 Chemical Shift — The NMR Fingerprint

When a nucleus is placed inside a molecule, the local electron cloud **shields** the nucleus from the external field. The **effective field** experienced is:

$$B_{eff} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$$

where $\sigma$ is the **shielding constant** (dimensionless).

The **chemical shift** ($\delta$) is defined in **parts per million (ppm)** relative to a reference standard (TMS for ¹H and ¹³C):

$$\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{reference}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \; \text{ppm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\delta$ is **independent of spectrometer frequency**, making NMR data universally comparable — a critical property for KTU-level analysis.

## 2.4 Spin-Spin Coupling (J-Coupling)

Indirect coupling through bonds between two nuclei (e.g., H-C-C-H) splits NMR peaks. The number of peaks in a multiplet follows the **$n+1$ rule**:

$$\text{Number of peaks} = 2nI + 1$$

For spin-1/2 nuclei, this simplifies to **$n+1$**, where $n$ is the number of equivalent neighboring nuclei.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Larmor frequency | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Resonance condition | rad·s⁻¹ |
| Larmor in Hz | $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ | Spectrometer tuning | Hz |
| Energy gap | $\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$ | Population difference | J |
| Boltzmann population ratio | $N_\alpha / N_\beta = e^{\Delta E / k_B T}$ | Signal strength | dimensionless |
| Chemical shift | $\delta = (\nu_s - \nu_r)/\nu_0 \times 10^6$ | Structural ID | ppm |
| Shielding field | $B_{eff} = B_0(1 - \sigma)$ | Local electronic environment | T |
| Multiplicity rule | $2nI + 1$ | Spin-spin splitting | count |
| Free Induction Decay | $S(t) = M_0 e^{-t/T_2} \cos(\omega_0 t)$ | Time-domain signal | a.u. |
| Signal-to-noise ratio | $S/N \propto B_0^{3/2} N_s$ | Sensitivity scaling | dimensionless |
| Curie magnetization | $M_0 = C B_0 / T$ | Field dependence | A/m |
| Solid-state MAS rate | $\nu_{rot} > \nu_{aniso}$ | Line narrowing | kHz |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU boards often expect students to write the **Boltzmann population difference** explicitly, including numerical evaluation at typical 7 T / 298 K conditions. Practice the full derivation.

## 2.6 Pulse Fourier Transform NMR (FT-NMR) — Why It's Essential

Traditional **Continuous Wave (CW) NMR** scans frequencies slowly. Modern **FT-NMR** applies a short, broadband RF pulse that excites all nuclei simultaneously, then records the **Free Induction Decay (FID)** — a time-domain signal that is **Fourier-transformed** into the frequency-domain spectrum.

$$\text{FID} \xrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \text{Spectrum}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse angle** for maximum signal: **Ernst angle $\theta_E$** given by $\cos \theta_E = e^{-T_R/T_1}$, where $T_R$ is the repetition time. Optimize to save scan time in ¹³C NMR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Larmor Precession Frequency from Torque

**Setup:** A magnetic moment $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$ in an external field $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$ experiences a torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$.

**Step 1 — Write the torque equation:**
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B} = (\gamma \vec{L}) \times (B_0 \hat{z}) = \gamma B_0 (\vec{L} \times \hat{z})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law for angular momentum:** $\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt$, therefore:
$$\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 3 — Since $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$, we obtain:**
$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 4 — In Cartesian components with $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$:**
$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma \mu_y B_0, \quad \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma \mu_x B_0, \quad \frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate the first equation again:**
$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = \gamma B_0 (-\gamma \mu_x B_0) = -(\gamma B_0)^2 \mu_x$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the simple harmonic motion form:**
$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} + \omega_0^2 \mu_x = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad \boxed{\omega_0 = \gamma B_0}$$

This is the **Larmor precession angular frequency**, expressed in **radians per second**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Population Difference (Boltzmann Statistics)

**Step 1 — Energy of a spin state with magnetic quantum number $m$ in field $B_0$:**
$$E_m = -\gamma \hbar m B_0$$

**Step 2 — For $I = 1/2$ nuclei, the two levels ($m = +1/2$ and $m = -1/2$) have:**
$$E_{\alpha} = -\frac{1}{2}\gamma \hbar B_0, \quad E_{\beta} = +\frac{1}{2}\gamma \hbar B_0$$

**Step 3 — Energy gap:**
$$\Delta E = E_{\beta} - E_{\alpha} = \gamma \hbar B_0$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of populations from Boltzmann distribution:**
$$\frac{N_{\alpha}}{N_{\beta}} = e^{\Delta E / k_B T}$$

**Step 5 — Compute population difference per nucleus:**
$$N_{\alpha} - N_{\beta} = N_{total} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}}{1 + e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply high-temperature approximation** ($k_B T \gg \Delta E$), so $e^{-\Delta E/k_BT} \approx 1 - \Delta E/k_BT$:

$$\frac{N_{\alpha} - N_{\beta}}{N_{total}} \approx \frac{\Delta E}{2 k_B T} = \frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2 k_B T}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example for ¹H at $B_0 = 7.05$ T and $T = 298$ K:**

$$\frac{\Delta E}{2 k_B T} = \frac{(2.675 \times 10^8)(1.054 \times 10^{-34})(7.05)}{2 \times (1.381 \times 10^{-23})(298)}$$

Compute numerator:
$$(2.675 \times 10^8)(1.054 \times 10^{-34})(7.05) = 1.988 \times 10^{-25} \; \text{J}$$

Compute denominator:
$$2 \times (1.381 \times 10^{-23})(298) = 8.230 \times 10^{-21} \; \text{J}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{N_{\alpha} - N_{\beta}}{N_{total}} = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{8.230 \times 10^{-21}} \approx 2.42 \times 10^{-5}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This tiny population excess (~24 ppm) explains why NMR is intrinsically insensitive** — a key reason why nanomaterial NMR often needs signal averaging (thousands of scans) and techniques like DNP (Dynamic Nuclear Polarization).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Free Induction Decay (FID) Expression

**Step 1 — After an RF pulse tips the magnetization by 90° into the xy-plane, the transverse magnetization $M_{xy}(0) = M_0$ begins precessing at $\omega_0$.**

**Step 2 — In the rotating frame, ignoring relaxation, the time-domain signal is:**
$$S(t) = M_0 \cos(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 3 — Include transverse relaxation via Bloch equation decay:**
$$S(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \cos(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 4 — The Fourier transform converts this to a Lorentzian peak in frequency domain:**
$$S(\omega) = \frac{M_0 \, T_2}{1 + T_2^2 (\omega - \omega_0)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM):**
$$\Delta \nu_{1/2} = \frac{1}{\pi T_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In Nanomaterials:** Reduced particle size increases surface defects, decreasing $T_2$ and broadening NMR lines. This is a frequent KTU exam theme.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Solid-State NMR and Magic Angle Spinning (MAS)

In solids, the dipolar coupling between nuclei and **chemical shift anisotropy (CSA)** broaden the spectra. Spinning the sample at the **magic angle** $\theta_m$ relative to $B_0$ averages out anisotropic interactions:

$$\cos^2 \theta_m = \frac{1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_m = 54.74°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The angle 54.74° is the **magic angle** — frequently asked in KTU 2024 exams. Remember: $3\cos^2\theta - 1 = 0$ gives this critical angle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NMR Spectrometer Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SAMPLE TUBE] --> B[HIGH FIELD MAGNET B0]
    B --> C[RF TRANSMITTER COIL]
    C --> D[RF PULSE GENERATOR]
    D --> E[COMPUTER CONTROL UNIT]
    E --> D
    B --> F[RECEIVER COIL]
    F --> G[AMPLIFIER]
    G --> H[ADC AND DIGITIZER]
    H --> I[FOURIER TRANSFORM PROCESSOR]
    I --> J[SPECTRUM DISPLAY]
    J --> E
    B --> K[FIELD SHIM COILS]
    K --> E
    B --> L[PROBE AND MAS MODULE]
    L --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> The probe houses both transmitter and receiver coils. For **solid-state MAS-NMR**, the probe includes a pneumatic spinner rotating the sample at 5–60 kHz at the magic angle.

## 4.2 NMR Energy Level Diagram and Resonance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL MAGNETIC FIELD B0 ALONG Z AXIS"]
        direction TB
        A["ENERGY LEVEL BETA m EQUALS MINUS ONE HALF E_HIGH EQUALS PLUS GAMMA HBAR B0 / 2"]
        B["ENERGY LEVEL ALPHA m EQUALS PLUS ONE HALF E_LOW EQUALS MINUS GAMMA HBAR B0 / 2"]
    end
    A -->|RF PULSE AT LARMOR FREQUENCY| B
    B -->|RF PULSE AT LARMOR FREQUENCY| A
    C["RF SOURCE nu_RF EQUALS gamma B0 / 2pi"] --> A
    C --> B
```

## 4.3 Pulse Sequence Flowchart (FT-NMR Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[IDLE STATE M_z EQUALS M_0] --> P1[APPLY 90 DEGREE RF PULSE]
    P1 --> P2[M_z TIP INTO XY PLANE]
    P2 --> P3[FREE INDUCTION DECAY BEGINS]
    P3 --> P4[RECORD TIME DOMAIN SIGNAL S_t]
    P4 --> P5{IS SNR ADEQUATE}
    P5 -->|NO| P6[REPEAT N SCANS AVERAGE]
    P6 --> P1
    P5 -->|YES| P7[FOURIER TRANSFORM S_t TO S_omega]
    P7 --> P8[DISPLAY FINAL SPECTRUM]
```

## 4.4 Spin-Spin Coupling Tree Diagram (Multiplet Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT[PROTON A NEIGHBORED BY 2 EQUIVALENT B PROTONS] --> BRANCH
    subgraph BRANCH[COUPLING TREE]
        direction TB
        Q1[B SEES A IN SPIN UP] --> Q1A[PEAK A1]
        Q1 --> Q1B[PEAK A2]
        Q2[B SEES A IN SPIN DOWN] --> Q2A[PEAK A3]
    end
    OUTPUT[TRIPLET 1 TO 2 TO 1 INTENSITY]
    BRANCH --> OUTPUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pascal's Triangle** for $I = 1/2$: 1 (singlet) → 1:1 (doublet) → 1:2:1 (triplet) → 1:3:3:1 (quartet) → 1:4:6:4:1 (quintet). Memorize for KTU 14-mark problems.

## 4.5 Functional Architecture: Nanomaterial NMR Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NANO[NANOMATERIAL SAMPLE] --> PREP[DISPERSION OR POWDER PACKING]
    PREP --> NMR[INSERT INTO NMR PROBE]
    NMR --> ACQ[ACQUIRE FID SIGNAL]
    ACQ --> FT[FOURIER TRANSFORM]
    FT --> ANA[SPECTRAL ANALYSIS]
    ANA --> ID1[IDENTIFY SURFACE VS BULK PEAKS]
    ANA --> ID2[QUANTIFY DEFECT CONCENTRATION]
    ANA --> ID3[MEASURE T1 AND T2 RELAXATION]
    ID1 --> REPORT[STRUCTURAL REPORT]
    ID2 --> REPORT
    ID3 --> REPORT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. List any two NMR-active nuclei and their gyromagnetic ratios.**

**Model Answer:**
NMR spectroscopy is a non-destructive analytical technique that exploits the interaction of nuclear spins with an external magnetic field and radiofrequency radiation. NMR-active nuclei must have a non-zero spin quantum number ($I \neq 0$).

- ¹H: $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹
- ¹³C: $\gamma = 6.728 \times 10^7$ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is chemical shift in NMR? Why is it expressed in ppm?**

**Model Answer:**
Chemical shift ($\delta$) is the relative change in resonance frequency of a nucleus caused by the shielding effect of surrounding electrons. It is expressed in **ppm** to make it **independent of the spectrometer's operating frequency**, enabling direct comparison of spectra recorded on different instruments.

$$\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{reference}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \; \text{ppm}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Larmor precession frequency for a nucleus of gyromagnetic ratio $\gamma$ placed in a magnetic field $B_0$. Show that resonance occurs when the applied RF frequency equals $\omega_0 / 2\pi$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the principle of **Magic Angle Spinning (MAS) in solid-state NMR**. Why is the angle 54.74° called the "magic angle"? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1:** The magnetic moment of a nucleus is $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$ where $\vec{L}$ is the angular momentum.

**Step 2:** In an external field $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$, the torque is $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$.

**Step 3:** Using $d\vec{L}/dt = \vec{\tau}$:

$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 4:** Solving component-wise gives oscillatory motion at $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$.

**Step 5:** In Hz: $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$.

**Step 6:** When an RF pulse of frequency $\nu_{RF} = \nu_0$ is applied, the nucleus resonates and absorbs energy $\Delta E = h\nu_0 = \gamma \hbar B_0$, transitioning from $m = +1/2$ to $m = -1/2$.

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1:** In solids, dipolar coupling and chemical shift anisotropy (CSA) have angular dependence of the form $3\cos^2\theta - 1$.

**Step 2:** Spinning the sample at angle $\theta$ averages this term. The time average becomes $\langle 3\cos^2\theta - 1 \rangle$.

**Step 3:** Setting this to zero:
$$3\cos^2\theta_m - 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos^2\theta_m = 1/3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_m = 54.74°$$

**Step 4:** At this angle, the anisotropic interactions average to zero, producing narrow lines similar to solution-state NMR.

**Step 5:** The angle is "magic" because it completely removes line-broadening due to dipolar and CSA interactions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Fourier Transform NMR (FT-NMR)**. Derive the expression for the Free Induction Decay (FID) signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Boltzmann population difference** for ¹H nuclei at $B_0 = 9.4$ T and $T = 300$ K. Discuss the engineering implications for NMR sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1:** In FT-NMR, a short broadband RF pulse excites all nuclei at once, replacing slow frequency sweeps used in CW-NMR.

**Step 2:** The pulse tips the magnetization vector by 90° into the xy-plane.

**Step 3:** Precession of transverse magnetization $M_{xy}$ induces a decaying sinusoidal voltage in the receiver coil — the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**.

**Step 4:** Mathematical expression:
$$S(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \cos(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 5:** The FID is digitized and **Fourier Transformed**:
$$S(\omega) = \int_0^{\infty} S(t) \, e^{-i\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 6:** This yields the conventional frequency-domain NMR spectrum with characteristic peaks for each chemical environment.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1:** Energy gap:
$$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0 = (2.675 \times 10^8)(1.054 \times 10^{-34})(9.4)$$
$$\Delta E = 2.651 \times 10^{-25} \; \text{J}$$

**Step 2:** Thermal energy:
$$k_B T = (1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300) = 4.143 \times 10^{-21} \; \text{J}$$

**Step 3:** Ratio:
$$\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T} = \frac{2.651 \times 10^{-25}}{4.143 \times 10^{-21}} = 6.40 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4:** Population excess:
$$\frac{N_\alpha - N_\beta}{N} \approx \frac{\Delta E}{2k_BT} = 3.20 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Implications:**
- Only **32 ppm excess** of nuclei contribute to signal → low inherent sensitivity.
- **Higher $B_0$** improves sensitivity as $\propto B_0^{3/2}$.
- **Lower T** increases excess but is limited by sample constraints.
- **DNP / Hyperpolarization** is used in nanomaterial studies to overcome this limit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ — **WRONG!** $\omega_0$ is in **rad/s**, so the $2\pi$ belongs in the Hz form $\nu_0 = \omega_0 / 2\pi$.
> - Do **NOT** confuse shielding constant $\sigma$ with chemical shift $\delta$ — they are inversely related.
> - For MAS, $3\cos^2\theta - 1 = 0$ is the derivation anchor — do not skip this step.
> - Always specify units for $\gamma$: rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ or MHz/T.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — NMR Spectroscopy (Module 3):**

- **NMR-Active Nuclei:** Must have $I \neq 0$. ¹H ($I=1/2$) is most common; ¹³C ($I=1/2$) is 1.1% abundant.
- **Larmor Frequency:** $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ (rad/s); $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ (Hz).
- **Gyromagnetic Ratio of ¹H:** $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ (= 42.577 MHz/T).
- **Energy Gap:** $\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$ — used for Boltzmann population calculations.
- **Population Excess:** $\Delta N / N \approx \gamma \hbar B_0 / (2k_BT) \approx 10^{-5}$ at room temperature.
- **Chemical Shift:** $\delta$ in ppm, referenced to **TMS (Tetramethylsilane) = 0 ppm**.
- **Multiplicity Rule:** $2nI + 1$; for $I=1/2$, simplifies to $n+1$.
- **Magic Angle:** $\theta_m = 54.74°$ where $3\cos^2\theta - 1 = 0$ (MAS in solid-state NMR).
- **Bloch Equations:** Govern $M_x, M_y, M_z$ dynamics with relaxation times $T_1$ (longitudinal) and $T_2$ (transverse).
- **FID Expression:** $S(t) = M_0 e^{-t/T_2}\cos(\omega_0 t)$ → Fourier transform → Lorentzian spectrum.
- **Ernst Angle:** $\cos\theta_E = e^{-T_R/T_1}$ for optimum sensitivity in repeated pulse experiments.
- **Nanomaterial Applications:** Surface vs. bulk atom identification, defect quantification, $T_1$/$T_2$ mapping for particle-size estimation, and ²⁹Si MAS-NMR for silicate nanostructures.
- **High-Field Advantage:** $S/N \propto B_0^{3/2}$ — driving modern spectrometers to 1.0+ GHz.
- **DNP Enhancement:** Hyperpolarization boosts signal by 100–1000×, critical for low-γ nuclei in nanomaterials.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Final Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, structure the response as **Definition → Principle → Derivation → Diagram → Application**. Always include a labeled diagram and at least one numerical calculation with full unit clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)** is a non-destructive, vibrational spectroscopic analytical technique that uses a **Michelson Interferometer** to modulate broadband infrared radiation and a **mathematical Fourier Transform** algorithm (executed on a computer) to convert the raw time-domain interferogram into a frequency-domain infrared absorption spectrum. It is the standard characterization tool in nanotechnology for identifying chemical bonds, functional groups, and molecular fingerprints of synthesized nanomaterials (nanoparticles, nanotubes, graphene oxide, polymers, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FTIR is categorized as a **spectroscopic characterization technique** in Module 3. Students must be able to (i) explain the role of the Michelson interferometer, (ii) describe the Fourier transform operation, (iii) interpret a transmittance/absorption spectrum, and (iv) identify characteristic IR bands of common functional groups (O–H, C=O, N–H, C–H, Si–O, etc.).

The fundamental output of FTIR is a plot of:

- **Transmittance** $T(\tilde{\nu}) = \dfrac{I(\tilde{\nu})}{I_0(\tilde{\nu})} \times 100\%$ versus **wavenumber** $\tilde{\nu}$ (in cm⁻¹), OR
- **Absorbance** $A(\tilde{\nu}) = -\log_{10} T(\tilde{\nu})$ versus wavenumber.

The standard spectral window is the **mid-infrared (MIR) region**: **4000 cm⁻¹ to 400 cm⁻¹** (wavelength 2.5 μm to 25 μm).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Fingerprint Reader"

Imagine you have a **piano** (the IR source emitting many musical notes simultaneously). Each key on the piano corresponds to a specific frequency of infrared light. Now imagine a room full of **glass wine glasses** (your sample molecules), each tuned to ring only when a specific note is played.

If you strike ALL piano keys at once, you get a **noise-like rumble** (this is the **interferogram**). You cannot tell which glasses are present by simply listening to the rumble. However, if you record the rumble very precisely with a microphone and then run it through a computer program that decomposes the sound into individual notes, you instantly know which glasses (functional groups) are in the room, by exactly which notes they absorbed.

**The interferogram is the recorded rumble. The Fourier Transform is the program that separates the rumble into individual notes. The result is the molecular fingerprint.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Dispersive IR spectrometers (older generation) scan one wavelength at a time. FTIR records ALL infrared wavelengths simultaneously — this is the **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage**, making FTIR **~100× faster** with a vastly superior **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (S/N)**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | **2.998 × 10⁸ m·s⁻¹** |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J·K⁻¹** |
| IR spectral range (MIR) | $\tilde{\nu}$ | **4000 – 400 cm⁻¹** |
| Typical spectral resolution | $\Delta\tilde{\nu}$ | **0.5 – 4 cm⁻¹** |
| Beam splitter material (MIR) | — | **Germanium-coated KBr** or KBr on CsI |
| Detector (MIR) | — | **Deuterated Triglycine Sulfate (DTGS)** or **Mercury Cadmium Telluride (MCT)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Broadband IR Source Emission:**
A Globar (silicon carbide) or Nernst glower emits broadband polychromatic IR radiation covering 4000–400 cm⁻¹. This beam is directed toward the **Michelson Interferometer**.

**Step 2 — Michelson Interferometer Operation:**
The beam hits a **beam splitter** (typically a thin film of Ge on KBr), which transmits 50% of the beam to a **fixed mirror** and reflects 50% to a **moving mirror**. After reflection, the two beams recombine at the beam splitter, producing **constructive or destructive interference** depending on the **optical path difference (OPD)** $\delta$.

**Step 3 — Interferogram Formation:**
The recombined beam passes through the **sample compartment**. The sample absorbs specific IR frequencies corresponding to vibrational transitions. The transmitted intensity, recorded as a function of the mirror displacement $\delta$, is the **interferogram** $I(\delta)$. For a single-frequency source of wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}_0$, the interferogram is a pure cosine:

$$I(\delta) = B(\tilde{\nu}_0) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta)$$

**Step 4 — Mathematical Fourier Transformation:**
For a broadband source, the measured interferogram is the sum (integral) of all cosine contributions. A computer applies the **inverse Fourier Transform** to recover the spectrum:

$$B(\tilde{\nu}) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$

**Step 5 — Background Subtraction:**
A reference interferogram is recorded without the sample (background, $I_0$). The final transmittance/absorbance spectrum is computed after ratioing:

$$T(\tilde{\nu}) = \frac{I_{\text{sample}}(\tilde{\nu})}{I_0(\tilde{\nu})}, \qquad A(\tilde{\nu}) = -\log_{10} T(\tilde{\nu})$$

**Step 6 — Functional Group Interpretation:**
Each dip (absorption band) in the transmittance spectrum corresponds to a specific molecular vibration. By matching observed bands against **IR correlation charts**, functional groups (and thus molecular identity) are determined.

### 2.2 Why FTIR Works — The Underlying Physics

Molecules absorb IR radiation when **two conditions** are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Frequency condition:** The IR photon energy matches the energy difference between two vibrational states:
$$E_{\text{photon}} = h c \tilde{\nu} = \Delta E_{\text{vib}}$$

2. **Selection rule:** The vibration must cause a **change in the dipole moment** of the molecule ($\dfrac{d\mu}{dx} \neq 0$). Symmetric homonuclear diatomics (e.g., N₂, O₂, H₂) are therefore IR-inactive.

### 2.3 Advantages of FTIR Over Dispersive IR (KTU Favourite Question)

| Feature | Dispersive IR | FTIR |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput (Jaquinot) | Low | **High** (~75× more) |
| Speed (Fellgett) | Slow | **Fast** (~100×) |
| Signal-to-Noise ratio | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Wavelength accuracy | Requires external calibration | **Connes advantage** (built-in He-Ne laser reference) |
| Mechanical complexity | Grating/prism rotation | Only one moving mirror |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I(\delta) = B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta)$ | Single-frequency interferogram | — |
| 2 | $I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\tilde{\nu}$ | General broadband interferogram (cosine FT pair) | — |
| 3 | $B(\tilde{\nu}) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$ | Fourier transform (spectrum recovery) | — |
| 4 | $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\delta_{\max}}$ | Spectral resolution (depends on max mirror travel) | cm⁻¹ |
| 5 | $A(\tilde{\nu}) = \varepsilon(\tilde{\nu}) \, c \, l$ | Beer–Lambert Law (quantitative analysis) | — (absorbance is dimensionless) |
| 6 | $A(\tilde{\nu}) = -\log_{10} T(\tilde{\nu}) = \log_{10} \!\left(\!\dfrac{I_0}{I}\!\right)$ | Definition of absorbance | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T(\tilde{\nu}) \times 100\% = \!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\%$ | Transmittance (in %) | % |
| 8 | $\nu = c \tilde{\nu}$ | Conversion: wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$ (cm⁻¹) ↔ frequency $\nu$ (Hz) | Hz |
| 9 | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Wavenumber definition | cm⁻¹ |
| 10 | $I'(\delta) = I(\delta) \cdot A(\delta)$ | Apodization (multiplicative weighting to suppress sidelobes) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Apodization Note:** The function $A(\delta)$ decays smoothly from 1 at $\delta=0$ to 0 at $\delta=\delta_{\max}$. Common choices: **Happ–Genzel**, **Norton–Beer (weak/medium/strong)**, **Blackman–Harris**, and **Triangular**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Nanotechnology Utility

- **Nanoparticle surface functionalization:** Verifying that citrate or PVP capping agents are bound to Au/Ag nanoparticles via C=O stretch at ~1700 cm⁻¹ or N–H bend at ~1560 cm⁻¹.
- **Graphene Oxide (GO) characterization:** Identifying O–H (~3400 cm⁻¹), C=O (~1730 cm⁻¹), C=C aromatic (~1620 cm⁻¹), and C–O (~1050 cm⁻¹).
- **CNT purification:** Comparing pristine CNT (no –OH band) versus acid-oxidized CNT (broad O–H at 3400 cm⁻¹).
- **Polymer nanocomposite curing:** Monitoring the disappearance of C=C (~1640 cm⁻¹) during thermoset curing.
- **Pharmaceutical QC:** Confirming polymorph identity of nano-drug formulations.
- **Forensic & environmental:** Identifying microplastics and airborne nanoparticles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Interferogram for a Monochromatic Source

**Starting setup:** A Michelson interferometer splits a beam of light of wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}_0$ into two beams. One reflects from a fixed mirror (distance $L_1$ from beam splitter), the other from a movable mirror (distance $L_2$). The two beams recombine at the beam splitter with an **optical path difference**:

$$\delta = 2(L_2 - L_1) = 2 \Delta x$$

where $\Delta x$ is the mirror displacement from the zero-path-difference (ZPD) position.

**Step 1 — Recombined beam intensity:**
At the beam splitter, the recombined beam has intensity given by the interference of the two reflected contributions. Using the principle of superposition, the resulting amplitude is proportional to:

$$E(\delta) \propto E_0 \!\left[e^{i 2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 L_1} + e^{i 2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 L_2}\right]$$

Factoring out a common phase:

$$E(\delta) \propto E_0 \, e^{i 2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 L_1} \!\left[1 + e^{i 2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta/2}\right]$$

Wait — the path-difference phase term is $2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta$ (the factor of 2 comes because the beam travels the path difference twice). Thus:

$$E(\delta) \propto E_0 \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta / 2 + \phi)$$

The intensity (time-averaged) is:

$$I(\delta) = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert E(\delta) \vert^2 = B(\tilde{\nu}_0) \cos^2(\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity** $\cos^2(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}[1 + \cos(2\theta)]$:

$$I(\delta) = \tfrac{1}{2} B(\tilde{\nu}_0) \bigl[1 + \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Remove the DC offset** (only the AC component is Fourier-transformed, since the constant offset is removed during background subtraction):

$$\boxed{I(\delta) = B(\tilde{\nu}_0) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta)}$$

> **Logical Reasoning:** This is the single-frequency interferogram. Note that the signal is **maximum** when $\delta = n / \tilde{\nu}_0$ (constructive interference) and **zero** when $\delta = (2n+1)/(2\tilde{\nu}_0)$ (destructive interference). This produces the characteristic oscillating "wiggle pattern" centered at ZPD.

### 3.2 Derivation of the General Fourier Transform Pair

**Starting point:** For a polychromatic source containing a continuum of wavenumbers $\tilde{\nu}$ with spectral density $B(\tilde{\nu})$, the total interferogram is the linear superposition (integral) of single-frequency contributions:

$$I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\tilde{\nu}$$

This is the **Fourier Cosine Transform** of $B(\tilde{\nu})$. Since the interferogram is symmetric ($I(\delta) = I(-\delta)$), we can extend the lower limit to $-\infty$:

$$I(\delta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\tilde{\nu}$$

**Inverse operation:** To recover $B(\tilde{\nu})$, apply the inverse Fourier Cosine Transform:

$$B(\tilde{\nu}) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$

> **Dimensional reasoning:** $I(\delta)$ has units of intensity (W·m⁻²). $B(\tilde{\nu})$ has units of intensity per wavenumber (W·m⁻² per cm⁻¹). The integrals are dimensionless, so the units cancel correctly because $d\tilde{\nu}$ and $d\delta$ multiply to form a dimensionless quantity when normalized.

### 3.3 Derivation of Spectral Resolution

**Setup:** The moving mirror can travel only a finite distance $L$, so the maximum optical path difference is:

$$\delta_{\max} = 2L$$

**Step 1 — Truncation of the integral:**
Because the interferogram cannot be recorded beyond $\delta_{\max}$, the FT integral is truncated:

$$B(\tilde{\nu}) = \int_{-\delta_{\max}}^{+\delta_{\max}} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$

**Step 2 — Identify this as a rectangular window multiplication:**
The truncation is mathematically equivalent to multiplying the true (infinite-domain) interferogram by a **boxcar function** $\Pi(\delta)$:

$$\Pi(\delta) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert\delta\vert \le \delta_{\max} \\ 0, & \vert\delta\vert > \delta_{\max} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the convolution theorem:**
Multiplication in the $\delta$-domain corresponds to **convolution** in the $\tilde{\nu}$-domain. The FT of a rectangle is a **sinc function**:

$$\mathcal{F}\{\Pi(\delta)\} = 2 \delta_{\max} \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta_{\max}) = 2 \delta_{\max} \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta_{\max})}{2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta_{\max}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the resolution:**
The first zero of the sinc function occurs when $2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta_{\max} = \pi$, i.e., $\tilde{\nu} = 1/(2\delta_{\max})$. The **Rayleigh criterion** says two lines are resolved when separated by the distance from the peak to the first zero. This gives:

$$\boxed{\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{\delta_{\max}} = \frac{1}{2L}}$$

**Numerical example:** If the mirror travels $L = 1 \text{ cm}$, the resolution is $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = 0.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (high resolution). For routine scans, $L = 0.05 \text{ cm}$ gives $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = 10 \text{ cm}^{-1}$.

### 3.4 Apparent Beam-Splitter Efficiency and Throughput (Jaquinot Advantage)

**Setup:** A circular aperture of area $A$ is placed at the interferometer output, accepting a solid angle $\Omega$ of radiation.

**Step 1 — Étendue (throughput) of the interferometer:**

$$\Theta_{\text{FTIR}} = A \cdot \Omega \quad (\text{sr} \cdot \text{m}^2)$$

**Step 2 — For a dispersive spectrometer, the slit width $w$ must be tiny to achieve the same resolution:**

$$\Theta_{\text{disp}} = A_{\text{slit}} \cdot \Omega = w \cdot h \cdot \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**
The FTIR has ~50–200× larger throughput than a dispersive instrument of the same resolution, leading to dramatically higher S/N. This is the **Jaquinot advantage**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical FTIR Spectrometer

The diagram below shows the complete signal-flow path inside an FTIR system — from broadband IR source to the final absorbance spectrum displayed on the computer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["A: IR SOURCE\n(Globar SiC / Nernst)"] --> bs["B: BEAM SPLITTER\n(Ge coated on KBr)"]
    bs -- "50% transmit" --> fix["C: FIXED MIRROR"]
    bs -- "50% reflect" --> mov["D: MOVING MIRROR\n(Linear translation L)"]
    fix --> bs
    mov --> bs
    bs --> samp["E: SAMPLE COMPARTMENT\n(KBr pellet / ATR crystal / thin film)"]
    samp --> det["F: IR DETECTOR\n(DTGS or MCT)"]
    det --> adc["G: ANALOG TO DIGITAL\nCONVERTER ADC"]
    adc --> heNe["H: He-Ne LASER REFERENCE\n(Connes Advantage 15798 cm-1)"]
    heNe --> cpu["I: COMPUTER\nFourier Transform + Background Ratio"]
    cpu --> disp["J: DISPLAY\nTransmittance vs Wavenumber"]

    mov -. "Computer controlled\nstepper motor" .-> cpu
    cpu -. "Trigger & clock signal" .-> adc
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Michelson Interferometer (Optical Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s["S: IR Source\n(Polychromatic)"] -->|"collimated beam"| bs2["BS: Beam Splitter\nat 45 degrees"]
    bs2 -->|"transmitted\nbeam"| fm["FM: Fixed Mirror\ndistance L1"]
    bs2 -->|"reflected\nbeam"| mm["MM: Movable Mirror\ndistance L2 from BS"]
    fm -->|"phase 2 pi nu L1"| bs2
    mm -->|"phase 2 pi nu L2"| bs2
    bs2 -->|"recombined\ninterfering beams"| c["C: Collimator lens"]
    c --> sp["Sample"]
    sp --> d["Detector"]

    mm -.->|"delta = 2 L2 minus 2 L1\noptical path difference"| mm
```

### 4.3 Logical Flow of Data Reduction (Interferogram → Spectrum)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["P1: Record SAMPLE interferogram\nI_s delta"] --> p2["P2: Fourier Transform of I_s delta"]
    p3["P3: Record BACKGROUND interferogram\nI_0 delta (no sample)"] --> p4["P4: Fourier Transform of I_0 delta"]
    p2 --> p5["P5: Compute ratio\nT nu = I_s nu / I_0 nu"]
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> p6["P6: Compute absorbance\nA nu = minus log10 T nu"]
    p6 --> p7["P7: Apply APODIZATION\nto reduce spectral artifacts"]
    p7 --> p8["P8: Apply PHASE CORRECTION\nMertz or Forman method"]
    p8 --> p9["P9: Display FINAL FTIR SPECTRUM\nAbsorbance vs Wavenumber"]
    p9 --> p10["P10: INTERPRET using\nIR correlation chart"]
```

### 4.4 Signal Conditioning Sub-Systems (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    opd1["OPD Sub-system:\nMoving Mirror on\nLinear Air Bearing"] --> interf["Interferogram\nOutput"]
    bs1["Beam Splitter\nCompensation Plate\n(blocks ghost fringes)"] --> interf
    interf --> amp["Low-Noise\nPreamplifier"]
    amp --> filt["Electronic\nBandpass Filter\n0.1 to 10 kHz"]
    filt --> dig["ADC sampling\ntriggered by He-Ne fringes"]
    dig --> mem["Buffer Memory\nin Computer"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **He-Ne laser (632.8 nm = 15798 cm⁻¹)** fringe pattern provides an **internal wavelength calibration standard** — this is the **Connes Advantage**, ensuring that every FTIR has a self-calibrating, ultra-precise wavenumber axis with absolute accuracy of ±0.01 cm⁻¹.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). State any three advantages of FTIR over conventional dispersive IR spectroscopy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** FTIR is a vibrational spectroscopic technique that uses a **Michelson interferometer** to modulate broadband infrared radiation, and a **mathematical Fourier Transform** to convert the resulting time-domain **interferogram** into a frequency-domain infrared spectrum, used for identifying molecular functional groups and chemical bonds in a sample.

**Three Advantages (3 × ½ mark = 1½ marks):**

1. **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage** — measures all wavenumbers simultaneously, giving ~100× faster acquisition.
2. **Jaquinot (Throughput) Advantage** — large circular aperture gives ~50× higher throughput, improving S/N.
3. **Connes Advantage** — built-in He-Ne laser gives highly accurate, self-calibrating wavenumber scale (±0.01 cm⁻¹).

*Total: 3 marks*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Michelson Interferometer used in FTIR.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Michelson Interferometer** (1½ marks) consists of:

- A **beam splitter** (Ge-coated KBr) that divides the incoming IR beam into two equal-intensity beams.
- A **fixed mirror** and a **movable mirror** that reflect the two beams back to the beam splitter.
- The recombined beam produces **constructive or destructive interference** depending on the **optical path difference** $\delta = 2(L_2 - L_1)$ (1½ marks).

As the moving mirror scans, the detector records an **interferogram** $I(\delta)$ that contains information about all IR frequencies. A Fourier Transform recovers the spectrum $B(\tilde{\nu})$.

*Total: 3 marks*

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** Derive the mathematical relationship between the interferogram $I(\delta)$ and the spectrum $B(\tilde{\nu})$ in an FTIR experiment. Clearly state the forward and inverse Fourier Transform equations used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Beer–Lambert Law** as applied in quantitative FTIR analysis. A 0.5 mm thick polymer film shows an absorbance of 0.85 at 1730 cm⁻¹. The molar extinction coefficient at this wavenumber is $\varepsilon = 450 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$. Calculate the concentration of the absorbing species. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider an IR source emitting a continuum of wavenumbers. Each wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$ contributes an intensity $B(\tilde{\nu}) d\tilde{\nu}$ within an interval $d\tilde{\nu}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** In the Michelson interferometer, a beam of wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$ produces an interferogram that is a cosine function of the optical path difference $\delta$:

$$I_{\tilde{\nu}}(\delta) = B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** By the **superposition principle**, the total interferogram from the broadband source is the integral of all contributions:

$$I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\tilde{\nu}$$

This is the **Fourier Cosine Transform** of $B(\tilde{\nu})$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Since the interferogram is an even function ($I(\delta) = I(-\delta)$), the limits can be extended symmetrically:

$$I(\delta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\tilde{\nu}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Applying the **inverse Fourier Cosine Transform**, the spectrum is recovered:

$$B(\tilde{\nu}) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** This pair of equations constitutes the mathematical heart of FTIR. In a real instrument, only a finite interferogram is recorded up to $\delta_{\max}$, so apodization is applied to suppress spectral artifacts.

---

**Part (b) — Beer–Lambert Law Application (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Statement of Beer–Lambert Law:**
The absorbance $A$ of a sample at a given wavenumber is directly proportional to the **concentration** $c$ of the absorbing species, the **path length** $l$, and the **molar extinction coefficient** $\varepsilon$:

$$A(\tilde{\nu}) = \varepsilon(\tilde{\nu}) \, c \, l$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Given data:**
- Absorbance: $A = 0.85$
- Path length: $l = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ cm}$
- Extinction coefficient: $\varepsilon = 450 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Rearrange for concentration:**
$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \, l}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Substitute values:**
$$c = \frac{0.85}{450 \, \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1} \times 0.05 \, \text{cm}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute denominator:**
$$450 \times 0.05 = 22.5 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Final result:**
$$c = \frac{0.85}{22.5} = 0.03778 \text{ mol·L}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Express with proper significant figures:**
$$\boxed{c \approx 3.78 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol·L}^{-1} \;\; \text{(i.e., 37.8 mM)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget to **convert mm to cm** for path length. If you keep $l = 0.5$ mm = 0.5 cm without conversion, you will get an answer **10× too small**, costing you at least 1–2 marks. Always check unit consistency in FTIR Beer's Law problems.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]* (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe the principle and construction of an FTIR spectrometer with the help of a labelled block diagram. List the major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A moving mirror in a Michelson interferometer travels a maximum distance of 1.25 cm. Calculate the spectral resolution of the instrument. Justify why this resolution is sufficient (or insufficient) for distinguishing the C=O stretching bands of two carbonyl compounds at 1715 cm⁻¹ and 1710 cm⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — FTIR Spectrometer Block Diagram & Components (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):** A well-labelled block diagram showing: **IR Source → Michelson Interferometer (Beam Splitter, Fixed Mirror, Moving Mirror) → Sample Compartment → Detector → ADC → Computer → Display.**

**Major Components & Functions (4 × 1 = 4 marks):**

1. **IR Source (Globar SiC, ~1500 K):** Emits broadband continuous IR radiation covering 4000–400 cm⁻¹. **(1 mark)**
2. **Beam Splitter (Ge on KBr):** Splits the IR beam into two equal halves — one transmitted, one reflected. KBr is transparent in the MIR region. **(1 mark)**
3. **Moving Mirror:** Translates linearly to vary the optical path difference $\delta$, generating the time-varying interferogram. **(1 mark)**
4. **Detector (DTGS at room temp, or MCT liquid-N₂ cooled):** Converts the IR signal into an electrical voltage proportional to intensity. **(1 mark)**

Additional components: **He-Ne laser** (internal wavenumber calibration), **ADC** (digitizes interferogram), **Computer** (performs FT, phase correction, apodization, baseline correction).

---

**Part (b) — Resolution Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Recall the resolution formula:**
$$\Delta\tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{\delta_{\max}} = \frac{1}{2L}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Given data:**
$L = 1.25 \text{ cm}$, so $\delta_{\max} = 2 \times 1.25 = 2.5 \text{ cm}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Substitute:**
$$\Delta\tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{2.5 \text{ cm}} = 0.4 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Result:**
$$\boxed{\Delta\tilde{\nu} = 0.4 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compare with band separation:**
The two C=O bands are separated by:

$$\Delta\tilde{\nu}_{\text{sep}} = 1715 - 1710 = 5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Apply the Rayleigh Criterion:**
Two bands are resolvable if their separation is **greater than** the instrumental resolution:
$$\Delta\tilde{\nu}_{\text{sep}} = 5 \text{ cm}^{-1} \;\; > \;\; \Delta\tilde{\nu} = 0.4 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
The resolution of 0.4 cm⁻¹ is **12.5× better (smaller)** than the band separation of 5 cm⁻¹. Therefore, the instrument **CAN easily resolve** the two carbonyl C=O stretching bands. This is a high-resolution FTIR suitable for fine spectral discrimination.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. Using $L$ instead of $2L$ in the formula — this gives **half the correct resolution**, losing 2 marks.
> 2. Forgetting to compare $\Delta\tilde{\nu}$ with band separation. The Rayleigh criterion is the key conceptual point.
> 3. Concluding "Yes, can resolve" without showing the **ratio** $5 / 0.4 = 12.5$. Always show the explicit comparison.

---

### 5.3 Quick-Reference IR Correlation Chart (FTIR Interpretation Aid)

| Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | Vibration Type | Functional Group |
|---|---|---|
| 3600 – 3200 (broad) | O–H stretch | Alcohols, carboxylic acids, water |
| 3500 – 3300 | N–H stretch | Primary/secondary amines, amides |
| 3300 – 2700 (broad) | O–H (chelated) | Strong H-bonded carboxylic acid |
| 3100 – 3000 | =C–H stretch | Aromatic, alkene |
| 3000 – 2850 | C–H stretch (sp³) | Alkanes |
| 2260 – 2220 | C≡C, C≡N stretch | Alkynes, nitriles |
| 1740 – 1715 | C=O stretch | Carbonyl (ketone/aldehyde/ester) |
| 1700 – 1680 | C=O stretch | Amide I band |
| 1650 – 1600 | C=C stretch | Alkenes, aromatic |
| 1600, 1500 | Ring stretch | Aromatic C=C |
| 1560, 1410 | COO⁻ asymmetric/symmetric | Carboxylate ion |
| 1300 – 1000 | C–O stretch | Alcohols, ethers, esters |
| 1100 – 1000 | Si–O stretch | Siloxanes, silicates, silica NPs |
| < 1000 | C–H out-of-plane bend | Aromatic substitution pattern |

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for FTIR (Module 3):**

- [x] FTIR = **Michelson Interferometer + Fourier Transform** of broadband IR radiation.
- [x] The **interferogram** $I(\delta)$ is recorded in the **time (or path-difference) domain**; the **spectrum** $B(\tilde{\nu})$ is recovered in the **frequency (wavenumber) domain**.
- [x] FT pair: $I(\delta) = \int B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta) d\tilde{\nu}$ and $B(\tilde{\nu}) = \int I(\delta) \cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta) d\delta$.
- [x] MIR spectral range = **4000 to 400 cm⁻¹**.
- [x] **Beer–Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon c l$ — used for **quantitative** FTIR analysis.
- [x] **Resolution formula:** $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = 1/\delta_{\max} = 1/(2L)$, where $L$ is mirror travel.
- [x] **Apodization** is needed because interferograms are finite (truncated); it suppresses sidelobes near sharp absorption bands.
- [x] **Three major FTIR advantages** (Fellgett, Jaquinot, Connes) are board-exam favourites.
- [x] **Two IR activity conditions:** (i) photon energy matches vibrational energy gap; (ii) vibration changes the **dipole moment** ($\partial\mu/\partial x \neq 0$).
- [x] **Sample preparation methods:** KBr pellet (solid), thin film (polymer), liquid cell (NaCl windows), ATR (Attenuated Total Reflection) for solids/pastes/powders without preparation.
- [x] **Characteristic IR bands** to remember: O–H (~3400), N–H (~3300), C–H (~2900), C≡N (~2200), C=O (~1700), C=C (~1600), Si–O (~1100), C–O (~1050).
- [x] **He-Ne laser** at 632.8 nm is the internal reference providing the Connes advantage.
- [x] **Detector choices:** DTGS (room temp, broad range) and MCT (cooled, faster, more sensitive).
- [x] **Phase correction** (Mertz or Forman) compensates for asymmetric mirror velocity and electronic delays.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of X-Ray Diffraction

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)** is a non-destructive analytical technique used to characterize the crystallographic structure, phase composition, lattice parameters, and crystallite size of materials. It is based on the constructive interference of monochromatic X-rays scattered by the periodic arrangement of atoms in a crystal lattice, governed by **Bragg's Law** ($n\lambda = 2d_{hkl}\sin\theta$). In the context of **nanomaterials and nanotechnology**, XRD is the primary tool for confirming the crystalline nature of nanoparticles, identifying polymorphs, and estimating the average crystallite size using the **Scherrer equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board Favourite):**
> XRD is an elastic scattering technique where incident X-rays of known wavelength ($\lambda$) interact with the atomic planes (separated by interplanar spacing $d_{hkl}$) of a crystalline sample, producing constructive interference at specific angles ($2\theta$) determined by Bragg's Law.

---

## 1.2 Properties of X-Rays Used in XRD

X-rays are a form of **electromagnetic radiation** with the following characteristics critical for KTU examinations:

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Wavelength ($\lambda$) for Cu K$\alpha$ | **1.5406 Å** (1.5406 × 10⁻¹⁰ m) |
| Energy range | **0.1 – 100 keV** |
| Speed | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s (in vacuum) |
| Penetration depth in solids | **1 – 1000 µm** |
| Generation | Bombardment of metal target (Cu, Mo, Co) with high-energy electrons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme labs, **Cu K$\alpha$ radiation** ($\lambda = 1.5406$ Å) is the standard. The K$\alpha$ line arises from the $L \rightarrow K$ shell electron transition in copper, filtered using a Ni filter to suppress K$\beta$ radiation.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "Fingerprint of Crystals"

Imagine you have a row of evenly spaced trees lining a road. If you clap your hands while standing far away, the sound bounces back to you as a weak echo. But if you clap from a specific **angle**, the echo suddenly becomes loud and clear — this happens because sound waves from many trees arrive at your ear **in phase (constructively)**.

**XRD works exactly the same way**, but with **X-ray waves** and **atomic planes** instead of sound and trees:

- The **atomic planes** in a crystal act as the "trees" (periodic scatterers).
- The **incident X-ray beam** is the "clap".
- The **detector** is the "ear".
- The specific **angle $2\theta$** at which the detector records a strong signal is the "loud echo" — confirming that X-rays from successive planes travelled a whole number of wavelengths out of sync and **interfered constructively**.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as fingerprints uniquely identify a person, every crystalline material has a **unique XRD pattern** (set of $2\theta$ peaks and intensities). This is why XRD is the gold standard for **phase identification** in nanomaterial research.

---

## 1.4 Why XRD is Indispensable for Nanomaterials

Nanoparticles (size 1–100 nm) exhibit properties that depend critically on their **crystal structure and size**. XRD simultaneously provides:

1. **Phase identification** — confirms if the nanomaterial is a specific polymorph (e.g., anatase vs. rutile TiO₂).
2. **Crystallite size** — via the **Scherrer equation**, given the broadening of diffraction peaks.
3. **Lattice strain** — from peak shift and additional broadening (Williamson–Hall method).
4. **Lattice parameters** — refined using Rietveld analysis or analytical methods.
5. **Degree of crystallinity** — ratio of sharp peaks to broad amorphous humps.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants Required for KTU XRD Numerical Problems:**
> - Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - Cu K$\alpha$ wavelength $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å $= 0.15406$ nm
> - Scherrer constant $K = 0.9$ (for spherical crystallites, dimensionless)
> - $1$ Å $= 10^{-10}$ m $= 0.1$ nm
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Bragg's Law

When an X-ray beam strikes a crystal, each atom in the lattice scatters the X-rays **elastically** (Thomson scattering). The scattered waves from millions of atoms interfere. For **constructive interference** (a strong diffraction peak), the path difference between X-rays reflected from successive parallel atomic planes must equal an integer multiple of the wavelength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bragg's Law (Master Equation of XRD):**
> $$n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$
> where:
> - $n$ = order of diffraction (integer: 1, 2, 3, ...)
> - $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident X-ray (Å)
> - $d_{hkl}$ = interplanar spacing for planes with Miller indices $(hkl)$ (Å)
> - $\theta$ = angle of incidence (Bragg angle) in degrees

---

## 2.2 Why Bragg's Law Works — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup:**
Two parallel atomic planes, separated by perpendicular distance $d_{hkl}$, are illuminated by parallel X-ray beams 1 and 2 at glancing angle $\theta$.

**Step 2 — Path Difference Calculation:**
The lower ray travels an extra distance equal to the sum of segments $(MX + XN)$ inside the crystal.

By basic trigonometry on the right triangles formed:
$$MX = XN = d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$

Hence the total path difference is:
$$\Delta = MX + XN = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Constructive Interference Condition:**
For waves to add constructively, the path difference must equal a whole number of wavelengths:
$$2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta = n\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Physical Meaning:**
- For a **fixed $\lambda$**, only those $d_{hkl}$ values that satisfy the equation (for some $\theta$ between 0° and 90°) will produce a peak.
- A crystal acts as a **3D diffraction grating** — each set of planes $(hkl)$ produces a peak at a unique $2\theta$.

**Step 5 — Limits of Bragg's Law:**
- Maximum $\theta = 90°$, so $d_{hkl}^{\min} = \lambda/2$ — X-rays with $\lambda = 1.54$ Å can detect planes spaced down to ~0.77 Å.
- For planes where $d_{hkl} < \lambda/2$, no diffraction occurs (angle becomes imaginary).

---

## 2.3 Miller Indices (hkl) — Identifying Atomic Planes

Miller indices are a notation system to label planes and directions in a crystal. To find Miller indices of a plane:

1. Identify the **intercepts** of the plane with the crystallographic axes ($a$, $b$, $c$) in units of lattice parameters.
2. Take the **reciprocals** of these intercepts.
3. **Clear fractions** by multiplying by the LCM.
4. Enclose in parentheses: $(h\ k\ l)$.

**Example:** A plane with intercepts $a/2$, $b$, $c$ has reciprocals $2, 1, 1$ → Miller index **(2 1 1)**.

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $(hkl)$ | A single plane (e.g., (111)) |
| $\{hkl\}$ | Family of equivalent planes (e.g., {100} includes (100), (010), (001), etc.) |
| $[hkl]$ | A crystallographic direction |
| $\langle hkl \rangle$ | Family of equivalent directions |

---

## 2.4 Interplanar Spacing ($d_{hkl}$) for Different Crystal Systems

For a **cubic crystal** with lattice parameter $a$:

$$\frac{1}{d_{hkl}^2} = \frac{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}{a^2}$$

For **tetragonal** ($a = b \neq c$):
$$\frac{1}{d_{hkl}^2} = \frac{h^2 + k^2}{a^2} + \frac{l^2}{c^2}$$

For **hexagonal** ($a = b \neq c$, $\gamma = 120°$):
$$\frac{1}{d_{hkl}^2} = \frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{h^2 + hk + k^2}{a^2}\right) + \frac{l^2}{c^2}$$

---

## 2.5 The Scherrer Equation — Estimating Crystallite Size

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a nanomaterial has small crystallites (< 100 nm), the diffraction peaks **broaden**. This broadening $\beta$ (in radians) is related to crystallite size by the **Scherrer equation**:
> $$D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$$
> where $D$ = average crystallite size (nm), $K$ = shape factor (≈ 0.9), $\beta$ = full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak (radians), $\theta$ = Bragg angle.

- **Instrumental broadening** must be subtracted: $\beta_{sample}^2 = \beta_{observed}^2 - \beta_{instrument}^2$
- **Strain broadening** also contributes; the Williamson–Hall method separates size and strain effects.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — XRD

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Calculate peak position | Å, degrees |
| 2 | $1/d^2 = (h^2+k^2+l^2)/a^2$ | d-spacing (cubic) | Å, Å |
| 3 | $\sin^2\theta = (\lambda^2/4a^2)(h^2+k^2+l^2)$ | Indexing peaks | dimensionless |
| 4 | $D = K\lambda/(\beta\cos\theta)$ | Crystallite size | nm |
| 5 | $\beta_{total}^2 = \beta_{size}^2 + \beta_{strain}^2$ | Williamson-Hall | rad² |
| 6 | $\epsilon = \beta/(4\tan\theta)$ | Lattice strain | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E = hc/\lambda$ | X-ray energy | J or keV |
| 8 | $\rho_{X-ray} = ZM/(N_A V)$ | X-ray density | g/cm³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** XRD is used in semiconductor fabs to characterize Si wafers, in pharmaceuticals to verify polymorphic forms of drugs, in metallurgy to identify phases in alloys (e.g., $\alpha$-Fe vs. $\gamma$-Fe in steel), and in catalysis to confirm crystal structure of nanopowder catalysts.

---

## 2.7 Instrumentation: The Bragg-Brentano Diffractometer

The modern powder XRD uses the **Bragg-Brentano geometry ($\theta - 2\theta$ configuration)**:

- **X-ray tube** (source): Cu target, operated at 30–40 kV, 20–30 mA.
- **Incident-beam optics**: Soller slits, divergence slit, monochromator (Ni filter or crystal).
- **Specimen**: Flat powder sample, spun to improve statistics.
- **Receiving optics**: Receiving slit, anti-scatter slit, detector (scintillation counter or solid-state Si(Li)).
