STM-based atomic manipulation is not merely a laboratory curiosity:

- **Quantum Corrals**: Don Eigler (1993) built a ring of 48 Fe atoms on Cu(111) that confined surface-state electrons into standing-wave patterns (direct visualization of quantum mechanical wavefunctions).
- **Atomic Logic Gates**: Prototype single-atom memory cells in atomic-scale logic.
- **Molecular Machines**: Single-molecule rotors, gears, and switches.
- **Defect Engineering**: Deliberate placement of dopant atoms in semiconductor surfaces.
- **Surface Chemistry**: Studying single-bond formation/breaking events.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Decay Constant $\kappa$ from the Schrödinger Equation

Consider a 1-D potential barrier of height $\phi$ (work function) and width $d$. The time-independent Schrödinger equation inside the barrier is:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e} \frac{d^2 \psi}{dz^2} + \phi \cdot \psi = E \cdot \psi$$

**Step 1: Rearrange the equation**

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dz^2} = \frac{2m_e(\phi - E)}{\hbar^2} \cdot \psi$$

**Step 2: For a tunneling electron, $E < \phi$** (the electron's energy is below the barrier top). Define:

$$\kappa^2 \equiv \frac{2m_e(\phi - E)}{\hbar^2}$$

**Step 3: Write the equation in standard exponential form**

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dz^2} = \kappa^2 \psi$$

**Step 4: The general solution inside the barrier is therefore**

$$\psi(z) = A \cdot e^{-\kappa z} + B \cdot e^{+\kappa z}$$

**Step 5: Apply the physical boundary condition** that $\psi$ must not diverge as $z \to \infty$ inside the barrier. Hence $B = 0$, and we are left with:

$$\psi(z) = A \cdot e^{-\kappa z}$$

**Step 6: The tunneling probability (proportional to $\vert \psi \vert^2$) is**

$$P_{tunnel} \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$$

**Step 7: Since current $\propto$ probability of electron reaching the other side, the tunneling current becomes**

$$I_t = I_0 \cdot e^{-2\kappa d} = I_0 \cdot e^{-2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2m_e(\phi - E)}}{\hbar} \cdot d}$$

**Step 8: For low bias $V$** such that $E \approx 0$ in the simplest picture, $E \ll \phi$, so $\phi - E \approx \phi$, yielding:

$$I_t = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{2\sqrt{2m_e \phi}}{\hbar} \cdot d\right)$$

This is the foundational equation underlying all STM operation and manipulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $2\kappa$ and not $\kappa$?** The current is proportional to the *probability density* $\vert \psi \vert^2$, not $\vert \psi \vert$ itself. Since $\psi \propto e^{-\kappa d}$, squaring gives $e^{-2\kappa d}$. Many textbooks use $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e\phi}/\hbar$ where the factor 2 is absorbed, leading to $I_t = I_0 e^{-\kappa d}$. Both notations are equivalent — verify the convention used in the question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Estimation of $\kappa$ and Current Sensitivity

**Given:**
- Work function: $\phi = 4.0\ \text{eV} = 4.0 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 6.408 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$
- $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$
- $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J}\cdot \text{s}$

**Step 1: Calculate the numerator under the square root**

$$2 m_e \phi = 2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 6.408 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$= 11.674 \times 10^{-49} = 1.167 \times 10^{-48}\ \text{J}\cdot \text{kg}$$

**Step 2: Take the square root**

$$\sqrt{1.167 \times 10^{-48}} = 1.080 \times 10^{-24}\ \text{J}\cdot \text{kg}^{1/2}$$

**Step 3: Divide by $\hbar$**

$$\kappa = \frac{1.080 \times 10^{-24}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}} = 1.024 \times 10^{10}\ \text{m}^{-1} = 1.024\ \text{Å}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the percentage change in current for $\Delta d = 0.1\ \text{Å}$ change in gap**

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = e^{\kappa \cdot \Delta d} - 1 = e^{1.024 \times 0.1} - 1 = e^{0.1024} - 1$$

$$= 1.1078 - 1 = 0.1078 \approx 10.8\%$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A change of just **0.1 Å (0.01 nm)** in tip height causes an ~**11% change in tunneling current**. This is why the STM can resolve individual atomic steps and why it is such a powerful manipulation tool — tiny vertical motions translate to large, easily measurable signals.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lateral Manipulation Energy Threshold

For lateral manipulation, the energy barrier to move an adatom is the **Ehrlich–Schwoebel barrier** $E_b$. The probability of the atom hopping per unit time is:

$$\nu = \nu_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_b}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $\nu_0 \approx 10^{13}\ \text{Hz}$ is the attempt frequency, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ is substrate temperature.

**For the manipulation to be reliable, we require $\nu \ll 1\ \text{Hz}$** (i.e., the atom stays put when not manipulated). This means:

$$\frac{E_b}{k_B T} \gg \ln(\nu_0) \approx 30$$

**At $T = 4\ \text{K}$ (liquid helium temperature):**

$$E_b \gg 30 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 4 = 1.66 \times 10^{-21}\ \text{J} \approx 10\ \text{meV}$$

This is why cryogenic temperatures are essential for stable atomic manipulation — thermal fluctuations at room temperature would cause adatom diffusion, destroying any deliberately built structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The IBM experiments on Xe/Ni(110) were performed at **4 K** under UHV (ultra-high vacuum, $\sim 10^{-10}$ mbar) to ensure both stability of the atoms and cleanliness of the surface.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Physical atomic-resolution drawings cannot be rendered as Mermaid graphs. Below are two functional architecture flow diagrams: (A) the STM manipulation sequence, and (B) the tip–atom–substrate force interaction block diagram.

## 4.1 STM Atomic Manipulation Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Sample Loaded in UHV Chamber] --> coolA[Cryogenically Cool to 4 K]
    coolA --> tipA[Prepare Atomically Sharp Tip via Field Evaporation]
    tipA --> imgA[Step 1: Acquire STM Topographic Image at I sub t approx 1 nA V bias approx 0.05 V]
    imgA --> identA[Step 2: Identify Target Atom Location in Image]
    identA --> posA[Step 3: Position Tip Above Target Atom Using Piezo XYZ Scanner]
    posA --> lowerA[Step 4: Lower Tip to Manipulation Height d sub manip approx 0.2 to 0.3 nm]
    lowerA --> disableA[Step 5: Disable Z Feedback Loop and Apply Constant Current Mode]
    disableA --> dragA[Step 6: Drag Tip Laterally Along Desired Path]
    dragA --> verifyA[Step 7: Withdraw Tip and Re image to Verify Atom Position]
    verifyA --> repeatA{Structure Complete?}
    repeatA -- No --> posA
    repeatA -- Yes --> endA[End: Atomic Structure Fabricated]
```

## 4.2 Tip–Atom–Substrate Force Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tipB[STM Tip Conductive Atomically Sharp] --> gapB[Tip Atom Vacuum Gap d]
    gapB --> surfB[Surface Adatom on Substrate]
    tipB --> f1B[Van der Waals Attraction F sub vdW]
    tipB --> f2B[Electrostatic Force F sub el]
    tipB --> f3B[Chemical Binding F sub chem]
    tipB --> f4B[Pauli Repulsion F sub Pauli]
    surfB --> f1B
    surfB --> f2B
    surfB --> f3B
    surfB --> f4B
    f1B --> sumB[Net Force F sub net]
    f2B --> sumB
    f3B --> sumB
    f4B --> sumB
    sumB --> modeB{Manipulation Mode}
    modeB -- F sub net attractive and small d --> lateralB[Lateral Pushing Sliding]
    modeB -- F sub net strong attractive --> verticalB[Vertical Pick and Place]
    modeB -- V pulse applied --> pulseB[Pulse Induced Bond Formation]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an STM Manipulation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    piezoA[XYZ Piezo Scanner nanometer precision] --> tipC[STM Tip Tungsten Pt Ir]
    sampleC[Conductive Sample on Stage] --> stageA[XY Positioner and Cryostat 4 K]
    tipC --> tunnelC[Tunneling Gap I sub t = I sub 0 exp negative kappa d]
    tunnelC --> preampC[Preamplifier 10 to the 9 gain]
    preampC --> fbC[Feedback Controller Maintains Constant I sub t]
    fbC --> piezoA
    ctrlC[Operator Computer DAC] --> biasC[Bias Voltage Source 0.01 to 2 V]
    biasC --> tipC
    ctrlC --> dacC[Manipulation Pulse Generator 0 to 5 V]
    dacC --> tipC
    ctrlC --> modeC[Mode Switch Imaging to Manipulation]
    modeC --> fbC
    fbC --> swC{Open or Closed Z Feedback}
    swC -- Open for manipulation --> piezoA
    swC -- Closed for imaging --> piezoA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operation Point:** During *imaging*, the Z-feedback loop is **closed** (constant current mode) and the tip rides above the surface contour. During *manipulation*, the Z-feedback is **opened** (or set to a very slow response) so the tip stays at a fixed, lowered height for direct mechanical interaction with the atom.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define STM-based atomic manipulation. Mention the historical significance of the IBM experiment with Xenon atoms on Nickel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

STM-based atomic manipulation is the technique of repositioning individual atoms on a surface using the atomically sharp tip of a Scanning Tunneling Microscope by exploiting precisely controlled tip–sample interaction forces. **(1 mark)**

In 1989, Don Eigler and Erhard Schweizer at IBM Almaden used a low-temperature STM (4 K, UHV) to arrange **35 Xenon atoms** on a Ni(110) surface to spell the letters "I-B-M." **(1 mark)**

This experiment was historically significant because it demonstrated for the first time that individual atoms could be precisely placed at will, opening the field of atomic-scale engineering and providing direct evidence that matter can be manipulated at the ultimate quantum limit. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Why is cryogenic temperature (4 K) essential for stable STM atomic manipulation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At room temperature, thermal energy $k_B T \approx 25\ \text{meV}$ is comparable to typical adatom binding energies ($10$–$100\ \text{meV}$), causing unwanted spontaneous diffusion of atoms. **(1 mark)**

At $T = 4\ \text{K}$, $k_B T \approx 0.34\ \text{meV}$, which is much smaller than the manipulation barrier, so atoms remain stationary unless deliberately dragged. **(1 mark)**

Additionally, cryogenic temperatures reduce thermal drift of the STM piezo scanner, suppress contamination, and sharpen the Fermi distribution of electrons — all of which improve positional accuracy to ~0.01 nm. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module – Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**A(i). [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the tunneling current in an STM and explain how its exponential dependence on tip–sample distance enables atomic-scale imaging and manipulation.

**A(ii). [7 marks]** Discuss the various modes of STM-based atomic manipulation (lateral, vertical, pulse-induced) with suitable examples and the role of tip–sample forces in each.

---

### **Model Answer for A(i):**

**Setting up the Schrödinger equation for the tunneling barrier:** Consider a 1-D rectangular potential barrier of height $\phi$ (work function) and width $d$ between tip and sample. Inside the barrier, the time-independent Schrödinger equation is: **(1 mark)**

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e} \frac{d^2 \psi}{dz^2} + \phi \psi = E \psi$$

Rearranging: $\dfrac{d^2 \psi}{dz^2} = \dfrac{2m_e(\phi - E)}{\hbar^2} \psi$. Since $E < \phi$ for a tunneling electron, define $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m_e(\phi - E)}}{\hbar}$. **(1 mark)**

**Solving the equation:** The general solution inside the barrier is $\psi(z) = A e^{-\kappa z} + B e^{+\kappa z}$. For physical acceptability, $B = 0$, giving $\psi(z) = A e^{-\kappa z}$. **(1 mark)**

**Tunneling probability:** The probability of finding the electron on the other side is $\vert \psi \vert^2 \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$. The tunneling current is proportional to this probability, so: **(1 mark)**

$$I_t = I_0 \exp(-2\kappa d) = I_0 \exp\left(-\frac{2\sqrt{2m_e \phi}}{\hbar} \cdot d\right)$$

(assuming $E \ll \phi$). **(1 mark)**

**Numerical sensitivity:** For $\phi = 4\ \text{eV}$, $\kappa \approx 1.024\ \text{Å}^{-1}$. A change of $0.1\ \text{Å}$ in $d$ produces ~11% change in $I_t$, making it possible to resolve individual atoms. **(1 mark)**

**Implication for manipulation:** This exponential sensitivity also means that small, controlled changes in tip height translate into large, reproducible changes in tip–atom interaction forces. By lowering the tip from imaging distance (~0.5 nm) to manipulation distance (~0.2 nm), the operator can switch from passive sensing to active mechanical contact with a single adatom. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Model Answer for A(ii):**

**(a) Lateral Manipulation (Sliding/Pushing):** The tip is lowered close to the surface (~$d = 0.2$ nm) and the Z-feedback is opened. The tip is then dragged laterally; the tip–atom attraction overcomes the adatom–substrate potential barrier, sliding the atom along with the tip. The IBM logo experiment (Xe/Ni(110)) used this mode. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Vertical Manipulation (Pick-up/Place):** The tip approaches a target atom so closely that the tip–atom chemical bond becomes stronger than the atom–substrate bond. The atom transfers to the tip, the tip moves to a new location, and a voltage pulse or contact redeposits the atom. Used for transferring individual metal atoms. **(2 marks)**

**(c) Pulse-Induced Manipulation:** A short voltage pulse (3–5 V, milliseconds) between tip and sample can break or form local chemical bonds. This is used, for example, to abstract a single H atom from a Si surface or to induce rotation in a single molecule. **(2 marks)**

**Role of forces:** In each mode, the balance of van der Waals attraction, Pauli repulsion, electrostatic force, and short-range chemical binding determines the manipulation outcome. Tuning the tip–sample distance, the bias voltage, and the substrate temperature controls the relative magnitudes of these forces. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]**

**B(i). [7 marks]** Explain the construction and working of a Scanning Tunneling Microscope with a neat block diagram. Discuss why STM requires (a) atomically sharp tips, (b) UHV environment, and (c) conducting samples.

**B(ii). [7 marks]** Describe the landmark IBM "Quantum Corral" experiment (Fe atoms on Cu(111)) and explain how it provides direct visual evidence of quantum mechanical wavefunctions of surface-state electrons.

---

### **Model Answer for B(i):**

**Construction:** An STM consists of (1) an atomically sharp metallic tip (W or Pt-Ir), (2) a three-axis piezoelectric scanner (XYZ) with sub-Å precision, (3) a vibration-isolation stage, (4) a coarse positioning mechanism, (5) a bias voltage source, (6) a tunneling-current preamplifier (gain ~10$^9$), and (7) a feedback controller. **(2 marks)**

**Working:** A small bias voltage (typically 0.01–2 V) is applied between tip and conducting sample. When the tip is within ~1 nm of the surface, electrons quantum-mechanically tunnel across the gap, producing a current $I_t = I_0 e^{-\kappa d}$. The feedback loop adjusts the Z-piezo voltage to keep $I_t$ constant, so the tip tracks the surface contour. Scanning XY produces a 3-D topographic map with atomic resolution. **(2 marks)**

**Why atomically sharp tips?** A tip terminating in a single atom produces a highly localized tunneling current, which gives true atomic resolution. Multiple-tip geometries produce image artifacts. **(1 mark)**

**Why UHV?** At atmospheric pressure, contamination layers (water, hydrocarbons, oxides) form on the surface within seconds, destroying both imaging and manipulation. UHV ($\sim 10^{-10}$ mbar) provides clean surfaces for hours-to-days. **(1 mark)**

**Why conducting samples?** STM requires a measurable tunneling current, which demands a conducting or semiconducting substrate. Insulating samples are imaged instead by AFM (atomic force microscopy). **(1 mark)**

---

### **Model Answer for B(ii):**

**Background:** In 1993, Don Eigler and coworkers at IBM used 48 Fe atoms arranged in a ring of radius ~7.3 nm on a clean Cu(111) surface to construct a "quantum corral." **(1 mark)**

**Procedure:** The Fe atoms were positioned one by one using lateral STM manipulation at 4 K. The Fe atoms act as nearly perfect potential walls for the Cu(111) surface-state electrons. **(1 mark)**

**Observation:** An STM topographic image of the inside of the corral revealed a beautiful standing-wave pattern of ripples corresponding to the confined 2-D electron gas — direct real-space visualization of quantum mechanical wavefunctions. **(1 mark)**

**Theoretical interpretation:** The 2-D free-electron wavefunction inside a circular boundary is given by Bessel functions: $\psi_{n,m}(r,\theta) \propto J_m(k_{n,m} r) e^{im\theta}$, with quantized wave vectors $k_{n,m}$ determined by the boundary condition $\psi(R) = 0$. The standing wave amplitude is: **(2 marks)**

$$\vert \psi(r,\theta) \vert^2 = \sum_{n,m} \vert c_{n,m} \vert^2 \vert J_m(k_{n,m} r) \vert^2$$

**Significance:** The quantum corral was the first direct real-space image of electron standing waves, providing striking visual confirmation of the wave nature of matter and demonstrating the power of STM manipulation to fabricate artificial quantum systems. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Mistakes:**
> 1. **Units, Units, Units:** Examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark for inconsistent or missing SI units in derivations of $\kappa$ or $I_t$. Always convert eV → J and nm → m.
> 2. **Factor of 2 confusion:** Forgetting whether the formula uses $\kappa$ or $2\kappa$ in the exponent is a very common mistake. State the convention explicitly.
> 3. **Vague "answer" without numerics:** A 7-mark derivation question expects at least one numerical example. Just stating the formula without numbers loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing boundary conditions:** In the Schrödinger derivation, students often forget to justify $B = 0$. Always state that $\psi$ must remain finite inside the barrier.
> 5. **Confusing imaging vs manipulation:** Examiners specifically look for whether the student understands that the Z-feedback is *closed* during imaging and *opened* during manipulation. Forgetting this loses 1 mark on a 7-mark question.
> 6. **Missing historical context:** Naming Eigler, IBM Almaden, year 1989, and the "I-B-M" demonstration is often a separate 1-mark allocation in Part A answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — STM-Based Atomic Manipulations**

- ✅ STM = Scanning Tunneling Microscope; invented by **Binnig & Rohrer (1981, Nobel 1986)**.
- ✅ **Tunneling current:** $I_t = I_0 \exp(-\kappa d)$, where $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e \phi}/\hbar$.
- ✅ For $\phi = 4\ \text{eV}$, $\kappa \approx 1.024\ \text{Å}^{-1}$ → ~**11% change in $I_t$ per 0.1 Å** of gap change.
- ✅ **Lateral resolution** ~0.1 nm; **vertical resolution** ~0.01 nm.
- ✅ Atomic manipulation pioneered by **Don Eigler (IBM, 1989)** with **35 Xe atoms on Ni(110)** spelling "IBM."
- ✅ **Quantum corral (1993):** 48 Fe atoms on Cu(111) → direct visualization of standing electron waves.
- ✅ **Three manipulation modes:** Lateral sliding, vertical pick-and-place, voltage-pulse induced.
- ✅ **Forces involved:** Van der Waals (attractive, $\propto d^{-3}$), Pauli repulsion (repulsive, $d^{-9\ \text{to}\ -12}$), electrostatic, short-range chemical.
- ✅ **Imaging vs manipulation:** Z-feedback **closed** for imaging, **opened** for manipulation.
- ✅ **Requirements:** Atomically sharp tip, UHV ($10^{-10}$ mbar), conducting sample, cryogenic temperature (4 K for stability).
- ✅ **Thermal stability condition:** $k_B T \ll E_b$ (binding energy) to prevent unwanted diffusion.
- ✅ **Key physical constant:** $1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$ — always convert when plugging into formulas with SI units.
- ✅ **Applications:** Quantum corrals, atomic logic, single-molecule machines, surface chemistry studies, defect/dopant placement in semiconductors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nanolithography - Soft Lithography, Scanning Probe Lithography, Photolithography, E-Beam Lithography, Focused Ion Beam Lithography, Dip-Pen Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nanolithography - Soft Lithography, Scanning Probe Lithography, Photolithography, E-Beam Lithography, Focused Ion Beam Lithography, Dip-Pen Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Nanolithography

## 1.1 What is Nanolithography?

**Nanolithography** is the branch of nanotechnology concerned with the design, fabrication, and patterning of structures at the nanometer scale (typically below **100 nm**), with feature sizes often reaching the sub-**10 nm** regime. The term originates from the Greek words *nano* (dwarf), *lithos* (stone), and *graphein* (to write) — literally meaning **"writing on stone at the nanometer scale."**

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, nanolithography is studied as the set of top-down fabrication techniques that enable the controlled transfer of geometric patterns from a master template (mask, stamp, or beam) onto a substrate (typically silicon, glass, or polymer) to produce functional nanostructures, nanodevices, and integrated circuits (ICs).

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme – Syllabus Highlight**
> The module covers **six** major nanolithographic routes. The board examiner expects you to classify each technique based on:
> 1. **Pattern transfer mechanism** (photonic, charged-particle, mechanical, chemical)
> 2. **Resolution limit**
> 3. **Throughput / speed**
> 4. **Mask requirement** (mask-based vs. maskless / direct-write)
> 5. **Application domain**

---

## 1.2 Master Classification of Lithographic Techniques

| Family | Energy / Probe Source | Resolution | Mask? | Throughput |
|--------|---------------------|------------|-------|-----------|
| Photolithography | UV / EUV photons | ~**38 nm** (193 nm ArF immersion) | Yes | Very High |
| Soft Lithography | Mechanical (elastomer stamp) | ~**30 nm** | No (uses a master) | Medium |
| Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL) | AFM/STM tip | ~**5–10 nm** | No (direct-write) | Very Low |
| Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN) | AFM tip + molecular ink | ~**15–50 nm** | No (direct-write) | Very Low |
| Electron-Beam (E-Beam) Lithography | Focused electrons | ~**2–10 nm** | No (direct-write) | Low |
| Focused Ion Beam (FIB) Lithography | Focused Ga⁺ ions | ~**5–20 nm** | No (direct-write) | Low |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "How to Print a Masterpiece on a Nanometer Canvas"

Imagine you are an artist who wants to paint a masterpiece on a very tiny canvas.

- **Photolithography** is like **photo-printing a photograph**: you shine a flashlight through a stencil (the photomask) onto a light-sensitive paper (photoresist). Wherever light hits, chemistry happens.
- **Soft Lithography** is like **using a rubber stamp**: you carve a master pattern, pour liquid silicone over it, peel off the rubber stamp, dip it in ink, and press it onto your surface.
- **Scanning Probe Lithography** is like **drawing with a fine-tipped pen** that is so sharp only one atom at a time touches the paper.
- **E-Beam / FIB Lithography** is like **shooting invisible microscopic bullets** (electrons or ions) at a surface, one pixel at a time, to "carve" your pattern.
- **Dip-Pen Nanolithography** is literally **dipping an atomic-force-microscope tip into "ink" molecules and writing with it**, exactly like writing with a fountain pen — but at the nanoscale.

> [!NOTE] **Intuition Box**
> Photolithography = mass production (billions of chips per year).
> E-beam / FIB / DPN = R&D and prototyping (slow but extremely precise).
> Soft lithography = cheap, accessible nanofabrication for chemistry and biology labs.

---

## 1.4 Importance in Modern Technology

Nanolithography underpins the **entire semiconductor industry** — every microchip, smartphone processor, and memory device is built using some form of lithography. The transition from micrometer to nanometer features (Moore's Law) has been driven almost entirely by advances in lithographic resolution, pushing from the **k₁ = 0.7** era (1990s) to today's immersion ArF lithography and EUV lithography at **13.5 nm** wavelength.

Beyond electronics, nanolithography is critical for:
- **Biomedical devices** (DNA/protein arrays, biosensors)
- **Photonics** (plasmonic nanostructures, metamaterials)
- **Energy** (perovskite solar cells, fuel cell membranes)
- **MEMS / NEMS** (nanoelectromechanical systems)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Photolithography — The Workhorse of the Semiconductor Industry

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

Photolithography (also called **optical lithography**) uses **ultraviolet (UV) light** projected through a patterned **photomask** to selectively expose a **photoresist** (a light-sensitive polymer) spin-coated on a substrate. The exposed regions undergo a chemical change:

- **Positive resist**: exposed areas become **soluble** in the developer (e.g., **PMMA**, **polymethyl methacrylate**; **DNQ/novolac**).
- **Negative resist**: exposed areas become **cross-linked and insoluble** (e.g., **SU-8**, **SU-8 epoxy**).

### 2.1.2 Process Flow (Bulleted Logic Steps)

1. **Substrate preparation**: Silicon wafer is cleaned, dehydrated, and primed with **HMDS (hexamethyldisilazane)** for resist adhesion.
2. **Spin-coating**: Photoresist is dispensed and spun at **1000–6000 rpm** to form a uniform thin film (typically **0.1 – 2 µm** thick).
3. **Soft-bake (Pre-exposure bake)**: Removes residual solvent; performed at **90–120 °C** for 60 s on a hot plate.
4. **Exposure (Step-and-Scan / Step-and-Repeat)**: UV light passes through the mask, projecting the pattern onto the resist.
5. **Post-exposure bake (PEB)**: Drives the **photoacid generator (PAG)** diffusion in **chemically amplified resists (CARs)**.
6. **Development**: Substrate is immersed in a developer (**TMAH – tetramethylammonium hydroxide, 0.26 N** for positive resist).
7. **Hard-bake**: Optional thermal step to harden the developed pattern.
8. **Etching / Ion Implantation / Metallization**: Pattern is transferred into the underlying substrate.
9. **Resist Stripping**: Photoresist is removed with **acetone, NMP, or oxygen plasma ashing**.

### 2.1.3 Sources of Illumination — Wavelength Evolution

| Source | Wavelength (nm) | Era |
|--------|----------------|-----|
| Mercury lamp G-line | 436 | 1970s |
| Mercury lamp H-line | 405 | 1980s |
| Mercury lamp I-line | 365 | 1990s |
| Excimer laser KrF | 248 | 1990s |
| Excimer laser ArF | 193 | 2000s |
| ArF Immersion | 193 (in water) | 2010s |
| EUV (Sn plasma) | **13.5** | 2018–present |

---

## 2.2 Soft Lithography — The "Rubber Stamp" Approach

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

Soft lithography, pioneered by **George M. Whitesides and Younan Xia** (Harvard University, 1993–1998), uses an **elastomeric stamp or mold** — almost always made of **PDMS (polydimethylsiloxane, Sylgard 184)** — to transfer patterns by conformal contact. PDMS is preferred because of its:
- Low surface energy (~**21.6 dyn/cm**)
- Elastomeric nature (Young's modulus ~**1.8 MPa**)
- Optical transparency down to **230 nm**
- Biocompatibility

### 2.2.2 Family of Soft Lithography Techniques

| Technique | Mechanism | Resolution |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Microcontact Printing (μCP)** | Ink transferred from PDMS stamp to substrate via self-assembled monolayer (SAM) | **~30 nm – 500 nm** |
| **Replica Molding (REM)** | Liquid prepolymer cast against PDMS mold and cured | **~30 nm – 10 µm** |
| **Microtransfer Molding (μTM)** | Liquid prepolymer fills PDMS mold, then transferred to substrate | **~1 µm – 100 µm** |
| **Micromolding in Capillaries (MIMIC)** | Liquid prepolymer drawn into channels of PDMS by capillary action | **~1 µm – 100 µm** |
| **Solvent-Assisted Micromolding (SAMIM)** | Solvent-laden PDMS stamp softens polymer film on contact | **~60 nm – 1 µm** |

### 2.2.3 Why "Soft"?

The word "soft" refers to the **elastomeric nature** of the stamp, in contrast to the rigid silica/chromium masks of photolithography. This conformability allows patterning on **non-planar, curved, or rough surfaces** — a capability impossible with conventional photolithography.

---

## 2.3 Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

SPL uses the **sharp tip** (radius **< 10 nm**) of a **Scanning Probe Microscope (SPM)** as a local tool to modify a substrate mechanically, electrically, thermally, or chemically. The tip is scanned across the surface in a **raster pattern** under computer control.

### 2.3.2 Sub-Families of SPL

1. **Local Anodic Oxidation (LAO) lithography**: A bias voltage applied between a conductive AFM tip and a substrate (e.g., Ti, Si) generates a localized water meniscus that **oxidizes** the surface.
2. **Mechanical Nanolithography**: AFM tip in contact mode **scratches** or **indents** a soft resist (e.g., PMMA, SAMs).
3. **Thermomechanical Lithography**: IBM "Millipede" memory — tip locally heats and indents a polymer medium.
4. **STM Lithography**: Tunneling current from STM tip induces **desorption, deposition, or electromigration** on H-passivated Si(100).

---

## 2.4 Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle

Invented by **Chad A. Mirkin (Northwestern University) in 1999**, DPN uses an **AFM tip** as a **"pen,"** a **substrate** (Au, Si, SiO₂) as **"paper,"** and **functional molecules** (alkanethiols, proteins, DNA) as **"ink."** A water meniscus naturally forms between the tip and substrate under ambient humidity (typically **30–60 % RH**), transporting the ink via capillary action.

### 2.4.2 Key Parameters

- **Tip radius**: 10–50 nm (sharpness dictates feature size)
- **Writing speed**: 0.1 – 10 µm/s
- **Feature size**: as small as **15 nm linewidth** (alkanethiol dots on Au(111))
- **Throughput**: greatly improved by **2D tip arrays** (up to 55,000 tips demonstrated)

---

## 2.5 Electron-Beam Lithography (EBL)

### 2.5.1 Operating Principle

EBL uses a **focused beam of electrons** (accelerated at **10 – 100 kV**, beam current **10 pA – 100 nA**) to directly expose a resist (typically **PMMA, ZEP-520A, or HSQ**). Because electrons are charged particles, they can be deflected by **electromagnetic lenses** with sub-nanometer precision — eliminating the need for a mask.

### 2.5.2 The Role of Electron–Resist Interaction

When high-energy electrons penetrate the resist:
1. **Forward scattering** within the resist broadens the beam.
2. **Backscattering** from the substrate creates the **"proximity effect"** — unintended exposure of neighbouring features.
3. **Secondary electrons** (energy **< 50 eV**) drive the actual resist chemistry (chain scission in PMMA).

---

## 2.6 Focused Ion Beam (FIB) Lithography

### 2.6.1 Operating Principle

FIB lithography uses a **focused beam of ions** — most commonly **Ga⁺ ions** from a **liquid-metal ion source (LMIS)** — accelerated at **5 – 50 kV**. Because ions are **~10⁵ times more massive** than electrons, they:
- Have much shorter penetration depth (few nm to tens of nm)
- Transfer higher energy per impact (sputtering, milling)
- Do not produce a large proximity-effect halo (lower backscatter)

### 2.6.2 Two Operating Modes

1. **Direct Milling / Sputtering**: The ion beam physically removes material (no resist needed).
2. **Ion-Beam Exposure of Resist**: Beam modifies a resist (e.g., **PMMA, HSQ**) for subsequent development.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Master Equation Table — KTU Board Examiner Favourite**

| Parameter | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|-----------|---------|-----------|------|
| **Rayleigh Resolution** | $R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | λ = wavelength; NA = numerical aperture; k₁ = process factor (≈ 0.4–1.0) | nm |
| **Numerical Aperture** | $NA = n \cdot \sin\theta$ | n = refractive index of medium; θ = half-angle of max cone of light entering lens | dimensionless |
| **Depth of Focus (DOF)** | $DOF = \pm \, k_2 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}$ | k₂ ≈ 0.5 – 1.0 | nm |
| **de Broglie Wavelength (non-relativistic)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}$ | h = Planck constant; m = electron mass; e = electron charge; V = accelerating voltage | nm |
| **de Broglie Wavelength (relativistic)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}}\right)}}$ | c = speed of light | nm |
| **Numerical Shortcut for Electrons** | $\lambda(\text{nm}) \approx \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \, (1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \, V)}}$ | V = accelerating voltage | nm |
| **Spot Size (Gaussian Beam)** | $d = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{NA}$ | Gaussian approximation of smallest focused spot | nm |
| **Proximity Effect Radius** | $\beta = \dfrac{1.65 \times 10^{3}}{V^{1.75}} \, (\mu m)$ | Empirical; in PMMA on Si | µm |
| **Ga⁺ Ion Penetration (SRIM)** | $R_p \approx \dfrac{k \, V^{n}}{Z^{m}}$ | V = voltage; Z = atomic number of substrate; k, n, m = fit constants | nm |
| **DOF Ratio vs Air** | $DOF_{\text{immersion}} = \dfrac{DOF_{\text{dry}}}{n}$ | n = refractive index of immersion fluid (water ≈ 1.44) | nm |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of Each Technique

- **Photolithography** → fabricates **>90 %** of all integrated circuits, memory chips, and CPUs globally.
- **Soft Lithography** → enables **low-cost microfluidics, biosensors, organic electronics** in chemistry and biomedical labs.
- **Scanning Probe Lithography** → used in **single-electron transistor research, quantum device prototyping**.
- **Dip-Pen Nanolithography** → used in **biosensing arrays, protein patterning, combinatorial drug screening**.
- **E-Beam Lithography** → used in **photomask fabrication, photonic crystal prototyping, R&D for sub-7 nm CMOS**.
- **Focused Ion Beam Lithography** → used in **mask repair, TEM sample preparation, circuit edit, failure analysis**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Resolution Limit

The **Rayleigh criterion** is the foundation of lithographic resolution theory. Let us derive it systematically from the physics of wave optics.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Two-Point Diffraction

Consider two infinitesimally small apertures illuminated by a monochromatic plane wave of wavelength $\lambda$. Each aperture acts as a point source and produces a circular Airy diffraction pattern in the far field (Fraunhofer regime), with the **first minimum** of the Airy pattern at an angle $\theta$ satisfying:

$$a \sin\theta = 1.22 \, \lambda$$

where $a$ is the diameter of the circular aperture (in this case, the lens diameter $D$). Rearranging:

$$\sin\theta = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}$$

The **Rayleigh criterion** states that two point sources are just resolvable when the central maximum of one Airy disk falls on the first minimum of the other. This corresponds to a transverse distance in the image plane:

$$R = 0.61 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{\sin\theta}$$

### 3.1.2 Defining Numerical Aperture

In an optical projection system, the **numerical aperture** is defined as:

$$NA = n \cdot \sin\theta_{\max}$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the medium in image space and $\theta_{\max}$ is the half-angle of the maximum cone of light collected by the lens. Substituting $\sin\theta = NA / n$ and introducing the empirical **process-dependent factor $k_1$** (which lumps together resist effects, illumination coherence, aberrations, etc.):

$$R = k_1 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

This is the most important equation in optical lithography. The board examiner expects you to:
- Quote it verbatim.
- State the **theoretical minimum of $k_1 = 0.25$** (single-point imaging with off-axis illumination and perfect resist).
- Recognize that historically $k_1$ has fallen from **0.8 (1980s)** to **0.35 (modern ArF immersion)**.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A 193 nm ArF immersion scanner has $NA = 1.35$ and is operated with $k_1 = 0.35$. Compute the half-pitch resolution.

**Solution:**

$$R = 0.35 \times \dfrac{193 \text{ nm}}{1.35}$$

$$R = \dfrac{67.55}{1.35}$$

$$R \approx 50.04 \text{ nm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Interpretation:** This matches the half-pitch of the **Intel 32 nm CMOS node** (circa 2010). The transition to **EUV at 13.5 nm** was necessary to push beyond ~30 nm half-pitch.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Depth of Focus

### 3.2.1 From Wavefront Curvature to DOF

The depth of focus (DOF) is the range of vertical positions of the wafer over which the aerial image remains acceptably sharp. Geometrically, it is governed by the curvature of the converging wavefront at the focal plane. For a perfect lens with marginal ray angle $\theta$, the longitudinal distance from focus to the first minimum is:

$$DOF_{\text{geometrical}} = \pm \, \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, n \, \sin^{2}\theta} = \pm \, \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, n \, (1 - \cos 2\theta)}$$

In the paraxial approximation ($2\theta$ small, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, $NA = n \sin\theta \approx n \theta$):

$$DOF \approx \pm \, \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, n \theta^{2}} = \pm \, \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, NA^{2}} \cdot n$$

The factor $n$ is then absorbed into the process factor $k_2$ (typically $k_2 \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$), yielding the standard textbook form:

$$DOF = \pm \, k_2 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Example — DOF in Air vs. Immersion

**Problem:** Compare the depth of focus of a 193 nm dry scanner ($n = 1.0$, $NA = 0.93$, $k_2 = 0.5$) with an immersion scanner ($n = 1.44$, $NA = 1.35$, $k_2 = 0.5$).

**Dry system:**

$$DOF_{\text{dry}} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{193}{0.93^{2}} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{193}{0.8649} \approx 111.6 \text{ nm}$$

**Immersion system:**

$$DOF_{\text{imm}} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{193}{1.35^{2}} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{193}{1.8225} \approx 52.95 \text{ nm}$$

> [!WARNING] **Engineering Trade-off (must memorize)**
> Going from dry to immersion **doubles the NA → halves the DOF**. This is why immersion lithography requires ultra-flat wafers, ultra-precise auto-focus systems, and topcoat-free resists.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Electron Wavelength (for E-Beam Lithography)

### 3.3.1 de Broglie Hypothesis

Louis de Broglie proposed that every particle of momentum $p$ has an associated wavelength $\lambda = h / p$. For an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$, the kinetic energy is:

$$eV = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} = \dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$$

Solving for momentum:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m e V}$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}$$

Plugging in SI values ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C):

$$\lambda(\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}$$

For $V = 100{,}000$ V (100 kV), $\lambda \approx 0.00387$ nm — about **50,000× smaller** than UV light.

### 3.3.2 Relativistic Correction (mandatory above 30 kV)

At high accelerating voltages, the electron's velocity approaches a significant fraction of $c$. The total energy becomes:

$$E_{\text{total}} = eV + m_0 c^{2} = \gamma m_0 c^{2}$$

The relativistic momentum is:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_0 e V \left( 1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}} \right)}$$

So:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}}\right)}}$$

The numerical engineering approximation is:

$$\lambda(\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \left( 1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \, V \right)}}$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example

**Problem:** What is the electron wavelength at 30 kV? Compare with 100 kV.

**At 30 kV:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{30{,}000 \left( 1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \times 30{,}000 \right)}}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{30{,}000 \times 1.02934}} = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{30{,}880.2}} = \dfrac{1.227}{175.7} \approx 0.00698 \text{ nm}$$

**At 100 kV:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{100{,}000 \left( 1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \times 100{,}000 \right)}}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{100{,}000 \times 1.0978}} = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{109{,}780}} = \dfrac{1.227}{331.3} \approx 0.00370 \text{ nm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight**
> Even at 30 kV, the **de Broglie wavelength is ~ 7 pm** — orders of magnitude smaller than any achievable feature. The resolution of EBL is **not limited by diffraction** but by:
> 1. **Electron beam spot size** (aberrations, finite source size)
> 2. **Forward and back-scattering in the resist** (proximity effect)
> 3. **Resist resolution** (PMMA can resolve ~10 nm features)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU Board Question Favourite

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master Comparison Table for Exam**

| Parameter | Photolithography | E-Beam | FIB | Soft Lithography | DPN | SPL |
|-----------|-----------------|--------|-----|------------------|-----|-----|
| **Probe** | UV / EUV photons | Electrons | Ga⁺ ions | PDMS stamp | AFM tip | AFM / STM tip |
| **Wavelength / λ** | 13.5 – 436 nm | ~ 0.004 – 0.007 nm | n/a (de Broglie ~0.002 nm) | n/a | n/a | n/a |
| **Mask Required?** | Yes | No (direct-write) | No (direct-write) | No (uses a master) | No (direct-write) | No (direct-write) |
| **Resolution (best)** | ~ 38 nm (ArF imm.) / 13 nm (EUV) | ~ 2 – 10 nm | ~ 5 – 20 nm | ~ 30 nm | ~ 15 nm | ~ 5 – 10 nm |
| **DOF** | ±50 – ±500 nm | ~ tens of nm | ~ tens of nm | n/a | n/a | n/a |
| **Throughput** | Very High | Very Low | Low | Medium | Very Low | Very Low |
| **Resist Type** | Optical resist (CAR) | E-beam resist (PMMA, HSQ) | Ion resist / direct mill | Polymer precursor | SAMs (thiols) | Variable |
| **Vacuum?** | No | Yes | Yes | No | No | No (or UHV for STM) |
| **Application** | IC mass production | Mask fab, R&D | Mask repair, circuit edit | Bio-MEMS, microfluidics | Biosensing arrays | Quantum devices |
| **Capital Cost** | Very High (~$200 M for EUV) | High (~$5 M) | High (~$1–3 M) | Very Low | Low | Low |

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Proximity Effect Parameter in EBL

When the e-beam strikes the resist, **backscattered electrons** from the substrate can expose regions tens of micrometres away from the intended spot. The total energy deposited at a point at radial distance $r$ from the beam centre is approximated by the sum of two Gaussian distributions:

$$f(r) = \dfrac{1}{\pi (1 + \eta)} \left[ \dfrac{1}{\alpha^{2}} \exp\left( -\dfrac{r^{2}}{\alpha^{2}} \right) + \dfrac{\eta}{\beta^{2}} \exp\left( -\dfrac{r^{2}}{\beta^{2}} \right) \right]$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = forward-scattering range (~**5 – 20 nm**)
- $\beta$ = backscattering range (~**1 – 30 µm**)
- $\eta$ = backscatter coefficient (~**0.5 – 0.9** for electrons on Si)

The ratio $\beta / \alpha$ typically exceeds **1000**, which is why the proximity effect is the **dominant resolution-limiting factor** in EBL, and why **proximity-effect correction (PEC)** software is mandatory for sub-100 nm patterning.

---
